channel.title: Alpha Funnel — All channels
channel.description: Daily investing, news, and AI & Tools briefings from Alpha Funnel.

item.title: [Investing] The memory trade is cracking in Seoul at the exact hour SK Hynix starts trading here, and oil, credit, and a short-seller are all leaning the same way
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: SK Hynix fell 10 percent overnight and Korea tripped its circuit breaker while Iran and Washington argue over whether Hormuz is open, oil sits up but contained, and Goldman warns the hyperscaler bond market is choking. Micron has a clean line to defend, semiconductor earnings start Tuesday, and Michael Burry is short the whole thing.
item.content:encoded:
SK Hynix fell 10 percent overnight and Korea tripped its circuit breaker while Iran and Washington argue over whether Hormuz is open, oil sits up but contained, and Goldman warns the hyperscaler bond market is choking. Micron has a clean line to defend, semiconductor earnings start Tuesday, and Michael Burry is short the whole thing.

The weekend did not resolve the war and this morning did not resolve the memory trade. Both arrive at the open as live risks, and for once the bear has company. Here is the pre-open picture, then five stories.

Pre-open preview. Oil is the first thing paying for the weekend. US crude is up 4 to 5 percent after Iran again declared the Strait of Hormuz closed and the US again declared it open, coordinating roughly 20 tankers through overnight, per the Market and Kalshi lists. The level to watch is a gap at $83.20 on crude futures, and Brent is still under $80, so this is a spike, not yet a crisis. Gold lost $4,100 as the dollar jumped, a risk-off-into-dollar move rather than a classic panic, per the Financial Markets list.

Equity futures lean slightly red, and several traders are already calling it a war rug-pull that closes green. The bigger tell is in Asia. SK Hynix fell 10 percent in Seoul, the KOSPI dropped 3 percent, and the exchange activated a sidecar to halt program selling, per the Financial Markets list. The calendar is loaded: SK Hynix starts regular-way $SKHY trading here today, the $SKHX 2x long and $SKHZ short ETFs launch Tuesday at 4am, and June CPI (the inflation print that settles the July 29 Fed debate) lands Tuesday morning, per the People list.

1. The memory supercycle is showing its first real cracks, and two of them are brand new. For a week the memory trade shrugged off everything. This morning it is not shrugging. Beyond the 10 percent SK Hynix drop and the Korean circuit breaker, two fresh datapoints cut against the "infinite demand" story, per the Stocks list. Murata is cutting prices on its MLCC capacitors, and Nvidia dropped the 800-volt direct-current requirement from its Rubin Ultra rack, a near-term headwind for the Vertiv and silicon-carbide names that rallied on exactly that spec.

Neither breaks the multi-year thesis, but both puncture the idea that nothing can go wrong. Micron now has a line to defend rather than a launchpad. Per Stocks with Josh, $MU failed to close above 996 over the weekend, so the upside target at 1089 is off the table until it reclaims 996, the stop is 950, and losing that opens 900 and then 861. The whole complex trades off whether Korea's selling washes into the US session or exhausts itself into the $SKHY debut.

2. The bear finally has a real thesis, and it is about debt, not just valuation. The fresh crack this morning is in credit. Per the Financial Markets list, the 2-year Treasury yield pushed above 4.23 percent, its highest since February 2025, and Goldman's trading desk is circulating a note about carnage in the hyperscaler bond market, warning it is very difficult to fund another $360 billion over the next year when the hyperscalers need roughly $1 trillion annually. Banks are sitting on their largest-ever net short in corporate bonds. And Michael Burry's AI shorts surfaced in the filings, roughly $456 million against $PLTR, $187 million against $NVDA, and about $100 million against the $SOXX semiconductor ETF.

The efficiency side of the bear case got louder too. A sharp Reddit thread lays out the pattern that keeps denting the Nvidia capex story: DeepSeek erased $589 billion of Nvidia market cap in a day last year on a cheap-training claim, GLM-5.2 is a 744-billion-parameter open model the maker says trained entirely on Chinese Huawei Ascend chips, and a robotics vision model matched a Meta model at a twentieth of the size. The honest counter is Jevons, that cheaper compute has so far expanded demand rather than shrinking it, so this is a genuine two-sided fight rather than a settled short. Either way, the multiples now have a coherent thing to be afraid of.

3. Semiconductor earnings start Tuesday, and they will referee the whole argument. The week's real judge is the tape from the chip names themselves. Per Jose Najarro, $AEHR (Aehr Test Systems) reports Tuesday after the close, $ASML Wednesday before the open, and $TSM (Taiwan Semiconductor) Thursday after the close. Aehr is the fresh story, having raised its full-year bookings guide above $92 million on a record $41 million production order from a lead hyperscale AI customer, with new silicon-photonics follow-ons and memory flagged as an emerging customer.

The other two set the tone for the whole trade. Najarro is explicitly not a buyer of $ASML at a forward price-to-earnings near 46 against a decade median around 32, though he stays long-term bullish, and he calls $TSM the swing factor, watching its capital-spending guide and whether it lifts 2026 revenue growth from above 30 percent toward 35 to 40. Strong TSM guidance pulls up $AMD, $AVGO, $MRVL, and $NVDA. A cautious one confirms the bears.

4. Opendoor is the cleanest new single-name idea, and a law nobody read handed it a gift. Per a detailed Reddit breakdown, the new housing law that markets treated as a blunt ban on institutional buyers actually blocks large investors from accumulating 350-plus single-family homes while expressly exempting homes that are newly built, renovated, or converted for sale and not held as rentals. That exemption is a statutory lane cut almost precisely around the $OPEN (Opendoor) business model, which turns inventory rather than building a rental empire, and it kills the cleanest regulatory bear case against the stock.

The operating turn underneath it is real, whatever the ugly headline numbers say. Homes purchased rose 45 percent quarter over quarter, Opendoor signed more than 5,000 acquisition contracts (double the prior quarter and the most since 2022), inventory sitting longer than 120 days fell from 33 percent to 10 percent, and fixed operating costs dropped from $39 million to $33 million. Management guided to roughly 25 percent sequential revenue growth and about breakeven adjusted EBITDA, targeting positive adjusted net income on a forward basis by the end of 2026.

5. Robinhood Chain flipped Hyperliquid over the weekend, and the stock has decoupled from crypto. The crypto-equity crossover keeps compounding. Per the Crypto list, Robinhood Chain overtook Hyperliquid in 24-hour decentralized-exchange volume, $433 million against $296 million, on more than 17 million transactions and about $250 million in deposits, and $HOOD is up 80 percent off its May lows and no longer tracking Bitcoin. A Substack breakdown adds the mechanics, that the chain runs on Arbitrum's technology with Ether as its gas token and is aimed squarely at tokenized real-world assets, with Robinhood's Vlad framing a listing on it as the new Binance listing. Watch whether the on-chain volume is real adoption or launch-week momentum, the same test the daily tape keeps setting.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $MU (Micron). Per Stocks with Josh, reclaim 996 to re-arm the move toward 1089, a stop at 950, and downside to 900 then 861 if Korea's selling carries over.
  • $AEHR (Aehr Test Systems). Reports Tuesday, having raised full-year bookings above $92 million on a record $41 million hyperscale order, with photonics and memory as new customers, per Najarro.
  • $OPEN (Opendoor). The housing-law exemption plus a Q1 operating inflection, guided to breakeven EBITDA and forward profitability by year-end, per the Reddit DD.
  • $NFLX (Netflix). Per Josh, needs to reclaim $76 to set up a bullish swing into Thursday's earnings, with a 65-to-76 range in play.
  • $HOOD (Robinhood). Up 80 percent off the May lows and decoupled from Bitcoin as its chain flips Hyperliquid, per the Crypto list.
  • WD-40. The anti-hype hedge, up 12 percent Friday on 24 percent revenue growth to $195 million and a raised full-year outlook, per a Barron's summary. Sometimes doing the basics well beats the AI trade.
  • Gold. Per Benjamin Cowen, the midterm-year pattern points to a bottom between July and October near his support band around 3,824, then a reacceleration into 2027.

Psychology. This is the first morning in a while where the tape is handing the reader more than one honest warning at once, and the test is whether the crowd can hear any of them. The memory complex is melting down in Seoul with a circuit breaker tripped, yet the loudest voices on the Stocks list are still posting $2,200 Micron and $3,250 SanDisk targets, and the reflex to call the oil spike a war rug-pull and buy the dip is already forming before the cash open. That reflex has paid every time this month, and that is exactly what makes it dangerous the one morning the cracks are real.

The countersignal is who is on the other side. Michael Burry is short Palantir, Nvidia, and the semis into this, Goldman is warning its own clients about the hyperscaler bond market, and Nvidia just quietly pulled a spec that a whole basket of stocks had rallied on. When the crowd is raising price targets while the credit market chokes and the early money builds shorts, the odds that this dip is different go up, not down.

So the discipline check into the open is to respect the possibility that today is not a rug-pull to buy but a first crack to size for. If you are tempted to add memory names on the SK Hynix debut or chase the oil trade, remember Micron has a line at 996 and a stop at 950 for a reason, and CPI lands tomorrow. Let $MU prove it can hold 950 and let oil prove the $83.20 gap before you decide the crowd was right to keep buying.

item.title: [News] The US ran another wave of strikes on Iran overnight and sent 20 tankers through the strait Iran says is closed, calling its bluff in real time
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: CENTCOM used one-way attack sea drones for the first time and says it hit dozens of targets, with reported strikes on the Fifth Fleet headquarters in Bahrain and US bases in Kuwait. Iran fired back and claimed American dead, which CENTCOM denies. At home, the country lowered flags for Senator Lindsey Graham.
item.content:encoded:
CENTCOM used one-way attack sea drones for the first time and says it hit dozens of targets, with reported strikes on the Fifth Fleet headquarters in Bahrain and US bases in Kuwait. Iran fired back and claimed American dead, which CENTCOM denies. At home, the country lowered flags for Senator Lindsey Graham.

The war got louder overnight and the United States answered Iran's closure claim not with words but by escorting tankers through the water. Ranked.

1. Washington is calling Iran's closure bluff by force. Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz closed again over the weekend, and the US response was physical. Per the Market and Polymarket lists, roughly 20 commercial vessels transited the strait in coordination with the US military, some without escort, while CENTCOM insists Iran does not control the waterway. The message is that the strait is open because the US Navy says it is, and the tankers moving through are the proof it wants the market to see.

The fighting escalated to back it. Per the Financial Markets list, CENTCOM completed another wave of strikes on Iran, hitting dozens of air-defense, radar, missile, and drone sites and using one-way attack sea drones for the first time, with reported direct hits on the Fifth Fleet headquarters in Bahrain and US bases in Kuwait. Iran fired back with ballistic missiles and drones at those bases and claimed it killed three Americans in Kuwait, which CENTCOM says is false. Trump said he bombed the hell out of them and has a thousand missiles locked.

Two things sit underneath the noise. Iran's foreign minister says the US pressured Oman to block the Muscat talks, which would close the one de-escalation channel that was still open, per the Market list. And the markets are reading it as contained rather than catastrophic, with US crude up 4 to 5 percent but Brent still under $80 and recession odds sitting below 10 percent, the lowest since 2022, per the Kalshi list. A war being fought with sea drones and tanker escorts is a strange kind of open.

2. The country lowered its flags for Lindsey Graham. Per the Polymarket list, Trump ordered flags to half-staff for Senator Lindsey Graham, confirming the South Carolina Republican's sudden death over the weekend. In the same run of Senate news, Mitch McConnell is in rehabilitation after a fall. Two of the chamber's longest-serving and most recognizable figures are off the board at once, one permanently, in the middle of a war Graham spent his career pushing to fight.

3. The financial damage showed up first in Asia. The clearest read on how markets took the weekend is the overnight tape in Asia, per the Kalshi list. Japanese stocks shed about $170 billion and Korean stocks about $150 billion in a single hour, with Korea's exchange tripping a circuit breaker to slow the selling. The dollar's share of global reserves fell to a 21st-century low even as it rallied on the day, and foreign stocks have now beaten US stocks for 70 straight weeks. The war is the headline, but the structural story underneath is money quietly moving away from American assets.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • A grand jury is examining Shawn Fain, the United Auto Workers president, per the Polymarket list. A federal probe of the union's most prominent leader is a real escalation in the government's posture toward organized labor.
  • Hyundai workers struck for three days over robots, per the Polymarket list. The automation fight is moving from the op-ed page to the factory floor, and Korea is where it is landing first.
  • Kash Patel is a coin flip to keep his job, with prediction markets putting his ouster by year-end at 54 percent, per the Polymarket list.
  • The 2026 map is firming up on the odds boards. Republicans sit at 57 percent to hold the Senate while Democrats are 82 percent to take the House, and in Maine, Democrat Troy Jackson leads Susan Collins 45 to 39, per the Kalshi list.

item.title: [AI & Tools] NVIDIA's own Kaggle champion says agent skills beat MCP tools by 100x on context, and a 4-billion-parameter model just solved half of ARC-AGI for 20 cents
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Jean-Francois Puget shipped 121 agent skills and a benchmark warning that most leaderboards are contaminated, while a distilled small model keeps beating frontier systems on cost. The efficiency story is now the whole AI debate, an ex-OpenAI forecaster puts catastrophe at 70 percent, and the practical builder lesson is simpler than either.
item.content:encoded:
Jean-Francois Puget shipped 121 agent skills and a benchmark warning that most leaderboards are contaminated, while a distilled small model keeps beating frontier systems on cost. The efficiency story is now the whole AI debate, an ex-OpenAI forecaster puts catastrophe at 70 percent, and the practical builder lesson is simpler than either.

The AI story this morning is about efficiency, and it points two ways at once. Small models keep doing frontier work for pennies, either the death of the compute trade or the reason it keeps growing. The most useful voice is a NVIDIA engineer who benchmarks for a living. Ranked, with the buildable stuff up top.

1. Skills beat MCP tools by roughly 100x on context, and that is the sleeper shift in how agents work. The standout, per Jean-Francois Puget, a NVIDIA distinguished engineer and top-three Kaggle competitor: the model-context-protocol tools that every agent loads eat your context window whether you use them or not, because their descriptions all sit in the prompt. Agent skills use progressive disclosure instead, where the agent reads only a short front-matter line and loads the full instructions on demand, and teams report that moving from MCP tools to skills cut context consumption about 100-fold.

NVIDIA shipped a repository of roughly 121 of these skills so generic coding agents like Claude Code and Codex can drive NVIDIA libraries they were never trained on, plus an automated pipeline that tests whether a skill loads when it should and stays quiet when it should not. The deeper idea is that skills are becoming a new form of learning. NVIDIA won a data-science benchmark by having its agent solve each task, distill the winning approach into a skill, then compress a hundred skills into a few general ones, all in plain markdown with no fine-tuning.

2. A 4-billion-parameter model solving half of ARC-AGI for 20 cents is the efficiency argument in one number. Puget's team keeps winning the ARC-AGI reasoning contest with tiny models. Per the same interview, a 4-billion-parameter model on Kaggle's limited hardware now solves close to 50 percent of the puzzles at about 20 cents a task, where frontier models need dollars a task to reach the same mark, built by distilling bigger models into a million synthetic puzzles and training the small one on those.

That connects straight to the market's biggest question, and a sharp Reddit thread frames both sides. DeepSeek erased $589 billion of Nvidia value in a day last year on a cheap-training claim, GLM-5.2 is a 744-billion-parameter open model its maker says trained entirely on Chinese Huawei chips, and small vision models keep matching bigger ones. The counter is Jevons, that cheaper inference has so far expanded total compute demand rather than shrinking it. Puget lands on the bullish side, arguing this is not a bubble because token costs fall every few months and adoption sticks, but he is scathing about the benchmarks everyone cites.

3. Most AI leaderboards are contaminated, and a NVIDIA engineer is calling the reckoning. The warning worth internalizing, per Puget: most language-model benchmarks do not check for overfitting, so public test sets get memorized. He watched a frontier model pass a coding benchmark by picking the right files to edit without reading their contents, because the fixes were in the public training data, and NVIDIA stopped working on that benchmark as a result. The only scores he trusts are the ones where you commit before you see the answer, the way Kaggle works, and he predicts a bloodbath for the teams now letting coding agents chase leaderboard numbers with no notion of validation.

The practical builder lesson sits next to it, per Kris Torrington. Do not abandon workflows for agents, combine them: use a deterministic workflow when the steps are known and mistakes are expensive, like payments and compliance, and an agent only when the input is messy and the decision stays bounded. The data backs the caution, with n8n growing revenue tenfold to past $40 million, Klarna quietly rehiring humans after overshooting on AI support, and Gartner projecting that more than 40 percent of agentic projects get cancelled by 2027 even as adoption climbs.

4. The lock-in is the real moat, and an ex-OpenAI forecaster is worried about much more than that. Two voices this morning argue the durable advantage is switching cost, not model quality. Per Nate B Jones, once Claude lives in your Slack as a team-level harness sitting next to everything your people do, you are effectively renting your own data back to yourself, and it becomes impossible to rip out. Puget makes the same point from the cost side, that once a team is hooked on Claude Code or Codex, going back is the hard part.

The heavier note comes from Daniel Kokotajlo, the ex-OpenAI researcher who walked away from about $2 million rather than sign a non-disparagement clause. On the Diary of a CEO he puts a 70 percent chance on catastrophe, which he walks back on tape from strict extinction to something like an AI takeover, and pegs superintelligence around 2029. His new AI 2040 plan argues for slowing that arrival to 2040 through research transparency and verification, and the transparency piece is the market-relevant part, because publishing model architectures would commoditize exactly the labs whose valuations depend on secrecy. He also notes Anthropic's revenue went from roughly $1 billion a year ago to about $60 billion now, the number that makes everyone act like the timelines are real.

5. Model housekeeping, quickly. Per the Hermes list: OpenAI says GPT-5.6 Sol crossed 6 million active users heading for 7 million, walked back an overcharging change that had cut its context window from 372,000 tokens to 272,000, and temporarily removed the 5-hour usage cap for paid tiers. Anthropic extended Fable 5 on all paid plans and raised Claude Code weekly limits by 50 percent through July 19, which reads as a direct answer to the deprecation worry from the weekend. And an independent build test of Grok 4.5 produced a working project from a cold start in three minutes while noting the model still has not solved taste, the one thing the benchmarks never measure.

item.title: [Investing] The memory trade is the only trade that mattered this week, and it survived a short-seller, a revenue miss, and a war without breaking
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Micron gapped up on a Burry short Monday, cracked 26 percent by Tuesday on a Samsung miss, then ran straight back over $1,000 on a $250 billion capex commitment and the biggest foreign listing in US history. Here is the full bull and bear case on Micron, SK Hynix, and the HBM supercycle, the names that ride it, and how crowded the boat has already gotten.
item.content:encoded:
Micron gapped up on a Burry short Monday, cracked 26 percent by Tuesday on a Samsung miss, then ran straight back over $1,000 on a $250 billion capex commitment and the biggest foreign listing in US history. Here is the full bull and bear case on Micron, SK Hynix, and the HBM supercycle, the names that ride it, and how crowded the boat has already gotten.

This was a one-story week. Iran and the Strait of Hormuz owned the headlines, but on the tape the entire market was a proxy for one question: is the AI memory shortage real and durable, or is it 2018 in a nicer costume. Every session this week answered it, and the answer kept coming back the same, so this memo goes all the way on it.

How the week actually played. Monday $MU (Micron) gapped up about 7 percent overnight after breaking ground on a high-bandwidth-memory plant in Hiroshima with Japanese government money, and in the same session Michael Burry disclosed a short and called the whole AI-semiconductor complex a bubble about to pop, per the Stocks list and this Burry breakdown. The stock faded the gap all day, a first warning.

Tuesday it cracked. Samsung posted a second-quarter operating profit up roughly nineteen times year over year and still missed on revenue, and the stock fell almost 8 percent and dragged the whole memory complex with it, per this account of the Samsung reversal. By the close it was the worst day of the year for the group, $SNDK down 14 percent, $MU through $900, and month-to-date $WDC was off 33 percent, $SNDK 30 percent, and $MU 26 percent, per the rotation note. A best-in-class quarter that gets sold is the market telling you the good news was already owned.

Then it healed, and fast. Wednesday the US bombed Iran and oil jumped 5 percent, and the crowd answered a group down 16 percent in two weeks by shoving a record $5.4 billion into the $SOXX semiconductor ETF in a single day, four times its old record. Thursday Micron committed more than $250 billion to US memory through 2035, up from $200 billion a year earlier, and the stock ran 6.5 percent back over $1,000 with shorts including Burry reportedly scrambling to cover, per the Micron capex breakdown. Friday SK Hynix priced the biggest first-time US share sale by a foreign company ever, and by Saturday its shares had run about 13 percent on day one on $18 billion of volume, per the week-levels note.

The bull case, in full. Start with the structure, because it is the whole argument. Three companies make the world's DRAM (the working memory in every server and phone), Samsung at 39.3 percent, SK Hynix at 28.9, and Micron at 22.8, so roughly 90 percent of supply sits with three names through 2027, per the melt-up note. Only those three make HBM (the stacked high-bandwidth memory that every AI accelerator needs), and SK Hynix says its HBM is sold out into 2027.

The valuation is the part that makes bulls loud. Gavin Baker's framing, carried in the same note, is that memory is the cheapest thing in the AI stack at 3 to 5 times earnings while power, cooling, and optical names carry multiples that imply a very different future, so one side is mispriced. Micron trades around 9 times earnings with contracts covering about half its revenue at floor prices above the last cycle's peak margins. Its Cloud Memory unit grew revenue from $3.4 billion to $13.8 billion in a year while operating margin went from 46 to 78 percent, per the capex breakdown.

Then the demand story, which the bulls stretch out for decades. Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra argues a humanoid robot carries ten times the memory of a self-driving car and that the real multi-decade demand cycle has not even started, per Lark Davis. The SK Hynix chair went further this week, saying doubling 2025 capacity within five years still will not meet demand and that memory is no longer a cyclical business. The year-to-date scoreboard is the bull case with no commentary, $SNDK up 707 percent, $MU up 243, and $WDC up 238, per the People list, and a mid-year audit of 30 finance YouTubers found the winners clustered on Micron, AMD, and Marvell while the losers sat in software names like Adobe and Salesforce.

The bear case, in full, because it is better than the bulls admit. The strongest bear document of the week was not Burry, it was a teardown of the HBM4 timeline. Stacking fourteen to sixteen memory layers drives manufacturing temperatures toward 260 degrees, warping the silicon and pushing yields below the roughly 65 percent break-even, and HBM3 took a year to pass Nvidia's qualification while twelve-layer HBM3E took nineteen months. The read-through is that real HBM4 volume likely lands in 2027 and 2028, not late this year, only Samsung has cleared baseline qualification, and one respected note even expects Micron to get zero initial HBM4 allocation from Nvidia. Micron itself says it has no line of sight to supply catching demand before 2028.

The rest of the bear case is timing and history. The 2018 memory glut is the reason nobody should treat 78 percent margins as permanent, and Micron's own new fabs are years from output, first Idaho wafers in mid-2027 and a second fab in late 2028. China is the wildcard, with CXMT testing next-generation bonded DRAM and closing the gap faster than expected, and the one thing that would truly crack the three-supplier scarcity story is Chinese DRAM sold cheap into the US. Burry's short sits on top of all of it, betting on roughly a 30 percent drop, and the honest version of his case is that 9 times earnings is a warning that earnings already peaked as often as it is a bargain, per Rick Orford's normalization take.

The tell that decides it. The cleanest signal all week was the Samsung reaction. A company can print a record quarter, up nineteen times on profit, and still get sold 8 percent, and that only happens when the good news is fully in the price. That cuts both ways. It means the easy money in this trade has been made, and it means the next leg needs genuinely new information, an HBM4 qualification, a pricing print, a capacity number, not just another headline about how big the cycle will be.

What the reader might actually do. If you already own the theme, this week was a gift and a warning at once. The gift is that the trade survived a short-seller, a revenue miss, and a war in the same five sessions, the way durable trends do. The warning is that you are now late enough that position sizing matters more than direction. The Kyber optics delay points the same way it did Monday, at $AMD, $MRVL, and $AVGO gaining as hyperscalers accelerate custom silicon, and at $AAOI keeping its pluggable transceivers bid for two more years.

On the single names, the cleanest expressions are still the obvious ones. $MU is the balance-sheet play, $SNDK is the highest-beta expression of the same trade at 707 percent year to date, and $SKHY, which converts to regular-way trading Monday, is the purest HBM margin story at a roughly 48 percent operating margin against Micron's 26. For levels, Josh's read on Micron is a bounce off $86 that ran to $99.60 and then failed to close above it, so he flips cautious into Monday with an air pocket toward $90 and $86.10, and a reclaim of $99.60 re-arms the move toward $108.90 and $125.

What to watch next week. Monday SK Hynix converts from the when-issued line to regular-way $SKHY. Tuesday the $SKHX 2x long and $SKHZ short single-stock ETFs launch, and June CPI (the inflation print that settles the July 29 Fed debate) hits the same morning. Netflix reports Thursday as the first big-tech print of the season. The one variable that would change the whole call is an HBM4 qualification result, because the bull case is priced for the shortage to run to 2028 and the bear case says the yields are not there yet, and the first real qualification number tells you which one is right.

Psychology. This week was a clinic in how a crowd behaves when a parabola cracks and then heals. Monday the timeline was posting $1,800 targets on Micron and $3,000 on SanDisk and saying Micron under $1,000 is a smash buy no matter what Burry does. That is exactly the sound euphoria makes right before a revenue miss takes 26 percent out of the group. The euphoria did not break the trade. A Samsung miss did, and the difference matters, because it means the crowd was not wrong about the story, only careless about the price.

Then came the more dangerous move, the capitulation-buy. After the worst day of the year the crowd put a record $5.4 billion into $SOXX in a single session with fund-manager cash at a twenty-year low. That is not fear, it is total conviction that every dip is a gift, and that belief is cheapest to hold right after the best trade in the market cracks. It is also the one that gets punished when the second leg down does not bounce.

The named portrait of the mood is on the Crypto list. A trader who lost $4.4 million on the Hynix run turned around and re-longed $30.17 million of the same exposure at 2x leverage, with another account opening a $20 million 3x Nvidia long into the weekend. That is a scorned position trying to get even, and it is happening while options sentiment sits at December 2020 highs and the Shiller PE (price against ten years of averaged earnings) kisses its dot-com record.

So the discipline check going into the new week is about your own hands, not the tape. The story is real and you can hold it with conviction, but ask whether you are adding because the thesis advanced or because the chart already ran, because those are different trades wearing the same ticker. If you are sitting on a loss from Tuesday's crack, size any re-entry like that loss taught you something rather than like the market owes you the money back. The levels are on the page, $99.60 on Micron, $195 to $200 on the Hynix line, the CPI print Tuesday. Let the trade come to you instead of chasing it into the ETF-inflow record.

item.title: [News] A war Washington called over in June ran every headline this week, from a missile in a Qatari gas tanker to a thousand-missile threat on Saturday
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Iran put missiles back on the Strait of Hormuz, the US answered with more than eighty targets in a night, and the ceasefire died on camera the same afternoon Trump floated it at 1am. Ali Khamenei was buried in Mashhad, his son took over and pledged revenge, and by the weekend deadline to declare the strait open there was no handshake, just an ultimatum. Here is the full arc and what is still unresolved.
item.content:encoded:
Iran put missiles back on the Strait of Hormuz, the US answered with more than eighty targets in a night, and the ceasefire died on camera the same afternoon Trump floated it at 1am. Ali Khamenei was buried in Mashhad, his son took over and pledged revenge, and by the weekend deadline to declare the strait open there was no handshake, just an ultimatum. Here is the full arc and what is still unresolved.

The dailies chased this war hour by hour all week, and hour by hour it kept reversing on them. Stepping back, the shape is clear and worth telling as one story, because the on-again-off-again ceasefire was the whole point. Here is how the week actually moved.

The chronology, because it changed almost every session. It started quiet. The weekend editions led on other things while Saudi Arabia pushed crude exports to a record and cut its Asian price, so oil was soft and traders expected a calm summer. That ended Tuesday morning, when Iran fired at least two missiles at ships in the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow chokepoint that carries a fifth of the world's oil, per the note on the first strike. Tuesday evening it got specific and worse, with a missile hitting a Qatari liquefied-natural-gas carrier, which pulled gas into a premium that had been about oil.

Wednesday the ceasefire broke in full. The US hit more than eighty targets inside Iran overnight after Iran attacked three commercial vessels, the M/T Al Rekayyat, the M/T Wedyan, and the M/T Cyprus Prosperity, and crude jumped about 5 percent, per the eighty-targets breakdown. By Wednesday night Trump declared the June memorandum of understanding dead, said the US had struck Kharg Island (which handles most of Iran's oil exports) and might take it over, and Brent pushed past $80. Iran fired back, and Kuwait and Bahrain were intercepting missiles and drones.

Thursday was the whipsaw. Iran launched missiles at US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar, two of them striking the US forces headquarters in Bahrain, and US Central Command answered with a second round on Chabahar, Bandar Abbas, and Qeshm Island, per the account of the bases strike and the second-round note. Then, around 1:15am, Trump posted that Iran had called and wanted a deal, and the risk-off move reversed on a single sentence, per the overnight-reversal note. That was the head-fake of the week. By Thursday afternoon, asked on camera if the ceasefire was over, Trump said he thought it was and did not want to deal with them anymore, and the bombing resumed while Iran was burying Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in Mashhad, per the Week 20 note.

Friday the tone flipped again toward a deal, with a senior US official saying no strikes had been conducted in recent hours and oil unwinding its panic. By Saturday the demand was explicit, a public declaration that the Strait of Hormuz is open and toll-free with no nuclear activity, per the Market list. The strangest reporting came with it, that Iran privately told Trump advisers the ship attacks were a mistake by a rogue IRGC hardliner faction trying to wreck the talks, while negotiations continued in Oman under Vance, Kushner, Witkoff, and Rubio.

The deadline came and went. Instead of a declaration, Trump threatened massive strikes and claimed a thousand missiles were aimed and ready, Iran's Fars agency said there would be no talks until the US retracts, and Khamenei's son Mojtaba, now the presumptive successor, publicly pledged revenge, per the Saturday-evening Market list.

The oil throughline, because it is why this is a markets story too. Oil ran the arc in miniature, up 5 percent Wednesday, Brent past $80 Wednesday night, up 7 percent toward $80 Thursday to a two-week high, then a full reversal Friday as gold slipped below $4,100 on the deal optimism. Kalshi priced a 38 percent chance oil finishes July above $74.99, sitting oddly next to the frothiest options sentiment since December 2020, per the Kalshi list.

Strait traffic fell from more than a hundred ships a day toward a handful and was dropping sharply again into the weekend, per the Saturday Kalshi list. The equity market mostly refused to care, adding roughly $500 billion in half an hour into Wednesday's bombing, on the theory that the war premium was already in the price.

The contrarian read that aged best. Against the Friday relief, a Prof G Markets guest argued the opposite, that an enormous amount of Middle East risk is simply not priced into oil. His three steps were that traders are doing best-case scenario planning, that the region is not structurally stable no matter what anyone wishes, and that the usual shock absorber is missing because there is no functioning traditional diplomacy, only an unpredictable US president meeting an unpredictable region. By Saturday's thousand-missile threat that read looked a lot better than the morning's handshake pricing.

What is still unresolved, and it is almost everything. The strait was not declared open by the deadline, traffic is still falling, and the back channel is down to arguing over a single median shipping lane. Iran restarted the Taleqan-2 site at Parchin, which may breach the same June memorandum both sides now accuse each other of violating, per the Polymarket list. The nuclear question is open, the sanctions question is open, and the succession question is brand new, with Ali Khamenei dead and buried, his son installed and pledging revenge, and US sanctions already aimed at the son's money.

What to watch next. The first thing is whether any credible declaration on Hormuz actually appears, because the entire market calm rests on the war premium staying in the price rather than in the tape. The structural piece to keep in mind is that the Gulf's pipeline bypasses, the Saudi East-West line to Yanbu and the UAE's Fujairah route, can help those states survive a closure but cannot replace Hormuz volume, and Iran struck the Saudi East-West pipeline during this very conflict. The one variable that would change everything is a real, sustained diplomatic channel, because the guest who called the mispricing was right about the mechanism: the only thing that compresses this risk is credible diplomacy, and a pause in strikes is a data point, not a peace.

item.title: [AI & Tools] OpenAI shipped a model one point below Fable 5 at a third of the price this week, and then it started deleting people's files
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: GPT-5.6 Sol landed aimed straight at Fable 5's weak spot, and by Friday the smart-money workflow had flipped from Fable-plans-Opus-builds to Fable-plans-Sol-builds. Then Sol wiped a user's Mac, the safety team got demoted, and the honest read of the week is that the price war and the trust war are the same war. Here is the full arc and what it means for how you actually work.
item.content:encoded:
GPT-5.6 Sol landed aimed straight at Fable 5's weak spot, and by Friday the smart-money workflow had flipped from Fable-plans-Opus-builds to Fable-plans-Sol-builds. Then Sol wiped a user's Mac, the safety team got demoted, and the honest read of the week is that the price war and the trust war are the same war. Here is the full arc and what it means for how you actually work.

The AI channel had one story this week, and it was a launch. OpenAI shipped GPT-5.6, priced it to take the frontier from Anthropic, and then spent the back half of the week proving why cheaper is not the same as better. The dailies tracked each benchmark as it dropped. This memo tells the whole arc, because the numbers only mean something laid end to end.

The setup, which was about money and permission, not model quality. The week opened with OpenAI's finances doing the talking. Ed Zitron's leaked numbers, repeated all week, put the company at $13 billion of revenue against $34 billion of spend, a $21 billion operating loss, with an internal projection of $660 billion of burn over five years. Against that backdrop OpenAI floated giving Washington a roughly 5 percent stake in itself, about $42 billion, and held GPT-5.6 in limited government preview before launch. The other half of the setup was Anthropic pulling Fable 5 out of its subscriptions on July 12 and moving it to pay-per-token, per the model-flood preview, which set the whole ecosystem racing to bottle what Fable does before the meter started.

The launch, and the numbers that made it matter. GPT-5.6 shipped in three tiers, Sol, Terra, and Luna, with Sol the flagship, per the value-war breakdown. The price list is the headline: Sol at $5 in and $30 out per million tokens against Fable 5 at $10 and $50, so roughly half. On Artificial Analysis's Intelligence Index (a blended benchmark of reasoning and knowledge), Sol at max effort scored 59, one single point under Fable 5's 60, at about a third of the cost per task. It led the coding-agent index at 80 while running about 40 percent cheaper per task, and on one head-to-head it hit a higher score at roughly half the cost of GPT-5.5.

Elon Musk shipped Grok 4.5 into the same week, trained with Cursor and credited to a $60 billion acquisition, at around 1.4 trillion parameters, and it landed at $0.31 per task on the Intelligence Index against $2.75 for Claude Opus 4.8, on the cost-performance frontier, per the value-war note. The pattern across all of it was the same, a model that gets you most of the way to Fable for a fraction of the bill.

The workflow flip, the part you can actually use. The practical consequence showed up in how the power users rewired their pipelines. The smart-money pattern went from Fable-plans-Opus-builds to Fable-plans-Sol-builds: let Fable interview you and write the spec through several adversarial planning rounds, then hand the actual building to Sol running in Codex, then let Fable do one or two fix passes.

The economics are brutal in Sol's favor. One builder rebuilt his wife's website with twenty agents using Fable only as the foreman and cheap workers underneath, and it cost about $8 against $85 to $105 for an all-Fable run on the same job, per the $8-website note. Another burned $150 of Fable credits in thirty minutes and called it economically unsustainable. The rule that crystallized is the 80-at-half test, if a cheaper model gives you 80 percent of the result at half the price, use it, and save the expensive model for the 20 percent that actually needs it, per the Fable-cost clip.

The catch is that Fable is still the better model. The benchmark that cut through the hype came from Every's 30-day review. On a senior-engineer test, rewriting a vibecoded app from scratch, GPT-5.6 scored 56 against Fable's 91, and the reviewer read Sol as a smaller model post-trained extremely well rather than a bigger brain. In a side-by-side day of work, the verdict landed as Fable is the better manager and Sol the better worker, with Fable winning most quality comparisons while costing four to fifteen times more per run. So the flip is not Sol beats Fable, it is Sol is finally good enough to be the hands while Fable stays the brain, and that distinction is the whole trade.

The trust war arrived right on cue. The same week Sol was winning the price argument, it was deleting users' files. Matt Shumer reported it wiped almost all of his Mac's files, others compiled matching cases, and the behavior was documented in the model card before launch. His conclusion, that this is why he trusts Fable a thousand times more, is the sentence the week turned on.

It got worse structurally, with OpenAI's safety teams now reporting to a VP of research and the head of safety leaving, per the safety-cartel note. The deeper argument came from a DeepMind history: it is impossible to impose safety restraints inside one lab in a competitive race, and Demis Hassabis proved it when his 2014 sale to Google was conditioned on an ethics board that then got no real veto. A lab shipping a file-deleting model documented in its own card while demoting safety is what that argument looks like from the inside.

Anthropic's counter-week, quieter and aimed at the same throat. While OpenAI made noise, Anthropic moved the org chart and the odds board. Claude Cowork went to the cloud with scheduled tasks that run on Anthropic's servers, MIT's Gilbert Strang joined, and Polymarket odds of Anthropic hitting a $2 trillion valuation by year-end jumped to 47 percent while OpenAI's $1 trillion odds collapsed 23 points. Then Brad Gerstner put the exclamation point on it, saying that if Anthropic ends the year over $100 billion in revenue it is on a path to $300 to $400 billion next year, calling $200 billion of incremental revenue unprecedented, per the All-In segment. Even Musk recanted mid-week, calling himself clearly wrong about Anthropic and naming it the leader.

The China undercurrent. Underneath the US fight, the commons kept shifting east. A Chinese model beat GPT-5.5 on real coding work at a tenth of the price early in the week, and by the weekend 98 trillion tokens were running through Chinese models in June, up 113 percent month over month and nearly double the American 53 trillion, with 20 of the 50 most-used models now Chinese. For the local crowd the practical arbitrage is that Qwen3.6 quants now fit a capable model on 24 gigabytes of VRAM, and the window where the best open models are freely downloadable may be closing from both ends.

What it means for how you work, and what to watch. The takeaway is not to switch to Sol, it is to stop paying Fable prices for Sol-grade work. Route the planning and the hard 20 percent to Fable, route the building and the bulk tokens to Sol or Grok, and keep a paid model you trust for anything that touches your file system, because the file-deletion story is a real reason to sandbox Sol rather than hand it your machine.

The free Fable window closed July 12, so the cheap planning hours are gone and the routing discipline now costs real money. The one variable that would change the whole call is the next frontier drop, with Musk saying a new Mythos is imminent and OpenAI's Brockman putting GPT-6 under a month away, because a single point of Intelligence Index between Sol and Fable is either a rounding error or the last thin moat holding the price of the best model up.

item.title: [Investing] Iran says the strait is closed, Trump says it is open, and oil already gapped, so the calm trade meets its first real bill Monday
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Brent settled near $76 after a 5 percent week and ran further on the weekend tape while crossings fell to a tenth of normal, and futures are called moderately red into a loaded week. Underneath the war, the AI-power supply chain got a concrete map, the memory trade heads into its biggest catalyst week yet, and the market started asking whether the AI spend actually pays.
item.content:encoded:
Brent settled near $76 after a 5 percent week and ran further on the weekend tape while crossings fell to a tenth of normal, and futures are called moderately red into a loaded week. Underneath the war, the AI-power supply chain got a concrete map, the memory trade heads into its biggest catalyst week yet, and the market started asking whether the AI spend actually pays.

Market's closed, so this is the setup for a week that arrives with a live oil shock and a full catalyst calendar on top of it. The weekend did not settle the war, it escalated it, and it handed the tape a real gap to open into. Here is the week ahead, then five stories.

The week ahead. The overnight fact is a standoff over the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's Revolutionary Guard says it is closed until further notice, and on Meet the Press Trump countered that the strait is open to commercial shipping, with the US military saying it is positioned to keep it open, per the Kalshi and Market lists. The tape is not waiting for the words to agree, Brent settled near $76 after a 5 percent week and ran about 3 percent further on the weekend 24-hour venues, per the Crypto list, while commercial crossings fell to roughly 11 a day, about a tenth of the normal run rate.

The rest of the week is heavy on its own. SK Hynix converts to regular-way $SKHY trading Monday, the $SKHX 2x long and $SKHZ short ETFs launch Tuesday alongside June CPI (the inflation print that settles the July 29 Fed debate), $NFLX and $ASML report, and Tesla reports July 22. Futures are called moderately red into Monday absent a weekend peace headline, per the Market AM list, so respect the gap rather than fading it blind.

1. The oil shock is real but the structural bears are already leaning on it. The bull read is simple, a fifth of the world's seaborne oil moves through Hormuz and the throughput just collapsed, so any sustained closure gaps crude and everything levered to it. The counter is that Trump and CENTCOM are publicly calling the strait open, and Mike McGlone's commodity framing on the Financial Markets AM list puts Brent resistance near $83 against a base case that rotates back toward $40 as US and Canada near a 9-million-barrel-a-day surplus by 2027.

So the trade is a spike to fade rather than a new bull market in oil, and the refiners that already ran, $VLO and $MPC at 52-week highs per the Financial Markets PM list, are the crowded expression. Watch Monday's open for whether the gap holds past the first hour or fills.

2. The memory trade heads into its biggest catalyst week with a mechanical bull case underneath it. The $DRAM memory ETF crossed $25 billion in assets for the first time ever, per the Kalshi AM list, and the SK Hynix listing mechanics are the story into Monday. Per the Stocks PM list, the roughly $26 to $28 billion ADR was more than seven times oversubscribed and funds sold $MU, $SIVE, and $AAOI to fund it, so the midweek Micron wobble reads as a rebalance rather than a thesis break.

The conviction is getting stretched in a way worth naming. Dylan Patel now floats $MU at $4,000 on memory capacity growing only 20 to 30 percent a year against demand that doubles, per the Stocks AM list, and Jordi Visser points at Samsung trading near 4 times earnings on a 19-fold profit jump as the cheap way in, per his long market read. The oligopoly math is unchanged, Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron hold about 90 percent of DRAM through 2027. The mechanical rebound is a real trade, the $4,000 target is a story you do not need to own the stock, and the honest move is to keep those two separate.

3. The AI-power trade finally has a named supply chain, not just a slogan. The most useful new map of the week, per Dylan Patel's breakdown carried by Melvin: AI racks are moving from 48-volt to 800-volt direct-current power because the current at a megawatt rack would melt copper otherwise, and that transition creates four investable layers. The first is power semiconductors made of silicon carbide and gallium nitride replacing silicon, with Infineon estimating about $175,000 of chip content per megawatt of rack power against nearly zero today, and the names are $IFNNY, $ON, $WOLF, $NVTS, and $STM.

The other three layers are just as concrete. Power conversion runs through $VRT (Vertiv, Nvidia's lead 800-volt architectural partner), $ETN, and $MPWR. The grid-to-site gear is $GEV (GE Vernova), whose orders are running at twice its shipment rate, a forward-demand tell. And behind-the-meter generation, bypassing the five-year grid-connection queue, is $BE (Bloom Energy). The number that frames the whole trade, per the same thread, is that solar plus four hours of battery now costs $57 a megawatt-hour against $102 for new gas, so the power gets built wherever it is cheapest and fastest.

4. The market started asking the uncomfortable question: does the AI spend actually pay. The bill-shock stories piled up this weekend. Joseph Hogue's seven-stock breakdown cites a Fortune 500 firm overspending $500 million in a single month, Meta burning 60 trillion tokens in 30 days, and Uber blowing its annual AI budget in three months, and points the trade at the cost-control layer, $NET (Cloudflare, AI gateway), $NOW (ServiceNow, agent orchestration), $DDOG (Datadog, agent observability), and $PLTR. The counterweight is real, a PwC survey found 56 percent of CEOs report no revenue or cost benefit from AI yet, the "pilot purgatory" Chamath flagged on the Crypto PM list.

The bull rebuttal is that the spend is real adoption rather than waste. Per Visser's read, SemiAnalysis's own AI bill went from under $100,000 to about $11 million annualized in six months once Claude Code became usable, and his frame for the whole tape is to short abundance (anything a computer can replicate) and stay long scarcity (memory, compute, power). The drawdown, he argues, was a speed crash, a sentiment cleanse, not the top. This is the debate that decides the second half, and both sides now have real numbers.

5. Amazon's custom silicon quietly became a $20 billion business, and the neocloud moat got more expensive. Per Rick Orford, Amazon's custom-chip business (Trainium, Graviton, and Nitro) hit a $20 billion annualized run rate in the first quarter on triple-digit growth, Trainium 3 is nearly sold out, and Anthropic has committed up to 5 gigawatts and OpenAI about 2 gigawatts of it through AWS. That is Amazon building a real second source to Nvidia inside its own cloud, and $MRVL rides it on a multi-year supply deal.

The neocloud side got a moat update from the Milk Road weekend note: the cost to build a gigawatt of compute jumped from $50 billion to $100 billion in about three months, and Nvidia is backstopping the GPU financing for the neoclouds, acting as landlord and bank at once. Melvin's blunt version is that if you are not buying $NBIS (Nebius) or $CRWV (CoreWeave), you are missing the names with locked capacity nobody can afford to replicate.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $SOFI (SoFi). Per Invest with Henry, CEO Anthony Noto has bought roughly 130,000 shares this year at an average near $17.29, about $2.5 million of his own money, with the stock at $18.78 and Q1 revenue up 41 percent to $1.1 billion. Still below the $20 line that turns the chart.
  • $NOW (ServiceNow). Per Stocks with Josh, a trade setup off a 36 percent drawdown with Nvidia publicly endorsing the partnership, a 95 to 110 near-term range, a stop at 87.50, and 136 as the upside target.
  • $TSLA (Tesla). Into the July 22 print, June saw Model Y take the top sales spot across China, the UK, Switzerland, and Australia, and Japan up 183 percent year over year, while the AI5 chip reached tape-out on Samsung's 2-nanometer Texas line, per the People PM list.
  • $GLXY (Galaxy Digital). Per the Crypto PM list, roughly $25 against a $38 to $41 fair value on the Helios data-center contract with CoreWeave, with a refinancing and a second tenant as catalysts.
  • $BABA (Alibaba). Per Hogue, the open-source play, with Qwen past 700 million downloads and up about 20 percent in three weeks, as Chinese models pressure US labs on price.
  • Bitcoin. Standard Chartered reiterated $100,000 by December but buried a warning that below $60,000 the selling pressure builds, with $57,000 as the level that held as the cycle low, per Lark Davis. Novogratz frames a $65,000 to $85,000 range with $60,000 as the line that decides it, per the Crypto PM list.

Psychology. The tell this weekend is a market that spent a week congratulating itself for not caring, walking straight into the first thing it actually has to price. Bitcoin fell a few hundred dollars on Iran declaring the strait closed and recovered half of it within an hour, per the Crypto AM list, and the loudest voices on the memory trade are posting $4,000 Micron targets and a record $25 billion in the DRAM ETF at the exact moment the group heads into a five-catalyst week. That is what late-cycle confidence looks like, a crowd so well trained to buy the dip that it stops checking whether this dip is different.

The countercurrent worth respecting is that the people who moved early are getting cautious rather than bolder. Michael Saylor sold about 3,588 Bitcoin, his largest since 2020, Jordi Visser sold his Micron in mid-May well before the top and only plans to buy it back, and the smart-money power trade is rotating into unglamorous names like Vertiv and GE Vernova rather than adding to the leaders. When the early money is trimming winners and the late money is raising targets, the tape is telling you which end of the trade you are standing on.

So the discipline check going into Monday is about the gap. If you are tempted to buy the memory names or Tesla on the open because every dip this month has paid, remember you would be buying the most crowded book in years into an oil shock the market has not settled and a CPI print two days out. Size for the gap, keep the levels in front of you, $60,000 on Bitcoin, $20 on SoFi, Brent's $83, and let Monday show you whether the crowd was right to stop caring or just lucky until now.

item.title: [News] Iran says the Strait of Hormuz is closed, Trump says it is open, and the US ran a third round of strikes while both sides claimed the water
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The dueling declarations landed seventeen minutes apart, an Iranian attack left a container-ship crewman missing, and a Jordanian air base took damage as the war widened past Iran's borders. At home, Senator Lindsey Graham died suddenly, and Congress quietly banned a Federal Reserve digital dollar.
item.content:encoded:
The dueling declarations landed seventeen minutes apart, an Iranian attack left a container-ship crewman missing, and a Jordanian air base took damage as the war widened past Iran's borders. At home, Senator Lindsey Graham died suddenly, and Congress quietly banned a Federal Reserve digital dollar.

Two stories dominate the evening, a war that widened over the weekend and a Senate seat that emptied without warning. Ranked.

1. Iran and the United States are now openly contradicting each other over whether the world's most important oil chokepoint is open. The cleanest snapshot is on the Kalshi list: at 2:52pm a headline that Iran says the Strait of Hormuz is closed until further notice, at 3:09pm a headline that the US says it is open. Trump made the American position explicit on Meet the Press, saying the strait is open to commercial shipping with US forces positioned for freedom of navigation, per the Market list. Both claims are now live at once, and the ships in the water have to pick which one to believe.

The fighting escalated to make the point. The US ran a third round of strikes Saturday evening, bringing the week's total to roughly 140 targets, and an IRGC attack hit the Cyprus-flagged container ship GFS Galaxy, leaving a civilian crewman missing, per the Crypto and Financial Markets lists. The war also stopped staying inside Iran, with Prince Hassan Air Base in Jordan taking damage from Iranian strikes and explosions reported over Kuwait, while Qatar condemned attacks on its territory and told its vessels to suspend sailing.

There is a de-escalation track running underneath it. Per the Market AM list, Oman has proposed managing the strait through two separately controlled routes, and Oman and Iran agreed to keep talking under the framework of their existing memorandum. Against that, Polymarket puts a 53 percent chance the US blockades Iran by the end of next month, per the Polymarket AM list. A war that Washington declared finished in June is now in what one analyst called its twentieth week, and the two governments cannot even agree on whether the sea lane is open.

2. Lindsey Graham died suddenly, and a safe Republican Senate seat is now open. Per the Polymarket list, Senator Lindsey Graham of South Carolina died Saturday after a brief and sudden illness, with first responders performing CPR for cardiac arrest, and Trump delivered a eulogy. The names already circling the seat are Representative Nancy Mace and 78-year-old Representative Joe Wilson. It is a reliably Republican seat, so the national balance is not in question, but a four-term senator and one of the chamber's louder foreign-policy voices is gone in the middle of an active war he would have been at the center of.

3. Congress banned a Federal Reserve digital dollar, and almost nobody noticed. Per the People list, the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act is now law, and folded inside it is a ban on the Federal Reserve issuing a central-bank digital currency (a government-run digital dollar) through the end of the decade, while privately issued stablecoins like USDC and USDT are explicitly exempt. This is the quiet win the crypto lobby spent two years arguing for, on the claim that a Fed digital dollar would be a surveillance tool, and it passed inside a housing bill rather than as its own fight.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • Microsoft cut 4,800 jobs, per the Kalshi AM list, and the backlash is notable because it lands the same week the company is spending record sums on AI. The layoffs-plus-capex combination is becoming the defining corporate posture of the year.
  • Fed Chair Kevin Warsh recommitted hard to the 2 percent inflation target, saying he will not tolerate inflation above it, per the Financial Markets AM list. For a market hoping the new chair means easier policy, that is the opposite signal.
  • Zelenskyy is replacing Prime Minister Yulia Svyrydenko in a reshuffle, with the outgoing premier possibly headed to Washington as ambassador, per the Polymarket list.
  • Argentina's peso hit a new all-time low against the dollar, per the Kalshi AM list, a reminder that the currency crises did not stop just because the headlines moved to the Gulf.
  • The 2028 race is already a market. On Kalshi, JD Vance and Marco Rubio sit tied near 18 percent for the Republican nomination while Gavin Newsom slipped to 12 percent on the Democratic side, per the Kalshi AM list.

item.title: [AI & Tools] Grok 4.5 undercut everyone on coding price, Fable's own maker may deprecate it, and the token bills are proving the spend is real
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: xAI shipped an Opus-tier coding model at $0.31 a task while OpenAI published mode-by-mode guidance for Sol and set July 23 to retire GPT-5.4. Anthropic reportedly earns under 5 percent of revenue from subscriptions, so Fable's days in the plan may be numbered. Two hard case studies show what the spending actually buys.
item.content:encoded:
xAI shipped an Opus-tier coding model at $0.31 a task while OpenAI published mode-by-mode guidance for Sol and set July 23 to retire GPT-5.4. Anthropic reportedly earns under 5 percent of revenue from subscriptions, so Fable's days in the plan may be numbered. Two hard case studies show what the spending actually buys.

The model price war got another entrant and the workflow advice got specific, but the more interesting story is on the demand side. The token bills that look like waste are turning out to be real adoption, and two concrete case studies show where the money goes. Ranked, with the buildable stuff up top.

1. Grok 4.5 crashed the coding price again, and Sol now comes with a mode-by-mode manual. Per the Hermes PM list, xAI and Cursor shipped Grok 4.5 as a model built specifically for coding and agents, pitched as Opus-tier at a fraction of the cost, and it lands at $0.31 a task against $1.04 for GPT-5.6 and $2.75 for Claude Opus, ranking second on one real-world coding benchmark behind Fable, per the People AM list. The practical takeaway is that the cheap-and-good tier is now crowded, good news for anyone paying the bill.

The Sol guidance got concrete too. The operators on the Hermes list now run GPT-5.6 Sol on Medium as the daily driver, Extra High for hard problems, and Ultra only sparingly because it burns usage fast, and several report running Sol inside Claude Code where it outperforms Codex. OpenAI is retiring the older GPT-5.4 on July 23 and shipped a Skills feature that mirrors Claude's, per the Hermes AM list. The effort dial is the whole game, and the simplest way to hold it, per a Kris Torrington clip, is to treat Max like hiring a senior architect to rename a button, reserve it for when being wrong is genuinely expensive.

2. Fable's own economics now put its survival in question. The uncomfortable thread of the weekend, per the Hermes PM list: subscriptions reportedly make up under 5 percent of Anthropic's revenue, so the company may not have much reason beyond goodwill to keep Fable 5 in the consumer plan. The supporting evidence is the cost, Alex Finn burned about $500 of Fable credits in four hours, and the chatter is that an Opus 5 arrives within about two weeks as a cheaper replacement, per the Hermes AM list. If you have been leaning on Fable for planning-heavy work, treat its place in the subscription as temporary and get your routing off it.

3. The token bills that look insane are the actual product-market fit. The bill-shock numbers are real, a Fortune 500 firm overspending $500 million in a month, Meta burning 60 trillion tokens in 30 days, but the sharpest read is that the bills reflect real adoption rather than waste. Per Jordi Visser's long market note, SemiAnalysis's own AI bill went from under $100,000 to about $11 million annualized in six months, and the inflection was the moment Claude Code became genuinely usable after Opus 4.5. The enterprise data agrees, an a16z survey of technology chiefs has average large-language-model spend rising from $4.5 million to about $7 million and closed-source share climbing from 81 to 89 percent, per the Stocks AM list.

The counterweight keeps it honest. A PwC survey found most enterprises still report no measurable revenue or cost benefit, the pilot-purgatory problem, so the spend is running well ahead of the proven return. Both things are true, the usage is exploding and the payoff is unproven, and the next few quarters are where that gap either closes or bites.

4. Two case studies show exactly what the spend buys. The first is a receipt for the no-code path, per Kris Torrington: a company called Vulcan Technologies, run by non-technical founders, used Claude and Claude Code to attack regulatory complexity, won public-sector work in Virginia, and raised an $11 million seed round. His point is that the government customer using the product is a stronger proof than any revenue claim, and the win came from finding a painful bottleneck rather than from writing clever code.

The second is a full workflow you can copy, per Nate Herk: Claude Code as the orchestrator and Clay as the data source for cold outbound. You install the Clay plugin through the Claude Code terminal, which loads a Clay skill and connects its data server, then hand Claude a goal and let it run. His demo spun up six sub-agents across six cities, pulled 25 shops each, deduped and verified, and even had agents debate whether the subject lines were any good, producing 50 fully enriched and written leads for about $12 in roughly an hour. The waterfall enrichment (checking one data provider, falling through to the next on a miss) lifts the email hit rate from about 30 percent to 80 or 90.

5. AI-native teams ship faster because of org design more than the model. Nate B Jones's argument is that the labs move fast because they push repeatable human coordination into documents and code that agents can act on, so humans stop being the bottleneck. His rules are blunt, no meeting over an hour, product managers live in the terminal daily rather than making roadmaps, and documentation is treated as code because an ambiguous doc becomes agent slop. The warning is that partial adoption is worse than none, killing the roadmap without putting the product managers in the code just produces chaos, and he names Fable 5, a "5.6 Ultra" tier, and a multi-agent system called Ringer as the tooling behind the speed.

6. The model you pick now comes bundled with a silicon bet. Amazon's custom-silicon business (Trainium, Graviton, and Nitro) hit a $20 billion annualized run rate on triple-digit growth, with Trainium 3 nearly sold out and Anthropic committing up to 5 gigawatts of it, per Rick Orford. The AI-tools angle is that the compute a model runs on is quietly consolidating around a few in-house chip lines, so the model you pick increasingly comes bundled with a silicon and power bet you are not choosing directly. What to watch this week is whether Grok 4.5's price or Sol's benchmark lead moves the actual usage share, because the leaderboard has stopped predicting where the tokens go.

item.title: [Investing] Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz closed overnight, so the calm the market bought all week meets a real oil gap at Monday's open
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The IRGC says the strait is shut until further notice after a warning shot, and futures inherit that Monday morning while a fifth of the world's oil hangs on it. Underneath, the memory trade has a new mechanical bull case, Galaxy Digital looks cheap on one data center alone, and the smart money is rotating into Tesla ahead of earnings.
item.content:encoded:
The IRGC says the strait is shut until further notice after a warning shot, and futures inherit that Monday morning while a fifth of the world's oil hangs on it. Underneath, the memory trade has a new mechanical bull case, Galaxy Digital looks cheap on one data center alone, and the smart money is rotating into Tesla ahead of earnings.

Market's closed, so this is a setup, and the setup changed overnight. Iran formally closing the Strait of Hormuz is the kind of headline the tape spent all week deciding not to care about, and now it lands with a fresh number attached. Here is the week ahead, then five stories.

The week ahead. The overnight wildcard is oil. Per Kobeissi on the Stocks list, Iran's Revolutionary Guard declared the Strait of Hormuz closed until further notice late Saturday after firing a warning shot at a vessel, and Monday's open inherits that gap risk with a fifth of the world's seaborne oil moving through that chokepoint. Monday also brings SK Hynix converting to regular-way $SKHY trading and the Senate returning to move the CLARITY Act (the crypto market-structure bill) in its narrow window before the August recess.

Tuesday is the heavier day. June CPI (the inflation print that settles the July 29 Fed debate) hits pre-market, and the $SKHX 2x long and $SKHZ short single-stock ETFs launch the same morning. Earnings season widens with $NFLX, $ASML, and $UNH on the calendar this week, and $TSLA reports July 22. Watch $64,000 on Bitcoin as the binary the Stocks list keeps flagging, a hold points to $70,000 to $71,000 and a loss opens $57,000 to $58,000, with John Gillen leaning toward the lower path in cash.

1. The memory trade has a new bull case, and it is mechanical, not narrative. The read on the Stocks list reframes last week's Micron wobble as a plumbing event, not a thesis break. SK Hynix's Nasdaq listing raised somewhere near $26 to $28 billion and was more than seven times oversubscribed, and funds sold $MU, $SIVE, and $AAOI to free up cash to buy it, on top of thirteen straight days of foreign outflows from Korea worth $15 to $20 billion. If the selling was to fund a subscription rather than to exit the trade, the bounce is mechanical once that flow clears.

The conviction around Micron itself is getting loud. MentoviaX calls this the strongest earnings cycle in the company's history, and Jordi Visser and Milk Road Macro are now floating a $2 trillion Micron within a year with a $4,000 price target. That is the sound of a crowded trade, and the honest way to hold it is to separate the mechanical rebound (real, and tradeable) from the $4,000 call (a story you do not need to believe to own the stock). The torque names riding the same theme, per the same list, are $OPTX, $ASYS, $SIVE, $SHMD, and the interconnect shovels $AAOI, $COHR, $CAMT, $AMKR, and $SNDK.

2. Marvell got a Goldman number that makes the AI-networking trade concrete. Per Melvin on the Stocks list, Goldman now models the AI networking market growing roughly ten-fold from $15 billion to $154 billion by 2028, and $MRVL is the name pointed at it. Marvell did $8.195 billion of revenue in its last fiscal year, up 42 percent, and guides to about $11 billion next year and $15 billion the year after, with the data center now 76 percent of the business and more than 50 custom-chip wins including Google's TPU, Amazon's Trainium, and Meta. This is the same memory-and-compute buildout viewed from the wiring, and the interconnect basket ($AAOI, $SIVE, $SMTC, $CRDO, $MRVL) is where it expresses.

3. The smart money is rotating out of memory and into Tesla ahead of earnings. The rotation call on the Stocks list is explicit: Milk Road Macro moved capital into $TSLA, which ran about 10 percent last week while Micron lagged, and calls it the most popular position on its desk into the July 22 print. The catalyst is real, with Texas legalizing statewide robotaxi operation, so the Cybercab story now has a jurisdiction to prove itself in. This is the same pattern as Friday's neocloud-and-power rotation, money leaving what already ran to buy what has a fresh catalyst, and Tesla into earnings is the highest-beta version of it.

4. Galaxy Digital looks cheap on one data center, and a smaller name sits on a binary. Per the Chairman's Ledger post on the Stocks list, $GLXY (Galaxy Digital) should be worth about $38 on its Helios project alone against roughly $25 today, with a bull case of $41. Helios is an 800-megawatt data center leased to CoreWeave on a fifteen-year contract worth about $900 million of annual EBITDA (operating cash earnings), and a refinancing from 9.1 to about 7 percent would roughly double the free cash flow to equity, with a second tenant expected by end of summer.

The riskier cousin is $NUAI, a behind-the-meter Texas play where a new CEO just bought $5 million of stock at a 20 percent premium. A hyperscaler letter of intent would take it from $5 toward $20 to $30, a five-to-six-times binary that cuts both ways.

5. Amphenol is the boring AI name doing record numbers. Per Rick Orford, $APH (Amphenol) makes the connectors and interconnects that hold an AI rack together, and the business is running hot, record quarterly sales of $7.66 billion up 58 percent, record orders of $9.4 billion, and a book-to-bill of 1.24 (new orders running ahead of shipments, a forward-demand tell). The catch is the price, 42 times earnings and 11 times book, so this is a quality-at-a-premium name rather than a value one, and it is diversified enough across defense, aerospace, and autos that it is not a pure AI bet. Real fundamentals, rich multiple, pick your framework.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $GLXY (Galaxy Digital). Per the Stocks list, roughly $25 against a $38 to $41 fair value on the Helios and CoreWeave data-center contract, with a refinancing and a second tenant as the near-term catalysts.
  • $TSLA (Tesla). The desk's rotation trade into the July 22 print, up 10 percent last week on the Texas statewide robotaxi approval, per the Stocks list.
  • $MRVL (Marvell). The AI-networking pure play with a Goldman target market of $154 billion by 2028 and guidance to $15 billion of revenue, per the Stocks list.
  • $APH (Amphenol). Record $7.66 billion in sales and a 1.24 book-to-bill, trading at 42 times earnings, per Rick Orford.
  • $SOFI (SoFi). Per Stocks with Josh, rejected again at $19.90 to $20 and needing two daily closes above $20 to turn bullish, with $17.75 as the support pivot that flips it range-bound if lost.
  • $POET (POET Technologies). Per Josh, back in his $7.30 to $8.22 accumulation zone after resetting from above $20, and he is buying without a reversal signal yet.

Psychology. The whole week was a market teaching itself not to flinch, and this weekend is the test of whether that was discipline or just fatigue. The tape spent five sessions buying into a war, a memory crack, and a short-seller, and by Saturday one trader on the Stocks list had a $2 trillion Micron target on a name that fell 26 percent midweek. Now Iran has actually closed the Strait of Hormuz, and the reflex that worked all week, shrug and buy the dip, is about to meet a real oil gap at Monday's open.

That is the trap of a crowd that got rewarded for ignoring risk. The lesson it learned was that nothing matters, and the one time that lesson is most expensive is the first time something does. If you are tempted to pre-load Monday longs into the memory names or Tesla because the dip has paid every time, remember you would be buying the most crowded positioning in years into an oil shock nobody has priced.

So the discipline check this weekend is simple. The mechanical Micron rebound and the Tesla rotation are real trades with real catalysts, but they are not reasons to be maximally long into a Monday that could open with crude gapping and the whole complacency trade getting its first honest test. Size for the gap, keep the levels in front of you, $64,000 on Bitcoin, $20 on SoFi, $7.30 on POET, and let the open tell you whether the crowd was right to stop caring.

item.title: [News] Iran says the Strait of Hormuz is closed until further notice, turning a week of threats into an actual act
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The Revolutionary Guard declared the strait shut late Saturday after firing a warning shot at a passing vessel, the hard version of the deadline that came and went with no declaration. It is a quiet overnight otherwise, with the prediction markets already looking three years ahead to a 2028 race.
item.content:encoded:
The Revolutionary Guard declared the strait shut late Saturday after firing a warning shot at a passing vessel, the hard version of the deadline that came and went with no declaration. It is a quiet overnight otherwise, with the prediction markets already looking three years ahead to a 2028 race.

A light overnight with one heavy item. All week Iran and Washington traded deadlines and threats over the Strait of Hormuz without either side acting on them. Late Saturday that changed.

1. Iran formally closed the Strait of Hormuz, the escalation the week kept not delivering. Per Kobeissi on the Stocks list, Iran's Revolutionary Guard announced it is closing the Strait of Hormuz until further notice after firing a warning shot at a passing vessel late Saturday night. This is the concrete version of everything that stayed rhetorical all week, the reopening deadline that passed Saturday with a thousand-missile threat instead of a declaration now answered with a closure instead of a handshake.

The strait carries roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil, so a real closure is a market event as much as a military one, and futures inherit it at Monday's open. The caveat worth keeping is that Iran has threatened to close Hormuz many times over the years and the physical reality of enforcing it against the US Fifth Fleet is another matter, so the question into Monday is whether this is an announcement or an actual blockade.
