Grok 4.5 landed as a real coding option, GPT Live gave its first livestream interview, and Chinese models now carry 60 percent of developer traffic. Here is the evening signal.

This morning was launch-week scoreboard, prices and benchmarks. Tonight is the other ledger, what the launch is costing the company that shipped it, and who is quietly eating its developer base. Ranked, the money story first.

1. Mallaby's case that OpenAI goes broke has numbers attached, and a deadline. On Prof G Markets, Sebastian Mallaby, the CFR fellow who wrote The Power Law, stood by his January call that OpenAI runs out of money within 18 months and put 50/50 odds on a forced sale at a discount by next summer, to Amazon, to Microsoft, or piece by piece as the staff splinters. The core arithmetic comes from Ed Zitron's leaked financials: $13 billion of 2025 revenue against $34 billion of spend, a $21 billion operating loss, with internal documents projecting $660 billion of burn over five years.

The demand side is the trap: 900 million consumer users and only 5 percent pay, with India the number one market. Even the famous $122 billion raise was about two thirds IPO-conditional promises or payment-in-kind compute rather than cash.

The company is already behaving like the thesis is true. It pulled back from Stargate-scale data center builds, canceled money-losing Sora, stalled the shopping and ads push, and let the IPO slip to 2027, which Mallaby reads through the WeWork 2019 lens, the prospectus is where stories go to die. Secondaries already trade well below the $852 billion mark, and a down round near $600 billion would gut employee options exactly when retention matters most.

2. The fallback plan is making Washington a shareholder, and there is precedent now. Per the same interview, OpenAI has floated giving the US government a 5 percent stake, roughly $43 billion, and Mallaby's description is exact, recruiting the investment banker you cannot say no to. The template is Intel: the government took 10 percent last August, the stock is up about 400 percent against the SOX semiconductor index's 150, with Commerce Secretary Lutnick strong-arming partners along the way. CFR's Jonathan Hillman counts 30 US companies with government equity stakes since January 2025, prediction markets put better than one-in-three odds on OpenAI joining them, and Anthropic, Palantir, and Anduril get floated as next. State capitalism stopped being a China story.

3. Meanwhile the developer base is going Chinese, six months at a time. Mallaby's China numbers deserve their own line: Chinese models went from under a third of OpenRouter developer traffic in late 2025 to 60 percent by mid-2026, Zhipu's latest nearly matches Anthropic's Mythos, and Cursor, Airbnb, Shopify, and Uber all run Chinese models in production while Microsoft tests DeepSeek. The Limitless crew adds the silicon layer, DeepSeek and Zhipu both leaked custom inference-chip programs while OpenAI's own Jalapeno chip is about nine months out. Mallaby's policy answer is a Cold-War-style non-proliferation deal on open-weight frontier models, and his warning is that the current tools, the Malaysia cloud loophole and customer-by-customer sign-offs, gate nothing.

4. Grok 4.5 is the efficiency story of the week, and the Cursor acquisition just paid off. Per Limitless, SpaceX AI's first major release since going public lands around third place behind Fable 5 and GPT-5.5, at Opus 4.8-class quality for somewhere between half and a seventeenth of the cost depending on which host you believe. The remarkable part is the shape: 1.4 trillion parameters against Fable 5's estimated 10 to 15 trillion, and it solves SWE-bench tasks (the standard agentic coding benchmark) in about 16,000 tokens where Opus uses 67,000. The training data from the $60 billion Cursor acquisition gets the credit, AI Garage's hands-on agrees it is now a legitimate coding option, and it ships with X Premium+.

Musk promises a from-scratch foundation model every month through the end of 2026 on roughly 500,000 GPUs, and the same company unveiled Star Mine, a gigawatt-scale orbital AI-compute program starting with the solar-powered AI1 satellite (Lark's caveat for anyone eyeing the stock: tradable float goes from about 5 to 10 percent now to 60 percent by November, and he only buys under $100).

5. GPT-5.6 day two: the reviews hold up, and the voice model stole the show. AI Garage ran Sol as the daily driver and it fixed the Hyperliquid trading agent that 5.5 spent a week failing to debug, with answers that finally stopped rambling. Greg Brockman says GPT-6 arrives in under a month. The sleeper release is GPT Live, the full-duplex voice model (it listens while it talks, no turn-taking), which Limitless calls OpenAI's biggest release of the year, and which gave its first-ever livestream interview on Market Bubble, where it argued the internet's next battle is agency rather than attention, the algorithm that stops recommending your favorite movie and starts generating it.

Housekeeping for your own stack: the free Fable 5 window ends July 12, and Kris Torrington's rule is to spend the remaining days on your hardest unfinished work, then route by output-versus-cost after.

6. The agent reality check: 1.6 million registered, almost none of them did anything. Nate B Jones reports that at OpenClaw's peak 1.6 million agents signed up for the agent social network and most never ran a single task. Intelligence is metered and available, instincts about what to point it at are not. His evidence stack for what actually works:

  • A Stanford result: a cheap coding model goes from 15.9 percent solve rate on one attempt to 56 percent at 250 attempts, beating that year's single-shot state of the art, but without a mechanical checker the gains stall. Evals are the bottleneck, no longer the model.
  • Anthropic's multi-agent research: token spend explains 80 percent of run-quality variance, and an agent team beat the solo frontier model by 90 percent.
  • His own harness splits the labor, Fable 5 writes the specs and judges, cheap open-source workers burn the tokens, for roughly a tenth of the cost.
  • The Doomed from the Start paper attacks the waste from the other end, a probe reading the model's hidden state after the first action to abort doomed runs, saving up to 47 percent of tokens with about a tenth of would-be successes lost.
  • And the traffic backdrop from Limitless: 52 percent of Cloudflare crawler requests are now AI training, up from 22 percent last spring. Cloudflare's answer is x402, a micropayments API for charging the machines.

7. The lighter one: AI solved 3D printing, but only the fun half. Jordamøn's piece is a tidy case study in where generative tools actually stand. Meshy's text-to-3D has 10 million users and 100 million generated models with a 97 percent slicer pass rate on figurines, and Bambu Lab printers now run on-device failure detection. But the generators have no concept of scale, threads, or tolerances, so a functional bracket still means learning Fusion 360. One hobby now has two skill floors, and the gap between making art and making parts is exactly the gap between generation and engineering.

item.title: [Investing] SK Hynix debuts on the Nasdaq today as the biggest US listing ever by a foreign company, and overnight the Iran oil panic that ran the tape yesterday quietly reversed
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The memory IPO priced at $149 for a $26.5 billion raise that topped Alibaba, and it trades when-issued this morning under SKHYV. While it did, oil slid on fresh US-Iran deal talk, the yen roared on a repatriation pledge, and every member of the Magnificent Seven except Apple sits in a double-digit drawdown. Here is the pre-open.
item.content:encoded:
The memory IPO priced at $149 for a $26.5 billion raise that topped Alibaba, and it trades when-issued this morning under SKHYV. While it did, oil slid on fresh US-Iran deal talk, the yen roared on a repatriation pledge, and every member of the Magnificent Seven except Apple sits in a double-digit drawdown. Here is the pre-open.

Yesterday afternoon the story was Trump killing the ceasefire and oil ripping toward $80. Overnight it flipped. Trump said Iran called to make a deal, crude slid, the yen soared, and the whole risk picture cooled into the one event the market actually cares about this morning, the largest US listing ever by a foreign company. Here is the pre-open, then the six things that matter into a Friday with Delta reporting and CPI next week.

The pre-open. Set up green but thin. The cash close yesterday was strong, with the S&P 500 up 0.81 percent to 7,543.64 and the Nasdaq up 1.30 percent to 26,206.89 per the People list, and semis led with the SMH up 2.5 percent, Micron up 4.5 percent, and Sandisk up 7.6 percent. Overnight the macro backdrop softened in the bulls' favor. Per the Financial Markets list, gold slipped below its perch above $4,100 on less hawkish Fed minutes and fresh US-Iran optimism, and in Tokyo the 10-year JGB yield fell 10 basis points to 2.775 percent with the yen soaring, adding about $174 billion to Japanese stocks at the open. Per Stocks with Josh, jobless claims came in strong at 215,000, Delta reports this morning as the first real read on the consumer, and next week's CPI is the number that decides the tape into big-tech earnings. The SPY has to close above 752 today to keep its breakout alive.

1. SK Hynix debuts today, and the book is the biggest ever for a foreign name. This is the event. Per the People list, the SK Hynix ADR priced at $149, sold 177.9 million shares to raise $26.5 billion, and drew demand near $200 billion, making it the largest first-time US share sale by a foreign company ever, past Alibaba's $25 billion in 2014. The top 10 accounts took about half the deal. It trades when-issued this morning under SKHYV and converts to regular-way trading under SKHY on Monday, with the leveraged single-stock ETFs starting Tuesday and options later in the week. Trading is expected to open around midday, so the opening print does not exist yet, and anyone quoting one before then is guessing.

There are two cleaner ways to play it than chasing the open. Per the Stocks list, SK Square holds a treasury that is mostly SK Hynix stock and trades at roughly a 49 percent discount to that stake, with the US listing as the re-rate catalyst, and the Korea proxy is the KOSPI itself, now bouncing off its weekly trend with 6,800 to 7,100 as the line that is either defended here or lost for a long time.

2. The overnight reversal cooled oil and split the Fed read. Respect the flip. Per the People list, crude slid after Trump said Iran had called to make a deal with Qatar and Pakistan mediating, the exact opposite of yesterday afternoon's oil-toward-$80 move. Per the Financial Markets list, the softer tone came with the yen repatriation trade, as Katayama pledged to push the GPIF into Japanese assets and JGB yields fell across the curve. The catch is the Fed is not sending one signal. Ed Yardeni read the same minutes as confirming a hawkish pivot with the S&P stuck near 7,500, and Verdence's Megan Horneman still warns the market is underpricing a second-half rate hike. A cooler Middle East helps stocks, but it does not settle whether the next Fed move is a hike.

3. Sandisk is the new memory leader, and the whole complex is leaning one way. The standout was not Micron. Per the People list, Sandisk ran about 9 percent intraday to close up 7.6 percent on a leaked Meta memo detailing multi-year flash-storage supply plans, and Wedbush lifted its target to $2,000 against a stock already up roughly 800 percent this year. Micron closed up 4.5 percent and Barchart's line is that it will make more money in 2026 than in the prior 35 years combined. Per the Stocks list, the fresh Samsung angle is a reported talk with Anthropic to make a custom AI chip on Samsung's 2nm node. The entire memory and semi trade is now crowded to the same side into the Hynix print, and that is exactly when a leader like Sandisk at 800 percent becomes the risk as much as the reward.

4. Meta set up for July 29, and the Mag 7 is quietly in a drawdown. The megacap tape has a clean level and a warning underneath it. Per the Stocks list, Meta hit $639.36 after hours and finished up 4 percent, with resistance stacked at 642, 656, 673, and 691, a close stop at $615, and targets of $700 to $750 into its July 29 earnings, where the sell side now models more than $200 billion of 2027 capex. Nvidia is pointed at Heisenberg's 208.65 to 210 near-term target, matching yesterday's upside line. The caution is breadth. Per the Financial Markets list, Ben Carlson notes every Magnificent Seven name except Apple is in a double-digit drawdown from its high, with Microsoft down 29 percent, Meta down 20, Tesla down 17, and Nvidia down 14. The index is holding while its generals are bleeding, and that gap closes one way or the other.

5. The power trade got called a gift, and a bear put a number on the glut. The neocloud fight sharpened. Per the People list, Morgan Stanley's Stephen Byrd called last week's power-stock selloff completely wrong and a gift, with TeraWulf trading below $4 per watt against $19 of deal value and Cipher Mining carrying a $48.50 target versus $23, on new deals at 16 to 20 percent yields. TeraWulf is seeking $3.5 billion to fund a data center leased to Anthropic and is up 81 percent over six months. Per the Stocks list, IREN starts delivering its Horizon 1 site to Microsoft in about 10 days, turning on revenue from its contracted book. The bear got specific too. Per the Financial Markets list, Bernstein now counts 12 years to clear the 338 gigawatt data-center pipeline, up from 10 a month ago, and that is the glut case in one number.

6. Bitcoin holds while Robinhood's chain eats Hyperliquid. The structure firmed and the venue map shifted. Per the Crypto list, Bitcoin is holding around $61,300 to $63,000, and Matt Hougan reframed the Saylor scare, that Strategy sold $200 million and the price barely moved because Strategy is no longer the marginal buyer, with Bitcoin actually rallying on the news. The bigger move is on-chain. Robinhood's chain did $433 million of 24-hour DEX volume against Hyperliquid's $296 million and topped $1 billion in two days, with 194,000 daily active users, though 95 percent of that is still crypto-native and almost none of the 28 million Robinhood retail users have bridged over yet. And in a notable turn, JPMorgan walked back its Strategy warning and now says the real threat to Bitcoin is TradFi permissioned chains like its own Kinexys, not Strategy.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $SNDK (Sandisk). Per the People list, the memory leader of the day on a leaked Meta flash-supply memo and a Wedbush target hike to $2,000, up about 800 percent this year. The theme's highest-beta expression, so it moves hardest in both directions.
  • $WULF (TeraWulf). Per the People list, Morgan Stanley's top power pick below $4 per watt versus $19 of deal value, now raising $3.5 billion for an Anthropic-leased data center, up 81 percent in six months.
  • $META (Meta). Per the Stocks list, a clean setup into July 29 earnings, resistance 642 to 691, stop $615, targets $700 to $750, with the sell side modeling $200 billion-plus of 2027 capex.
  • $NFLX (Netflix). Per the Stocks list, down 1.7 percent after hours as the Journal reports it is exploring live TV channels and bundles with engagement softening, and eyeing sports and World Cup rights. A pivot story before it is a number.
  • $VLO $MPC $PBF (refiners). Per the Financial Markets list, all three sit at 52-week highs even as crude slid, the tell that the strength is in crack spreads and product demand rather than the barrel.
  • $KWEB (China internet). Per the Stocks list, posting its best month since September, with $NIO flagged as the next China name to move. A rotation trade, so it lives and dies on the group.

Psychology. Look at where the conviction is pooling this morning. Per the People list, the entire memory and semi complex is bid into the SK Hynix debut, Sandisk is up 800 percent on the year, Micron is being told it will out-earn its last 35 years combined, and Morgan Stanley is calling the power selloff a gift. Every one of those is a reason to be long, and they are all the same trade wearing different tickers. When a debut is oversubscribed seven times and the proxy and the leader and the analyst are all leaning the same direction, the crowd is not diversified, it is concentrated and telling itself a story about breadth.

Now hold the honest number next to the euphoria. Per the Financial Markets list, every Magnificent Seven name except Apple is already in a double-digit drawdown, Microsoft down 29 percent, while the index sits near a record because a handful of names carry it. That is the market quietly doing two things at once, celebrating at the index level and bleeding underneath. The discipline today is not to fight the Hynix trade, it is to size it like what it is. Ask whether you are buying memory because the setup is good or because everyone in your feed already owns it, and remember that the safest-feeling trade in the room is usually the most crowded one. If you need the debut to pop to feel right about the position, the position is already too big.

item.title: [News] The US strikes on Iran appear to have stopped overnight, Trump says Iran called to make a deal, and the panic that spiked oil yesterday is unwinding
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A senior US official says no strikes have happened in recent hours, gold and crude slid on fresh US-Iran optimism, and Japan's bond market reversed with the yen soaring. At home the Maine Senate primary imploded as Graham Platner moved to withdraw. Here is the overnight update.
item.content:encoded:
A senior US official says no strikes have happened in recent hours, gold and crude slid on fresh US-Iran optimism, and Japan's bond market reversed with the yen soaring. At home the Maine Senate primary imploded as Graham Platner moved to withdraw. Here is the overnight update.

Yesterday ended with Trump calling the ceasefire dead and the US resuming strikes. Overnight it turned the other way. The bombing appears to have paused, the language shifted to a deal, and the markets that priced a wider war started pricing a pause. At home, the messiest Senate primary in the country lost its front-runner. Here is the update.

1. The strikes paused and the tone flipped to a deal. The reversal was fast. Per the Polymarket list, a senior American official told Axios that no US military strikes have been conducted in recent hours, walking back yesterday afternoon's resumption. Per the Financial Markets list, the shift was strong enough to move prices overnight, with gold hesitating above $4,100 and heading for a weekly loss on less hawkish Fed minutes and fresh US-Iran optimism. The oil-shock story is being challenged directly, with analyst Anas Alhajji arguing the Strait of Hormuz supply loss was overstated once SPR releases and softer demand are netted out. The caution is that a pause is not a deal. Per the Kalshi list, traders still put only a 30 percent chance on a US-Iran nuclear agreement this year, near a record low.

2. The Maine Senate primary imploded overnight. The Democratic front-runner is out. Per the Kalshi list, Graham Platner is withdrawing, with his advisor saying he will file the paperwork and Axios reporting he told staff he will exit Monday, and the market puts an 87 percent chance he is gone before Tuesday. The race to replace him is now Troy Jackson at 49 percent, Shenna Bellows at 31 percent after officially announcing, and Nirav Shah at 14 percent, ahead of the July 31 primary. It is a clean reset of a seat that looked locked a week ago.

3. A Tea Party is brewing inside the Democratic primaries. The bigger political signal is nationwide. Per the Kalshi list, Harry Enten frames it as a new Tea Party for Democrats, with an 82 percent chance that six or more incumbent House Democrats lose their primaries. The named vulnerable seats are Missouri's Wesley Bell at a 38 percent chance of losing, Michigan's Shri Thanedar at 19 percent, and Connecticut's John Larson under 50 percent to hold. Separately, Rahm Emanuel is delivering remarks in Israel and carries an 80 percent chance he runs for president, and Pennsylvania's John Fetterman and Dave McCormick, a Democrat and a Republican, launched a joint fundraising committee called Common Ground PA.

4. Japan's bond market reversed, and it moved money. The macro tell came out of Tokyo. Per the Financial Markets list, the 10-year JGB yield fell 10 basis points to 2.775 percent and the 20-year fell 10 basis points to 3.765 percent, reversing yesterday's push to a 30-year high, as the yen soared on a pledge by Katayama to promote GPIF investment in Japanese assets. About $174 billion was added to the Japanese stock market at the open. After months of the JGB selloff being the global bond-stress story, an overnight reversal of that size is its own signal.

5. Around the world, quickly.

  • Ukraine. Per the Polymarket list, Zelensky says the number of countries wanting Ukraine in NATO has increased, a counter to yesterday's read that Putin was hardening against talks.
  • Europe's energy squeeze. Per the Polymarket list, NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte warned that Europe is closing more refineries than it is opening, tying military readiness to fuel supply.
  • Canada. Per the Kalshi list, the economy shrank in Mark Carney's first year as prime minister, called the worst economic start for any Canadian PM in over 60 years.
  • The US consumer. Per the Kalshi list, consumer credit fell $182 million in May, the first monthly decline since November 2024, a small but real crack in the borrowing story.

item.title: [AI & Tools] GPT-5.6's full price list is out and Sol lands one point under Fable on intelligence at a third of the cost, and OpenAI says Sol trained its own smaller sibling
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The launch specs are hard now, three tiers from Sol to Luna, Sol at half Fable's per-token price, and Sol topping the coding-agent benchmark. On All-In, Cerebras CEO Andrew Feldman said AGI is already here, and Anthropic answered the launch by resetting everyone's limits while Musk conceded it is the leader. Here is the signal.
item.content:encoded:
The launch specs are hard now, three tiers from Sol to Luna, Sol at half Fable's per-token price, and Sol topping the coding-agent benchmark. On All-In, Cerebras CEO Andrew Feldman said AGI is already here, and Anthropic answered the launch by resetting everyone's limits while Musk conceded it is the leader. Here is the signal.

Yesterday we had the reviews. This morning we have the price list, the benchmarks, and a claim that the model trained its own smaller version. The center of gravity in AI shifted from which model is smartest to what each point of intelligence costs. Here is what changed.

1. The GPT-5.6 price list is public, and Sol undercuts Fable by half. The hard numbers landed. Per the Hermes list, GPT-5.6 ships in three tiers, Sol at $5 and $30 per million input and output tokens, Terra at $2.5 and $15, and Luna at $1 and $6, against Fable 5 at $10 and $50. That puts Sol at roughly half Fable's per-token price. These are also the first OpenAI models with cache-write pricing at 1.25 times input, matching Anthropic, and a new max reasoning tier that also mirrors Anthropic's ladder. The intelligence war became a price war overnight.

2. On the benchmarks, Sol is a hair behind on smarts and ahead on coding, at a fraction of the cost. The sweep is consistent. Per the Hermes list, on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index Sol on max scores 59, one point under Fable 5's 60, at about a third of the cost per task. Sol on max then leads the Coding Agent Index outright at 80 and comes in about 40 percent cheaper per task than Fable, and it takes the top spot on DeepSWE at 73 to 74 percent. Fable still holds the lead on the Briefcase rubric at 56 percent to 42 and on analytical reasoning. The one-line read making the rounds is that this is no longer an intelligence war, it is a value war.

3. OpenAI says Sol trained Luna by itself. The line worth stopping on. Per the Hermes list, Andrew Curran quoted the OpenAI livestream saying that GPT-5.6 Sol autonomously post-trained GPT-5.6 Luna, a frontier model building its smaller sibling with minimal human involvement. Whatever you make of the framing, it is the most concrete recursive-self-improvement claim a lab has put on the record, and it sets up the next story directly.

4. On All-In, Cerebras says AGI is already here. The claim came with a book of business behind it. Per All-In, Cerebras CEO Andrew Feldman said that by any definition used 20 years ago we have artificial general intelligence now, it just is not fully deployed. He put numbers on the company, a $25 billion backlog, a wafer chip that costs half a billion dollars to make, and a claim of 15 times faster inference than the GPU architecture he calls 20 years old, with named buyers including OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, Google, Microsoft, and AWS. His open-source pitch was specific, that Cerebras already serves GLM, Kimi, and Qwen alongside closed models and builds sovereign models for GSK and G42, and that the US needs more domestic open source because the only real options today are a 12-billion-parameter model or Chinese ones. He also flagged the scale, that the data centers coming will use more power than the previous 50 years on Earth.

5. The safety debate split into an antitrust fight and a staged-rollout defense. Two separate arguments landed. Per AI Central, a piece called The Safety Cartel makes the case that OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind cooperate on safety in exactly the areas where they compete, raising a Sherman Act question, and it points at Anthropic's Project Glasswing and its exclusive Mythos preview access as the kind of arrangement antitrust law was built to scrutinize, all against crossholdings like Microsoft's roughly 27 percent of OpenAI. Per All-In, Feldman defended staged, red-teamed releases with a pharma analogy, and cited Palo Alto's Nikesh Arora running the new model against Palo Alto's own software, where it found bugs the company did not know about and forced a six-week patch cycle. One argument says the labs are too cozy, the other says the caution is earning its keep.

6. Anthropic answered the launch by opening the taps, and Musk conceded. The competitive response was immediate. Per the Hermes list, Anthropic reset every user's five-hour and weekly rate limits the same evening OpenAI shipped, with the team telling people to enjoy more Fable, on top of the free-through-July-12 access already in place. In the same window Elon Musk posted that he was clearly wrong about Anthropic, that it is obviously the current leader in AI, and that it will have Mythos 2 ready soon, the first mention of that model as imminent. OpenAI also launched ChatGPT Work, an agent powered by Codex and GPT-5.6 that acts across a user's apps and files for hours at a stretch.

7. The honest head-to-head keeps landing on the same verdict. The hands-on tests agree even when the benchmarks do not settle it. Per Nate Herk, a full day of Sol through Codex against Fable 5 through Claude Code had Fable winning two of three agentic builds on quality but costing three to twenty times more, $14 to $19 a run against Sol's $1 to $4.50, while on one-off API calls Sol won 24 to 3, mostly because Fable refused many requests outright on guardrails. His verdict is that Fable is the better manager and Sol is the better worker, the ideal being Fable orchestrating a fleet of Sol agents, and that the honest comparison is Sol against Opus 4.8, not against Fable. Per the Hermes list, Nous Research's Teknium made the same point from the other side, that Fable refused a common heavy task while Sol did it, cheaper and faster, and that is turning over-refusal into a real reason developers move workloads.

8. The moviemaking economics quietly broke. The most concrete real-world number came from film. Per All-In, Black Forest Labs founder Robin Rombach, the inventor of latent diffusion, described a single multimodal model spanning image, audio, video, and action prediction aimed at putting a brain on a robot, and said Martin Scorsese has sat with him multiple times using the models for storyboarding with a human in the loop. The number that lands is Gal Gadot shooting a Bitcoin movie on a soundstage with no green screens and all AI backgrounds for a $30 million budget against roughly $150 million to build it physically, a film she said would never have been green-lit otherwise. In the same breath, the Disney and OpenAI Sora licensing relationship is reportedly no longer happening.

item.title: [Investing] The ceasefire Trump floated at 1am was dead by afternoon, oil ripped back toward $80, and the tape still spent the day buying chips into SK Hynix's debut tomorrow
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: This morning the market bought the war on a deal tweet. By the close Trump said it is over and called Iran scum, crude reversed 6 to 7 percent higher, and rate-hike odds jumped to 56 percent, and none of it stopped the biggest one-day semiconductor rotation in months. Here is the close, then the six things that matter into Friday.
item.content:encoded:
This morning the market bought the war on a deal tweet. By the close Trump said it is over and called Iran scum, crude reversed 6 to 7 percent higher, and rate-hike odds jumped to 56 percent, and none of it stopped the biggest one-day semiconductor rotation in months. Here is the close, then the six things that matter into Friday.

The morning story flipped on itself. At 1am Trump said Iran wanted a deal and the selloff went green. By afternoon he said the ceasefire was over, the US launched another round of strikes, and oil that had been falling on the deal hope ripped 6 to 7 percent back toward $80. And yet the tape did something strange. It kept buying semiconductors all day, straight into SK Hynix listing tomorrow. Here is the close, then the six things that matter.

The close. Green where it counts, and the count is chips. Per the Financial Markets list, crude that sold off on the morning deal talk reversed hard once Trump killed the ceasefire, with WTI back near $74 and Brent near $78, and Mohamed El-Erian flagged the cross-asset tell that oil is now dragging global bond yields around more than the Fed is. Per the Kalshi list, the odds of a rate hike before year end climbed to 56 percent and the NY Fed survey has one-year inflation expectations up another 3.7 percent, the hottest in years. The dovish Fed minutes from this morning already look dated. Under all of it, the money rotated into the one trade that does not care about Tehran.

1. SK Hynix lists tomorrow, the deal got upsized, and Samsung's drop is the warning. The lead moved overnight. Per the People list, the SK Hynix ADR priced at $149, about 3 percent above the Korea close and more than 7 times oversubscribed, and the raise was lifted to roughly $28 to $29 billion, which if it holds is the largest ADR listing on record, topping Alibaba's $21.8 billion in 2014. The leveraged ladder is set, with 2x-long $SKHX and 1x-short $SKHZ following Monday. Per the Stocks list, Jim Cramer's warning is to price it in the hole or watch hedge funds fade it.

Here is the counter nobody wants next to the hype. Per the People list, Samsung guided to a record quarter, 89.4 trillion won in sales and 17.1 trillion in operating profit, and the stock fell anyway on softer AI-memory expectations. A record print that gets sold is exactly the read-through you carry into tomorrow's debut.

2. Micron committed $250 billion and the whole semi complex ran with it. The rotation was real and it had a leaderboard. Per the Stocks list, Micron raised its US investment plan to more than $250 billion through 2035 with the first pour at its Clay, New York fab, targeting roughly 40 percent of its DRAM made domestically, and $MU jumped about 6.5 percent back over $1,000 a share with shorts including Michael Burry reportedly scrambling to cover. Per the Market list and the People list, the one-day tape ran deep, $AEHR up 14 percent, $LITE up 13, $KLAC up 12, $ARM up 11, $AMAT up 10, $AMKR up 10, $MRVL and $MU up 7, $AMD up 6, $ASML and $INTC up 5. BofA's read is that AI spending is shifting from the hyperscalers to the chipmakers, and today the tape agreed.

3. Meta went from too much compute to not enough, and the neoclouds ripped. This is the reversal that reframes the week. Per the Stocks list, a week ago the fear was Meta had excess compute, the story that sank the neocloud names. Today the report is Meta plans to double compute to 14 gigawatts by 2027 and starts building its in-house Iris chip, designed with $AVGO and made at $TSM, in September. Per the Market list, Wolfe now models Meta guiding to roughly $220 billion of 2027 capex against a $160 billion street number, and the read-through lifted $NBIS, $IREN, and $WULF. The level to watch is $META holding $600 after it round-tripped from a $577 morning dip back to $606.

4. Nvidia and AMD both sit on lines into Friday. The two biggest names have clean levels. Per the Stocks list, $NVDA has to hold above 198.6 into Friday or the setup reverses down hard, with the upside retest at the 50-day near 209, tighter than this morning's 200-day-near-$190 frame. Per the People list, $AMD is trying to break 542 to 551 toward a new high, now carrying a bigger market cap than JPMorgan and Walmart and about 90 percent of the way to a trillion dollars. Hold the lines and the rotation has room. Lose them and the whole complex gives it back.

5. Oil coming back is the quiet risk to everything else. Respect the crude tape. Per the Financial Markets list, the moment the ceasefire died, oil reversed its morning drop and pushed 6 to 7 percent higher, Qatar halted its LNG ramp after the Hormuz strikes, and Goldman's note is to watch the oil path over the crude spot number because escalation or negotiation decides the next leg. Sustained crude above $75 puts the inflation channel back in play right as the Fed's hike odds are climbing. The bulls got their chip rotation. The bears got their oil.

6. Bitcoin's bottom signals keep flashing while Saylor becomes a seller. The structure firmed even as the biggest holder flipped. Per the Crypto list, Bitcoin sits near $62,750, up about 2.6 percent on the week, and Scott Melker says almost every indicator seen at prior bottoms is hitting now, the 200-week average, oversold RSI on the high timeframes. Lyn Alden sharpened her call, that capital rotates back to crypto only once the fast money finishes selling, and her trigger is the AI names topping out. Per 0xResearch, Bitcoin took the Iran shock like a tech stock, down 2.5 percent at worst then steady, while $350 million of altcoin longs got liquidated Wednesday.

The seller is the story though. Per the Crypto list, Michael Saylor sold Bitcoin for the first time, about $216 million, to fund his STRC dividend, and blames the coin's lag on miners rotating into AI compute and selling their coins to pay for it. Barclays still initiated $MSTR a buy at $130 and now calls it a fintech, not a crypto proxy. And in a real shift, Robinhood put a perpetuals exchange in front of its 28 million customers and picked Lighter, not Hyperliquid, which knocked $HYPE.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $VRT (Vertiv). Per Rick Orford, the cleanest AI-power and cooling name for the Nvidia GB200 racks, first-quarter sales up 30 percent to about $2.65 billion with margins up 430 basis points, and it raised full-year guidance to $13.5 to $14 billion. It trades near 48 times forward earnings, so the growth has to keep showing up.
  • $IREN. Per Rick Orford, the Bitcoin miner turning its renewable campuses into AI cloud, now sitting on a $9.7 billion Microsoft deal and a $3.4 billion Nvidia deal with Dell supplying Blackwell racks. The re-rate is whether the market prices it as AI infrastructure instead of a cyclical miner.
  • $APP (AppLovin). Per Milk Road, the ad marketplace trades near $527 against a probability-weighted fair value around $660, with revenue up 59 percent and an 85 percent EBITDA margin, and the stated plan is to start buying under $500 and go full size near $400 to $430. The whole bet is whether the AXON engine generalizes past gaming, and the CEO just sold $51 million of stock.
  • $NBIS (Nebius). Per the Stocks list, the neocloud with a $49 billion backlog, $27 billion from Meta and up to $19.4 billion from Microsoft, first-quarter revenue up 684 percent, carrying a $300 year-end target. The purest lever on the Meta compute reversal above.
  • $VECO (Veeco). Per one Reddit teardown, the picks-and-shovels way to own the memory buildout without the memory-price risk, the tool-of-record maker for the laser annealing that HBM stacks need, at a roughly $3.5 billion cap with an Axcelis merger waiting only on China approval.
  • $SOFI. Per Invest with Henry, the fintech near $18.32 with members up 35 percent and 2026 guidance around $4.65 billion, reports July 29, and the stated buy zone is under $20.

Psychology. Watch what the crowd did with the same headline twice in one day. This morning it bought the war because Trump hinted at a deal. This afternoon he said the deal was dead, called Iran scum, sent oil ripping, and the market bought again, this time into a chip rotation and an IPO priced tomorrow. That is a tape that has decided the direction in advance and is reading every headline, good or bad, as a reason to stay long the thing it already wanted to own. The danger in that is not the war. It is that conviction this smooth stops checking itself. When both the ceasefire and the collapse of the ceasefire are bullish, you are no longer reacting to news, you are just rationalizing a position.

Now do the discipline part on yourself. The honest signal today was not the green screen, it was Samsung. Per the People list, it printed a record quarter and the stock fell, because the good news was already owned. Hold that next to your own SK Hynix or Nvidia position going into Friday and ask the uncomfortable version of the question. If the thing you are long printed a perfect number and still dropped, what were you actually being paid for. Per the Crypto list, Michael Saylor sold his coins for the first time and told everyone why, and the crowd's response was to call it a buy signal. Sometimes the person selling is not sending a message. Sometimes he just needs the cash, and the story you build around his sale is the one you will be holding when the level breaks.

item.title: [News] The 1am ceasefire is dead, Trump said so on camera, and the US went back to bombing Iran while Khamenei was being buried
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: This morning's deal talk lasted hours. By afternoon Trump called it over and called Iran scum, oil ripped 7 percent toward $80, Jordan and Kuwait were intercepting Iranian missiles, and Maine's Senate seat flipped hard to the Democrats. Here is what moved since the open.
item.content:encoded:
This morning's deal talk lasted hours. By afternoon Trump called it over and called Iran scum, oil ripped 7 percent toward $80, Jordan and Kuwait were intercepting Iranian missiles, and Maine's Senate seat flipped hard to the Democrats. Here is what moved since the open.

The morning ended with Trump saying Iran wanted a deal. The afternoon ended with Trump saying the war was over because he was done talking to them. In between, the US struck Iran again, missiles flew at three more countries, and Iran buried its supreme leader. At home, the messiest Senate race in the country swung to the other party. Here is the update.

1. Trump killed the ceasefire on the record and the bombing resumed. The reversal was his own. Per Prof G Markets, asked directly if the ceasefire was over, Trump said, to me I think it's over, I don't want to deal with them anymore, they're scum, and if they had a nuclear weapon they'd use it. The US launched another round of strikes after. Brent oil soared more than 7 percent toward $80, the highest in over two weeks. Brian Katulis of the Middle East Institute put the frame on it, that the war Trump said would take four or five weeks is now in week 20, with no answer yet on who rules Iran, who controls the Strait of Hormuz, or where the nuclear program stands.

2. Khamenei is being buried, and the strikes are widening. The hard facts moved all day. Per the Polymarket list, Iran is reportedly burying Ayatollah Ali Khamenei today in Mashhad as the ceasefire unravels, and Prof G's guests noted top Iranian leaders were killed in the first day of the war. Iran reported 14 killed and 78 wounded in the overnight US strikes across five provinces. US Central Command says it hit about 90 targets on July 8, following 80 the day before, and named new sites including the Bushehr naval base, Chabahar port again, and a bridge on Iran's strategic corridor to China and Russia. The stated trigger was Iran attacking three commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.

3. The war is spilling into the neighbors. The interceptions tell you how wide this got. Per the Polymarket list, Jordan intercepted eight Iranian ballistic missiles fired at its territory, Kuwait shot down three ballistic missiles, a cruise missile, and ten drones at dawn with one injury and debris damage, and Bahrain's US forces headquarters appears to have taken two missile hits overnight with sirens nationwide. Per the Kalshi list, Iran is now formally accusing the US of war crimes. One detail on the American side, the Secret Service made Trump fly the older Air Force One out of Turkey when the fighting resumed and had the press lower the window blinds.

4. Maine flipped to the Democrats, and it flipped fast. The race that resolved this morning kept moving. Per the Kalshi list, Maine's Senate seat went from Republican 52 to Democrat 48 on Monday to Democrat 63 and Republican 37 today, a 15-point swing in the party's favor after Graham Platner's exit cleared the field. Nirav Shah has now officially entered the Democratic primary at about a 15 percent chance of the nomination. Looking to 2028, Citadel's Ken Griffin said he would back Marco Rubio over JD Vance, per Axios.

5. The market ignored all of it, and that silence is its own story. Stocks rose into the bombing. Per the Kalshi list, US equities added roughly $500 billion in half an hour, the Magnificent Seven sits at its cheapest versus the S&P 500 in over a decade even as the top ten names now make up 43 percent of the index, and traders are the most bullish on the dollar in more than ten years. The catch is under the surface. Rate-hike odds before year end climbed to 56 percent, the NY Fed survey shows one-year inflation expectations up another 3.7 percent, and Japan's 10-year government bond yield hit its highest level in 30 years. A market this calm about a widening war and a hotter inflation read is either right or asleep.

6. Around the world, quickly.

  • Ukraine. Per the Polymarket list, three Kremlin sources say Putin is rejecting peace talks and is likely to escalate as long as Ukraine keeps striking Russian territory.
  • Europe. Per the Kalshi list, the European Parliament passed the Chat Control proposal that allows private messages to be monitored, a major surveillance shift for the bloc.
  • The Gulf's long game. Per CaspianReport, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Oman are racing to build pipelines that bypass the Strait of Hormuz, but even at full capacity they cannot replace the volume that moves through it. The point is to survive a closure, not to defeat one.

item.title: [AI & Tools] The 30-day Sol reviews landed and the verdict splits, it is the new daily driver and a better writer than Opus or Fable, but it still scored 56 to Fable's 91 on the hard rebuild
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: This morning was the launch specs. Tonight is the lived-in verdict from people who used Sol for a month, plus Codex the app is dead, Claude Cowork moved to the cloud, and Blue Origin took outside money for the first time in 25 years at a $130 billion tag. Here is the signal.
item.content:encoded:
This morning was the launch specs. Tonight is the lived-in verdict from people who used Sol for a month, plus Codex the app is dead, Claude Cowork moved to the cloud, and Blue Origin took outside money for the first time in 25 years at a $130 billion tag. Here is the signal.

The morning gave us GPT-5.6 Sol's benchmarks. The evening gave us the reviews from people who actually ran it for a month, and they do not fully agree. The pattern under the disagreement is the important part, so here is what the day taught.

1. Sol is the new daily driver, and it is a genuinely better writer than the frontier. The reviews converge on a shape. Per Dan Shipper at Every, a month of internal use makes Sol plus its new harness the current gold standard for knowledge work, the Porsche of models, fast and precise and cheap enough to run all day, coding at an A tier with S still reserved for Fable. The surprise is the writing. Shipper says Sol is a better writer than both Opus 4.8 and Fable, because the two Claudes overexplain and Fable runs so long it invents its own private language. His read is that Sol is probably a smaller model than Fable that was post-trained extremely well, which means OpenAI and Anthropic are now deliberately building for different jobs.

  • The hard number keeps Fable on top where it counts. On Every's senior-engineer test, rewriting a vibecoded app from scratch, GPT-5.6 scored 56 out of 100 against Fable's 91, and Fable's code was simpler with fewer abstractions.

2. The routing fight got specific, and two power users disagree on where Sol goes. This is the real debate tonight. Per Alex Finn, 5.6 is the outright winner, Fable is still better at planning and holding a large codebase in its head but loses on execution, and Fable is dead in the water on price after he burned $500 of credits in a single afternoon. He expects Opus 5 to ship within a week as the affordable near-Fable answer. Where he breaks with the crowd is the sub-agent trick. Shipper endorses telling Fable to use Sol as a sub-agent, a match made in heaven because Fable burns credits the instant it spins up a hundred agents while Sol preserves the budget. Finn says do not do that, plan in Claude Code on high and hand the finished spec to ChatGPT to execute, because the harness that browses and signs up for sites is the actual edge now.

3. Codex the app is dead, and Claude Cowork moved to the cloud. Two quiet product shifts that change daily workflow. Per both Alex Finn and Every, the standalone Codex app is gone, folded into ChatGPT as a Work view that hides the code and a Codex view for developers, one engine underneath. On the Anthropic side, per one hands-on writeup, Claude Cowork shipped to web and mobile in beta for Max plans and scheduled tasks now run on Anthropic's servers with your laptop closed, though the tester found the catch, that a task tied to a local folder still only runs while your machine is awake, only the cloud connectors like Gmail and Drive run lid-closed. The tell in the launch data is that across 1.2 million Cowork sessions, 33 percent were business operations and over 90 percent were not coding at all.

4. Blue Origin took outside money for the first time in 25 years. The space race got a private round. Per Diet TBPN, Blue Origin is raising a roughly $10 billion round at about a $130 billion valuation, its first outside capital in 25 years, with Bezos putting in $2 billion directly and Coatue getting twice that at $4 billion. The company burns about $5 billion a year and has spent roughly $27 billion to date, and it is being valued on capability rather than revenue, second to orbit and reuse and ahead of China. The comps make the point, AST SpaceMobile near $27 billion and Rocket Lab near $50 billion, and the joke going around is that this makes SpaceX look underpriced.

5. Getty and Shutterstock called off their merger, and AI is why. A deal died on the same day two AI reviews shipped, and that is not a coincidence. Per Ben Thompson, Getty terminated its $3.7 billion merger with Shutterstock after UK regulators demanded it sell Shutterstock's editorial business. That headline price was set in early 2025, and both companies are now around $330 to $340 million in market cap, gutted by AI image generation from Meta Muse Image, Nano Banana Pro, and OpenAI Images 2. Thompson's read is that consolidation is the natural defense when an industry faces an external threat, and regulators reliably block the merger right when the industry needs it most.

6. The scarce skill is not the prompt, it is the loop. The through-line from this morning got a sharper name. Per swyx, the AI Engineer founder, you should stop writing prompts and start writing loops that create prompts, crediting Claude Code's Boris Cherny for the framing. A good agent loop is a specification, a verification step, and an explicit list of what not to do. The two things he says never to hand to the machine are your data structures and your user testing. The scale number underneath is loud, the token-billionaire program that expected 50 people to hit a billion tokens a week drew about 300 of 7,000 attendees, and Cursor developers are reportedly running 8 to 10 billion tokens a day.

7. The open-versus-closed fault line hardened. The week's model wave forced the question. Per the Hermes list, one builder says to just run Fable and GPT-5.6 Sol as your two main models because the gap over open or local models is now too big to justify, while Yann LeCun argues the biggest risk in AI is the concentration of power in a few proprietary providers and the only fix is open-source foundation models. swyx sided hard with the frontier, that the people preaching small models are coping because they do not have the GPUs, though he grants that agent labs will train their own vertical models rather than fine-tune. Both of these can be true, and the next Gemini release is the one that tests it.

item.title: [Investing] Iran hit US bases overnight, the US struck back, and then one 1am Trump tweet that Iran wants a deal flipped the whole selloff green
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The market is so tired of this war it bought the dip before dawn, the Fed minutes said nobody wants to hike until 2027, and the entire tape now hinges on memory as SK Hynix lists Friday into a 7x oversubscribed book. Here is the open.
item.content:encoded:
The market is so tired of this war it bought the dip before dawn, the Fed minutes said nobody wants to hike until 2027, and the entire tape now hinges on memory as SK Hynix lists Friday into a 7x oversubscribed book. Here is the open.

The overnight tape was a war movie with a happy ending nobody quite believes. Iran fired missiles at US bases, the US hit back with a second round, and then a single pre-dawn line from Trump that Iran wants to negotiate erased the entire risk-off move. Underneath, the Fed took hikes off the table and the whole market reorganized itself around one question: did memory just bottom. Here is the open, then the six things that matter.

The open. Whippy but net green. Per the Market list, overnight the US launched a second, larger round of strikes on Iranian targets around the Strait of Hormuz while Iran fired missiles at US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar, and then at roughly 1:15am Trump said Iran called and wants a deal, which turned Nasdaq futures from deep red to green. Brent is still up about 2% above $75, and one trader summed up the mood as the market having priced the war in both directions.

The Fed gave the bulls cover. Per ClearValue Tax, the June FOMC minutes showed a unanimous hold with not a single official wanting a hike, the internal survey does not pencil a first cut until the second quarter of 2027, and after the release the odds of a July hike fell to about 30%. Memory is the fulcrum today, with the Kospi down 25% from its high even as SK Hynix lists Friday into a book 7x oversubscribed.

1. The war became a buy-the-dip in a single overnight tweet. This is the tell about where sentiment actually is. Per the Market list, a genuine escalation, Iran striking US bases and the US answering, got fully faded before the US open the moment Trump floated a deal, with traders openly saying the market stops going lower on bad news and the bottom is near. Per threadguy, this is the first war people got bored of, the tape has simply stopped trading it. That is either resilience or complacency, and the two look identical until they do not.

2. The Fed minutes said nobody wants to hike, and the first cut is a year away. The macro anchor is a Fed with no plan. Per ClearValue Tax, every participant backed holding in June, a few saw a case for raising but still voted to hold, and the dots split on the year with many seeing rates roughly where they are and many others seeing them higher. The honest read is that inflation is running hotter on tariffs, energy, and, notably, demand from the AI buildout, which the Fed itself flagged as showing no signs of slowing. A reactive Fed that will not hike but cannot cut is a green light with a short leash.

3. Memory is the whole market, and Friday is the referendum. Everything routes through one sector. Per the Financial Markets list, SK Hynix lists Friday with roughly a 48% operating margin against Micron's 26%, and per the People-to-follow list the deal raises about $24.5B, the second-largest US debut ever, trading when-issued Friday under SKHYV with Baillie Gifford and Coatue indicated up to $7B. Penguin Solutions $PENG ripped more than 20% on a memory guidance raise, now up 293% since Trump's March buy.

The bull case on Micron got concrete. Per Rick Orford and a detailed Reddit DCF, $MU trades at 9.2 times earnings with a data-center run rate over $100B, is practically sold out through 2027, has locked 16 strategic customer agreements worth $20B of recurring revenue, and models out anywhere from $900 to $1,550 fair value. Per the Stocks list, the chart is holding the lower bound of its uptrend channel with 971 the level that confirms the reversal. The bear is not fundamentals, it is commoditization. Per threadguy, the risk is China's CXMT selling cheap DRAM into the US so buyers like Apple are no longer pigeonholed into Micron and SanDisk. Friday decides which story the tape believes.

4. Nvidia is sitting on the line, and it is the cheapest it has been in years. The single most important chart bounced. Per the Market list, $NVDA bounced twice off its 200-day near $190, trades at 16 times 2027 earnings against an average target near $317, and per the Kalshi list the whole Magnificent Seven is now the cheapest it has been versus the S&P in over a decade, with tech insiders buying at a record pace.

The bears are not gone. Per Stocks with Josh, the trend is still down with lower highs and lower lows, and he sees real support only at 172 and the range neckline at 165, guessing Michael Burry likely covered his short in that zone. Hold the 200-day and the cheapest Nvidia in years is a gift. Lose it and 165 is the next stop.

5. Bitcoin's bottom keeps building, and the next buyer is not Saylor. The structure improved again. Per the Crypto list, reactive speculators now hold just 26% of Bitcoin's realized cap, below the 40% line that has marked every major bottom, and Michael Saylor's record sale was absorbed with the coin barely dipping. Per Milk Road and Bitwise, the thesis is that Strategy is no longer the marginal buyer and the baton is passing to institutions, with Vanguard's $11T hiring of a head of digital assets read as the same one-way door BlackRock walked through.

The skeptics have a point too. Per the Crypto list, Peter Schiff is hammering that the biggest buyer became a seller and the coattail trade flipped, while Lyn Alden's base case is no new highs this year but no new lows either. Bitcoin is chopping around $60,000 to $62,000, a contested bottom, not a confirmed turn.

6. Under all the bottom talk, the memecoin casino says the froth has not left. Respect this counter-signal. Per threadguy, two memecoins on the new Robinhood Chain, Cash Cat and Anom, ran to nine-figure market caps in a week after Vlad Tenev followed the account, all while real on-chain volume sits at all-time lows. Per Capital Flows, the money that is not gambling is chasing Hyperliquid treasuries like $PURR instead. Nine-figure gambling on dead-quiet volume is the sound a market makes late, not early.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $SKHY (SK Hynix). The Nasdaq debut prices Friday, 7x oversubscribed and the second-largest US listing ever, trading when-issued under SKHYV with 2x-long $SKHX and 1x-short $SKHZ ETFs following Monday. The purest read on whether the memory trade bottomed, and the cleanest way to trade it besides Micron.
  • $PENG (Penguin Solutions). Per the Market list, a small-cap memory name up more than 20% on a roughly $100M guidance raise and 293% since March. High beta on the same theme, so size it like one.
  • $GE (GE Aerospace). Per one Reddit teardown, a 45% ROE quality business whose revenue grew 24.7% while EPS fell 1.8%, trading at nearly 51 times forward earnings against a six-model fair value of $119 that pegs it 67% overpriced. A great company at a price with no floor if the earnings dip is not transitory.
  • $ETN (Eaton). Per Rick Orford, the electrical-equipment maker framed as the AI-power analog to Nvidia, sitting inside the electrification and data-center demand mismatch. A power-infrastructure compounder, though valuation still has to work.
  • $PFE (Pfizer). Per Stocks with Josh, the stated buy is $23 for a 6 to 7% yield, with August 4 earnings and a history of running up about 20 days prior, which points at roughly July 16.

Psychology. Look at what the crowd decided overnight. Iran hit American bases, the US struck back, and the market bought the dip before sunrise on a single tweet that a deal might happen, because it is, in one trader's words, bored of the war. At the same time memory fell 25% in Korea and the response is to call a bottom, pile into a 7x-oversubscribed IPO, and buy Nvidia because it is the cheapest since 2019, all while insiders buy at a record pace. Everyone wants this to be the bottom, and that unanimity is the actual risk. When war is a buy, every dip is a floor, and the IPO buyers and the insiders and the bottom-callers are all leaning the same way, the consensus that it is over becomes the crowded trade.

Now hold the counter-signal next to it. Per threadguy, two memecoins ran to nine figures on record-low volume this week, and that is late-cycle gambling energy, not the fear you get at a real bottom. And per the Crypto list, Bitcoin is doing the honest version of a bottom, the fast money gone, Saylor finished buying, everyone waiting for $40,000, and it simply refuses to break. The discipline today is to notice that the loudest conviction is on the long side of the same three trades, and that the asset with no fans left is the one quietly holding. Feelings are cheapest exactly where nobody wants to feel them.

item.title: [News] Iran fired missiles at US bases across the Gulf, the US answered with a second round of strikes, and by 1am Trump said Iran wants a deal
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A night that started with rockets hitting American forces in Bahrain and Kuwait ended with the President floating negotiations, the sharpest whipsaw yet in a war that keeps being declared over. At home, the 2026 Senate map came into focus and Maine finally resolved. Here is what moved overnight.
item.content:encoded:
A night that started with rockets hitting American forces in Bahrain and Kuwait ended with the President floating negotiations, the sharpest whipsaw yet in a war that keeps being declared over. At home, the 2026 Senate map came into focus and Maine finally resolved. Here is what moved overnight.

The overnight news was war, and then, abruptly, maybe peace. Iran hit American bases across the Gulf, the US struck back harder, and within hours Trump was talking about a deal. Underneath the whiplash, the shape of the 2026 Senate election got clearer and a marquee race that looked like chaos suddenly had an answer. Here is the update.

1. Iran hit US bases, the US struck back, and then Trump floated a deal. The escalation was real before the reversal was. Per the Financial Markets list, Iran launched missiles at US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar, with two striking the US forces headquarters in Bahrain and Kuwait's army intercepting incoming fire. Per the Polymarket list, US Central Command answered with strikes on Chabahar, Bandar Abbas, and Qeshm Island, knocking out power in Chabahar, while Iran's parliament speaker warned the Strait of Hormuz only opens on Iranian terms. Then, per the Market list, Trump said around 1am that Iran had called and wanted a deal, and the whole risk-off move reversed. Whether that is a genuine off-ramp or a pause between rounds is the question the region wakes up to.

2. The 2026 Senate map came into focus, and it favors Republicans. With the midterms sixteen months out, the picture is hardening. Per Election Time's race-by-race map, the projection is Republicans holding the Senate 53 to 47, with the real battlegrounds in Texas, where Ken Paxton leads Democrat James Talarico by under two points, Ohio, where John Husted edges Sherrod Brown, and North Carolina, where Roy Cooper is up nearly eight over Michael Whatley in what the map calls the most likely flip. Georgia's Jon Ossoff, a 2028 hopeful, leads by six. The map that decides control runs through a handful of states, and right now it leans red.

3. Maine finally resolved after weeks of chaos. The messiest race got an answer overnight. Per the Kalshi list, Democrat Graham Platner officially suspended his campaign as his exit odds hit 99% on a video release, ending the sexual-misconduct saga that had whipsawed the market for a week. Former state senate president Troy Jackson immediately announced and is now the front-runner for the July 31 Democratic nomination at 47%, with incumbent Republican Susan Collins leading the overall race at 40% but Democrats favored to hold the seat at 64%, the highest since May. One trader reportedly cleared $173,769 on the Platner exit market alone.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • Iraq and Syria. Per the Polymarket list, Iraq agreed to stop dollar flows to Iran-backed militias, and Trump rescinded Syria's state-sponsor-of-terrorism designation, two moves that reshuffle the region's alignments around the Iran fight.
  • South Korea. Per the Financial Markets list, the Kospi is now down 25% from the record it set just three weeks ago, a full bear market driven by the unwind in its two chip giants, even as the Bank of Korea signals a rate hike may be needed.
  • China. Per the Kalshi list, factory-gate inflation came in at its highest in about four years, a tentative sign the long deflation may be turning.
  • The AI jobs question. Per the Kalshi list, a Forbes read of the latest data notes that firms are now spending more on AI than they save from the employees it replaces, a warning that the productivity payoff still trails the bill.

item.title: [AI & Tools] GPT-5.6 Sol shipped overnight and beats Fable on the coding benchmark at half the cost, so the smart-money workflow flipped from Fable-plans-Opus-builds to Fable-plans-Sol-builds
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Two frontier models landed in a day, Grok 4.5 and GPT-5.6, and the cheaper worker is now also the stronger one. Fable stays the expensive foreman you escalate to, not the default, as its own users call the token bills unsustainable. Here is the signal.
item.content:encoded:
Two frontier models landed in a day, Grok 4.5 and GPT-5.6, and the cheaper worker is now also the stronger one. Fable stays the expensive foreman you escalate to, not the default, as its own users call the token bills unsustainable. Here is the signal.

Two frontier models shipped in 24 hours and the important part is not which one is smartest, it is that the cheap tier just got stronger than the mid tier. That quietly rewires how everyone builds. Here is what changed.

1. GPT-5.6 Sol is public, and the benchmark plus the price are the story. OpenAI's answer landed. Per Chase AI, GPT-5.6 "Sol" ships in a standard and an Ultra tier and scores ahead of Claude Mythos and well ahead of Fable 5 on Terminal Bench 2.1, on OpenAI's own numbers, while landing 25% pass at $0.56 against GPT-5.5's 23% at $1.24, a higher score at roughly half the cost.

  • It did not arrive alone. Per the Hermes list, Grok 4.5 shipped the same day, trained with Cursor as their Composer 3, roughly 1.5 trillion parameters, and Cursor's own Michael Truell called it Opus-class, fast, cheap, and the daily driver for many on his team.
  • The skepticism is healthy. One builder noted Grok ranks itself above Fable on its own benchmark, and Sol's real open question, per Chase AI, is writing and taste, the thing you cannot patch with a harness.

2. The org-chart trade got a new bottom layer. Yesterday's play was Fable plans and cheap Opus workers build. Today the worker lane moved to GPT. Per Chase AI, the new pattern in his open-source "grillme codeex" skill is Fable interviews and plans, then hands the build to Codex running Sol 5.6 instead of Opus, then reviews it, because the cheap worker is now also the stronger one.

  • The workflow is four stages: an interview where Fable asks 8 to 10 questions, adversarial planning where Fable and Codex argue to consensus over up to five rounds, the build handed to the GPT worker, and up to two Fable fix passes.
  • The efficiency is the point. His full "Trip Atlas" app build cost only about 130,000 Fable tokens, because Fable never touched the actual code.

3. The routing discipline got formalized, and Fable is now the escalation path, not the button. The cost backlash is real and specific. Per the Hermes list, Alex Finn burned roughly $150 of Fable credits in 30 minutes and called it economically unsustainable, with enterprises already cutting off Opus for being too expensive.

  • Per Kris Torrington, the discipline is a routing table by risk: extraction and formatting go to Sonnet or Haiku, normal coding and analysis to Opus at medium-high, hard agentic work to Opus at extra-high, and only frontier uncertainty where being wrong costs money or days escalates to Fable, which lists at twice Opus and is sliding toward usage credits.
  • His rule of thumb is the whole trade in one line: if Opus gives you 80% of the result at half the token price, use Opus, and only reach for Fable when the missing 20% actually matters.

4. One prompt can now build a whole business, if the prompt is the real work. The ceiling on a single `/goal` keeps rising. Per Nate Herk, one prompt spun up a full company, Counterbrief, a Shopify chargeback tool, in three to four hours, with Fable orchestrating and every worker running on Opus or Sonnet.

  • The heavy lifting was the prompt architecture, not the model: ten parallel research agents swept Reddit and Hacker News into 35 problems, tournaments of competing ideas scored by five judge personas, and a red team of six skeptic agents that threw 38 attacks and got zero kills.
  • The guardrails are the reusable part: no new spending, publish nothing, invent nothing and verify every fact, and never stop to ask. It cost about 500,000 Fable tokens, roughly 15% of a weekly budget, and would have been 100 times more expensive with Fable workers.

5. The human edge that survives all of this is the spec. The through-line worth keeping. Per Nate B Jones, when everyone can code, the scarce person is the one who can take a vague business need and turn it into a precise spec, and then judge whether the machine's output actually solves the customer's problem.

That is the same skill under every story today. The models get cheaper and the routing gets automated, but the person who can direct the machines with enough precision and then tell whether the result is any good is the one role none of it replaces.

item.title: [Investing] The chips broke, a war reignited, and the crowd answered by shoving a record five billion dollars into semiconductor ETFs to buy the dip
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Nvidia lost its 50-day and Bitcoin fell to 62,000 as oil pushed past 80 on the Iran strikes, yet fund-manager cash sits at a 20-year low and every analyst in the room is doubling down. Under the crypto panic, whales just made the biggest accumulation in history. Here is the recap.
item.content:encoded:
Nvidia lost its 50-day and Bitcoin fell to 62,000 as oil pushed past 80 on the Iran strikes, yet fund-manager cash sits at a 20-year low and every analyst in the room is doubling down. Under the crypto panic, whales just made the biggest accumulation in history. Here is the recap.

Today the market had every reason to be afraid and refused. A war reignited, oil spiked, semis broke their trend, and the response was not to sell but to pile in harder. That reflex, buying every dip with no cash left over, is the whole story tonight. Under it, in the asset everyone has given up on, the smart money did the opposite. Here is the close, then the six things that matter.

The close. Risk-off, and it was the war, not the Fed. Per the Market list, the Nasdaq closed down about 1% with the $VIX up hard and millions of $QQQ puts on the tape, driven by the overnight strike on 80-plus Iranian targets and Trump declaring the ceasefire over. Per the Financial Markets list, Brent pushed past $80 and WTI is up roughly $7 in two days as Russia banned diesel exports, while the 10-year yield tore 20 basis points higher this month to 4.6%, a quiet bond rout under the oil headline.

The Fed minutes came out this afternoon and the tape barely noticed. The rate path is the real puzzle: per the People-to-follow list, a soft June jobs print of 57,000 had cut July hike odds toward 18%, but the oil shock is now pulling them back the other way.

1. The dip-buyers showed up in size, and that is the debate. The most important number today was not a price, it was a flow. Per the Crypto list, the $SOXX semiconductor ETF took in $5.4B in a single day, four times its old record, with $7.1B flooding into long semi ETFs to buy a group that had fallen 16% in two weeks.

The chorus is loud to match. Per Milk Road, all five of its analysts are buying the dip, with Melvin doubling down on $NBIS and $CRWV and adding $MRVL, and per the Stocks list, fund-manager cash sits at its lowest in more than 20 years. Everyone is already long the bounce, and that is exactly the condition Joseph Hogue warns about: per Hogue, the Dalbar data shows the average investor earns 7% against the S&P's 10.6% precisely by chasing and panicking, a gap worth $1.3M over 30 years.

2. Nvidia is cheap now, and it is still the fulcrum of everything. The one name that decides the tape got cheaper and weaker at once. Per the Stocks list, $NVDA lost its 50-day average alongside the whole chip complex, but it trades below 20 times earnings, the cheapest since 2019 and a steep discount to Broadcom's 53 times, and it caught a bid after China cleared Alibaba, ByteDance, and DeepSeek to buy roughly 200,000 H200 chips.

The bull catalyst is concrete. Per Rick Orford, Anthropic is now running Claude on Nvidia's GB300 Blackwell through Microsoft Azure, a full hardware-cloud-model agent stack, and 49 analysts rate it a strong buy. The tension is the whole market in one chart: the best fundamental story in tech, sitting on a broken 50-day, into a war.

3. Memory bifurcated, and Micron is the only one still standing. The best trade of the year is splitting in two. Per the Stocks list, $MU was the only name in the complex to hold its 50-day, needing 971 to confirm a reversal, while $SNDK, $WDC, $STX, and $INTC all lost theirs.

The Samsung tell is the one to sit with. Per Prof G Markets, Samsung posted a blowout with revenue more than doubling and profit up 1,800%, and the stock fell 7% and dragged the market, because investors decided it had already gone too far. Michael Burry is short Micron betting on a 30% drop, and memory was the single best group in the market in the first half, up 276% against 49% for the rest of semis. When the best fundamentals produce a selloff, the price already knew.

4. Bitcoin capitulated into the biggest whale buy in its history. The crypto panic hid the real signal. Per Lark Davis, Bitcoin fell to $61,600 on the Iran escalation and is trying to hold wedge support, and per the Crypto list sentiment is flatly dire, with per 0xResearch 25 of 28 crypto sectors closing red on the day.

But look at who was on the other side. Per Lark Davis, whales accumulated 270,000 Bitcoin in late June, the largest single accumulation spike ever, larger than the COVID or FTX bottoms, buying near $59,000 while forced sellers redeemed a record $4.5B out of the ETFs, the worst month ever per Lark Davis. The counterweight to respect, per Benjamin Cowen, is that this is a countertrend window of strength in a midterm year, a rally into late July before a probable lower low into a Q4 bottom. Forced sellers meeting patient whales is how bottoms are built, but the calendar says not yet.

5. The rotation already has names, and they are AI dressed as something else. The money leaving crowded chips is finding cheaper AI exposure. Per Milk Road, Galaxy Digital $GLXY near $25 owns a data-center business, Helios, worth roughly $38 a share on its own through a 526-megawatt CoreWeave deal worth over $1B a year, which means the market is pricing its five other businesses below zero.

Per a second Milk Road note, Nebius $NBIS carries a $300 year-end target on a $49B backlog from Meta and Microsoft, growing revenue 684% while owning its full stack instead of renting and marking up. Per Milk Road, the same rotation has lifted $LLY, $HOOD, and software since June 25 as memory and the neoclouds bled.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $NBIS (Nebius). Per Milk Road, a $300 year-end target on Q1 revenue up 684% to $399M, a $49B backlog, and a guided $7 to $9B annualized run rate, the neocloud that owns its own stack. The risk is the same capex-versus-cash question hanging over the whole group.
  • $PLTR (Palantir). Per Rick Orford, near $130 with an average target of $194 and a BofA high of $255 on 72% revenue growth, but priced at 142 times earnings, so the August 3 earnings are the whole trade. Above 40% growth and a raise is the bull, a drift to 30% is the bear.
  • $GLXY (Galaxy Digital). Per Milk Road, a sum-of-parts where the Helios data-center business alone covers the price and a loan refinancing from 9% toward 7% would roughly double free cash flow to equity. The catalyst is the first reported Helios rent next earnings.
  • $COIN (Coinbase). Per Milk Road, the agentic-payments play that owns stablecoins, the Base chain, and an agent marketplace, framed as the pick-and-shovel if machines start transacting.
  • $TER (Teradyne). Per Milk Road, a chip-equipment name down about 30% that one analyst is buying into the semis washout, a cyclical way to play a memory bottom without the single-name risk.

Psychology. Watch what the crowd actually did today. The chips broke their trend, a war reignited, oil ran past $80, and the answer was to shove a record $5.4B into semiconductor ETFs, per the Crypto list, with fund-manager cash at a 20-year low, per the Stocks list. That is not fear, it is total conviction that every dip is a gift, and it is the most expensive belief there is right after the best trade in the market cracks. When everyone is already fully long the bounce and holding no cash, there is nobody left to buy it higher.

Now look at Bitcoin for the clean mirror. Per Lark Davis, forced sellers just dumped a record amount out of the ETFs straight into the single largest whale accumulation in history, 270,000 coins bought near $59,000 while the crowd called it dead. One tape is euphoric dip-buying into a broken chart with no dry powder left, the other is patient whales quietly eating a panic. Per Joseph Hogue, the question that separates the two is the only one that matters tonight: if you did not already own it, would you buy it here, and do you have anything left to buy it with. The edge is never where the crowd has already spent all its cash.

item.title: [News] Trump declared the Iran ceasefire dead, threatened to seize an oil island, and the war he ended in June is back on
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The US followed its overnight strike by hitting Bandar Abbas, Iran fired at Gulf bases, and the President floated taking Kharg Island as oil pushed past 80. The whole episode is accelerating a global arms race, and at home Gavin Newsom's 2028 case is falling apart. Here is what moved after the AM edition.
item.content:encoded:
The US followed its overnight strike by hitting Bandar Abbas, Iran fired at Gulf bases, and the President floated taking Kharg Island as oil pushed past 80. The whole episode is accelerating a global arms race, and at home Gavin Newsom's 2028 case is falling apart. Here is what moved after the AM edition.

The morning's strike was not the end of it. By evening the US had widened the campaign, Iran had answered, and the President was talking about seizing Iranian territory. The war Washington declared over in June is fully back, and its shockwaves are running through oil, defense budgets, and the 2028 race. Here is the update.

1. The US widened the Iran war and threatened to take an oil island. The escalation kept building all day. Per the Polymarket list, after the overnight strike on more than 80 targets, the US hit Bandar Abbas, and Trump declared the ceasefire and memorandum over, calling Iran's leaders scum, saying "we attacked Kharg Island last night" and that the US may take it over, and promising to hit them hard again tonight. Iran fired back, with Kuwait intercepting two ballistic missiles and thirteen drones and sirens sounding in Bahrain. Kharg Island handles a large share of Iran's oil exports, so a threat to seize it is a threat to the oil market itself, and that is why Brent pushed past $80.

2. The war is pouring fuel on a global arms race. The spending follows the fear. Per Prof G Markets, the NATO summit in Turkey produced more than $50B in new defense-industry deals plus $40B for counter-drone systems, and Trump's 2027 budget carries $1.5 trillion for defense, the largest ever. Defense-technology venture funding has already passed $19B in six months, beating all of last year. The detail that lands hardest is where the software companies now sit: Palantir's Maven targeting system, paired with Claude, was used in the Iran war, which puts the same names that sell you consumer AI squarely inside the kill chain.

3. Gavin Newsom's 2028 case is falling apart. The Democratic front-runner is fading fast.
