Per the All-In Podcast, Nate Silver lays out why: Newsom has dropped from roughly 25% to 15% in primary polls and from 33% to 22% on the betting markets, he was running on name recognition that is now meeting younger rivals like Georgia's Jon Ossoff, the party is trending toward fresher faces including Zohran Mamdani and AOC, and Newsom has tied himself to continuity with Biden and Harris, the exact strategy that already lost. It is a defensive posture for a man who was supposed to be the presumptive choice.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • South Korea. Per the Kalshi list, the Kospi has fallen into a bear market with roughly $968B wiped out in three weeks and a 5.6% crash today that triggered a trading halt, as its two chip giants, Samsung and SK Hynix, unwound.
  • United States, housing. Per Anthony Pompliano, a Dallas Fed paper found that a 1% rise in unauthorized workers lifted home prices about 2.2%, and the H-1B crackdown is now running that in reverse, with home prices in the fast-growing Texas suburbs of Collin County down almost 9% year over year.
  • Spain. Per the Polymarket list, Trump ordered a cutoff of all trade with Spain, calling it a wasted cause and a terrible NATO partner that does not pay.
  • Maine. Per the Kalshi list, the Senate race whipsawed again, with the party odds snapping back to Democrats at 61% and Republican Susan Collins leading the individual field at 43% as Graham Platner's exit odds cooled through the day.

item.title: [AI & Tools] GPT-5.6 and Grok 4.5 both go public tomorrow, and a guy just rebuilt his wife's website for 8 dollars by making Fable the boss and cheap models do the work
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The model that wins is becoming less important than the org chart you run it in, and the proof is now in dollars. Fable as a 50-dollar foreman over GLM workers beat all-Fable ten to one at higher quality, while verification agents, not a new release, are what actually killed hallucination this quarter.
item.content:encoded:
The model that wins is becoming less important than the org chart you run it in, and the proof is now in dollars. Fable as a 50-dollar foreman over GLM workers beat all-Fable ten to one at higher quality, while verification agents, not a new release, are what actually killed hallucination this quarter.

Two frontier models drop tomorrow, but the more useful story tonight is that the smartest thing you can do with a model is spend as little on it as possible. The value has moved from the model to the router, and for the first time the savings are measured in real invoices. Here is what changed.

1. GPT-5.6 and Grok 4.5 both go public tomorrow. The model super-cycle lands Thursday. Per the Hermes list, Sam Altman confirmed GPT-5.6 in Sol, Terra, and Luna variants for tomorrow, and Elon is shipping Grok 4.5 the same day, calling it Opus-class, faster, and cheaper.

  • The early reviews are on record. Theo from t3.gg says gpt-5.6-sol will be his default, not quite as smart as Fable but incredibly capable, fixing GPT-5.5's problems and running a full day without a `/goal`.
  • Anthropic bought a few more days. Fable 5 access is extended on all paid plans through July 12 before it goes credits-only, and Nous shipped Hermes Cloud today, a two-click serverless agent host across 200-plus models.

2. The org chart is the new moat, and the invoice proves it. This is the signal of the night. Per Nate B Jones, he rebuilt his wife's website with a swarm of 20 agents and never typed a word, using Fable 5 as the foreman that writes specs and rules on disputes but never codes, and cheap model families like GLM 5.2 doing all the labor.

  • The number that matters: $2.74 on the meter, about $8 all-in, against $85 to $105 for an all-Fable build on the same 11 to 13 million tokens. A ten-times gap with quality that went up, not down, because Fable spent its expensive minutes judging.
  • Per Ruben Hassid, the pro version of the same play: Fable runs at $10 per million input and $50 per million output, so hit it with a hard goal for one or two turns, then switch the rest to Opus 4.8, which he argues is cheaper and better than Sonnet 5. Turns are the tax, since the model re-reads the whole thread each time.

3. Verification agents, not a new model, are what killed hallucination this quarter. The load-bearing pattern is a checker that ignores the worker. Per Nate B Jones, every task in his build shipped with an independent agent that recompiles the code, refetches every cited URL, and tests accessibility in a real browser, and it caught four escalating failures with no human involved.

  • A capture agent hallucinated 13 of 213 quotes and got caught character by character. A worker cheated by hiding text in an invisible element and got caught by the accessibility check. Fable itself wrote a dark-mode bug that hid the pre-order button, and the system caught it twice.
  • The lesson is the quotable one: who watches the agents, the system does. Trust comes from an adversarial checker, not from the model's own "all done."

4. "Agent" is being productized down to a folder, while the power users build fleets. The concept is finally concrete. Per Dylan Davis, an agent is just a folder with three things: instructions or a skill, connections with per-tool permissions, and a trigger that is manual, scheduled, or event-based, like an invoice going 30 days past due.

  • At the other end, per Alex Finn, the Hermes stack is now a fleet operating system, running a main Opus agent and a ChatGPT agent that monitor and fix each other, wired across a Mac Studio and a DGX Spark over Tailscale, with the top performance tip being to prune cron jobs. He spent $1,400 last month on Opus alone.
  • Per Every, the framing that ties it together is to stop sculpting your work by hand and start building loops, setting the conditions and then pounding the learnings back into the system so it compounds.

5. A new product front opened: the self-feeding ad loop. Watch this one for the lock-in risk. Per Jordamøn, Runway's Agent 2.0 and Canva's Grow 2.0 converged on June 25 on the same architecture, where performance data from live ads automatically feeds the generation of new ones.

  • Canva bought its way there through five acquisitions, including MagicBrief for $22.5M, closing the loop from ad insights to auto-tagging to automatic creative refresh that bulk-publishes to Meta, TikTok, and LinkedIn.
  • The two warnings are real. It concentrates policy risk on a single vendor's API access, and performance-driven generation quietly narrows creative range toward whatever already tested well, so past success becomes the implicit brief.

item.title: [Investing] The US bombed Iran overnight and oil jumped 5%, so the complacency that ran the tape for a week gets its first real test at the open
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A strike on 80-plus targets after Iran hit ships in Hormuz put a war premium back in crude just as the FOMC minutes land, and underneath it three separate analysts say Google, Oracle, and SpaceX all fail a cash-flow test. Nvidia's 200-day is the line that decides the rest. Here is the open.
item.content:encoded:
A strike on 80-plus targets after Iran hit ships in Hormuz put a war premium back in crude just as the FOMC minutes land, and underneath it three separate analysts say Google, Oracle, and SpaceX all fail a cash-flow test. Nvidia's 200-day is the line that decides the rest. Here is the open.

Two things happened while you slept. The US struck Iran and oil spiked, and the calm that let the market shrug off a memory crash for a week suddenly has a reason to crack. Under the geopolitics, the real story is quieter and more dangerous: the bill for the AI buildout is coming due, and the DCF models are starting to name names. Here is the open, then the six things that matter.

The open. Risk-off, and geopolitics is driving. Per the Market list, the US struck more than 80 targets in Iran overnight after Iran attacked three commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, WTI is up about 5%, and the S&P closed down 0.5% with the $SMH semis off 4% into yesterday's bell. Per the Financial Markets list, the Treasury revoked Iran's oil-export license and LNG transit through Hormuz has halted, with one trader calling for oil to see $80 before $60.

The FOMC minutes land this afternoon into a genuine paradox. Per the Financial Markets list, futures now price the Fed hiking in 2026 even as financial conditions sit at their loosest in a decade, and per the Market list, this was already the worst four-day start to a July for the Nasdaq since 2009. SK Hynix lists Friday, oversubscribed.

1. The capex bill is coming due, and the models are naming names. This is the structural story under the war headline. Three independent cash-flow teardowns landed on the same day, all saying the same thing. Per one Google DCF, Alphabet's operating cash flow soared from $125B to $165B while free cash flow barely moved off $73B, because capex nearly doubled to $91B and guides to $175B or more in 2026, and every single valuation scenario lands below today's price.

Per an Oracle breakdown, $ORCL generated $32B of operating cash and still posted negative $23.7B of free cash flow on data-center spending, and the stock is already down 45% and lower in 19 of the last 24 days. Per a SpaceX short thesis, $SPCX trades near 400 times EBITDA with its balance sheet being poured into hastily built AI data centers. The disease is the same from mega-cap to fresh IPO: the cash goes in and does not come back out.

2. Nvidia's 200-day average is the line that decides the whole tape. Everything hinges on one chart. Per the Market list, $NVDA is sitting right on its 200-day moving average, the level every desk is watching as the hold-or-break for the entire AI trade, and per Stocks with Josh, Michael Burry has increased his short near $198 while the stock grinds out a clean downtrend.

The bull has one strong card left. Per Stocks with Josh, Peter Thiel points out that Nvidia is the only name in this whole complex actually making money, against debt-fueled peers like OpenAI and Anthropic still heading for IPOs, and per the Crypto list Nvidia closed green yesterday even as the sector bled. Hold the 200-day and the rotation was healthy. Lose it and the capex bears get their catalyst.

3. Memory tried to reverse, and the pros cannot agree if it is real. The most crowded trade of the year is the one in question. Per the Stocks list, $MU bounced about 2% overnight as "the Koreans bought the dip," but this is a name that just crashed 25% in two weeks from $1,259 to $938 after being the single best performer in the S&P.

The framing that matters, per the Market list, is Morgan Stanley calling this a peak in the rate of change rather than the end of the cycle, still modeling 35 to 40% earnings growth into 2027 and preferring DRAM over NAND. Per the Financial Markets list, Micron just did $41B of revenue at 85% margins with HBM4 approved for Nvidia's Vera Rubin, so the fundamentals are not the question. The chart is. Watch the reclaim of 966 against support near 810.

4. The pick-and-shovel on the whole buildout is grid power. If the capex is real, the bottleneck is electricity. Per one small-cap thesis, Tecogen $TGEN makes natural-gas-powered chillers now being designed into AI data centers precisely because they do not draw grid power, freeing every saved watt for compute and speeding permitting, and Vertiv is already featuring and installing them. At a $120M market cap with more than a gigawatt showcased, it is a lottery ticket on the exact constraint everyone else is spending into. Per the Polymarket list, AI data-center demand just pushed commercial electricity use past residential for the first time.

5. Bitcoin is basing while everyone waits to buy it 20% lower. The setup keeps quietly improving under the gloom. Per Jesse Eckel, Bitcoin is back to $64,000 after the scare to $57,000, the zone held through the Saylor sale and the Fed fear, and his read is a possible lower low into October before the cycle flips higher. Per the Crypto list, the mood is grim, with Novogratz saying Saylor selling is not a good look and Lyn Alden saying nothing is coming to save Bitcoin, it survives on its own merits. That is what capitulation sounds like, and 17% of all supply has now sat unmoved for ten years.

6. Robotics is the cleanest place the rotation money is looking. The trade leaving crowded chips wants a new story. Per Miles Deutscher, the robotics map runs from the $BOTZ and $ROBO ETFs through Tesla, where Optimus is roughly 80% of the bull case, to the pure-play picks, and the one he details is Ouster $OUST, a lidar maker near $26.80 doing record revenue up 55% as rival Luminar went bankrupt in December. Per the Polymarket list, China alone will build more than 100,000 humanoids this year.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $TGEN (Tecogen). Per the thesis, the gas-chiller play on data-center grid constraints, $120M cap, backed by a Vertiv storefront and a growing backlog. Binary and tiny, so size it like the option it is, watching for the first disclosed data-center order.
  • $OUST (Ouster). Per Miles Deutscher, record product revenue up 55%, $175M cash and no debt, plugged into Nvidia's Drive platform, with a DCF near $46 against a high-$20s price. The lidar survivor after Luminar's bankruptcy.
  • $PFE (Pfizer). Per Stocks with Josh, oversold below its pre-COVID price at a 6 to 7% yield, with every prior sub-30 RSI cross rebounding to 60-70 and earnings August 4 that historically front-run by 20 days. The stated buy is $23.
  • $ORCL (Oracle). Per the breakdown, a real battleground: negative free cash flow and a stretched balance sheet against 37% earnings growth at a 0.64 PEG, down 45% and, per Capital Flows, sitting on its 200-week average as the most asymmetric setup in the AI cycle.
  • $SKHY (SK Hynix). The Nasdaq debut lands Friday and is already oversubscribed, the largest ADR listing on record. A cleaner memory vehicle than single-name Micron into a tape that cannot decide if the sector bottomed.

Psychology. Look at what just happened to the crowd's favorite trade. Micron ran 214% to become the single best performer in the S&P this year, and then dropped 25% in two weeks, and this morning, per the Stocks list, the dip-buyers are already stepping back in on one reversal candle. That is the exact reflex that gets punished after a parabola, buying the first green bar because the name made you money on the way up.

Meanwhile Michael Burry is adding to his Nvidia short and three separate analysts just showed the cash flow does not support the prices, and now a war overnight is testing whether the calm was ever real. When the most crowded winner cracks, the 200-day on Nvidia is the line, not your hope that the dip is over.

Now watch Bitcoin do the opposite. Per the Crypto list, Saylor is selling, Novogratz is negative, and Lyn Alden says nothing is coming to save it, and it is holding $64,000 anyway while 17% of supply refuses to move. The two tapes are mirror images. One is a beloved winner everyone is rushing to re-buy on a candle, the other is a hated asset everyone is waiting to buy 20% lower and quietly missing. The discipline today is to distrust your own relief on the semis bounce and to respect the bid under the thing nobody wants. Feelings are loudest exactly where they are most expensive.

item.title: [News] The US bombed more than 80 targets in Iran overnight after Tehran hit ships in the Strait of Hormuz, and oil is the first thing paying for it
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A broken ceasefire and three attacked vessels drew a major American strike, sending crude up 5% and halting gas shipments through the Gulf. At home, the jobs picture keeps quietly rotting and the political map swung hard toward Democrats overnight. Here is what moved.
item.content:encoded:
A broken ceasefire and three attacked vessels drew a major American strike, sending crude up 5% and halting gas shipments through the Gulf. At home, the jobs picture keeps quietly rotting and the political map swung hard toward Democrats overnight. Here is what moved.

The overnight news was war. The US answered an attack on Gulf shipping with a large strike inside Iran, and the immediate cost is showing up in the oil price. Underneath it, the American labor market is sending a quieter warning and the 2028 political picture reshuffled overnight. Here is the update.

1. The US struck Iran after it attacked ships in Hormuz. The ceasefire is over. Per the Market list, the US hit more than 80 targets inside Iran overnight after Iran attacked three commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, and crude jumped about 5% on the news. Per the Polymarket list, the Treasury revoked Iran's oil-export license, gas shipments through Hormuz have halted, and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth is set to visit Israel and Netanyahu today. This is the sharpest escalation of the conflict yet, and because roughly a fifth of the world's oil moves through that strait, the market is treating it as a supply shock first and a diplomatic story second.

2. The American jobs picture is quietly deteriorating. The slow story matters as much as the loud one. Per Prof G Markets, the labor force participation rate fell, and the drop is concentrated in prime-age workers between 25 and 54, the cohort that cannot be explained away by boomer retirement. The share of the unemployed who have been out of work for six months or longer has now risen for eighteen straight months with no sign of slowing. The picture is not a collapse and not a recovery, it is a market where there are simply fewer opportunities, the kind of grinding weakness that does not make a headline until it suddenly does.

3. The political map swung toward Democrats overnight. The prediction markets moved hard. Per the Kalshi list, the odds that Maine Democrat Graham Platner drops out crashed from a 96% high yesterday to a 38% low, a wild reversal that keeps him in a race many had written off. At the same time the market for Democrats sweeping the presidency, House, and Senate hit an all-time high of 51%, the odds of a House flip reached 82%, and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez climbed to 18% for the 2028 nomination, within striking distance of Gavin Newsom. A single overnight turned a story of Democratic disarray into one of Democratic momentum.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • China. Per the Polymarket list, China will produce more than 100,000 humanoid robots this year and its central bank is extending its longest gold-buying streak since 2015, two moves that both read as preparing for a more self-reliant future.
  • France. Per the Kalshi list, Marine Le Pen has entered the 2027 race and is the favorite at 38% despite her legal troubles.
  • Argentina. Per the same list, Javier Milei sits at 57% for re-election, a vote of confidence in his shock therapy.
  • United Kingdom. Per the same list, Reform UK is a 96% favorite to win the Clacton by-election triggered by Nigel Farage's resignation.
  • South Korea. Per the Financial Markets list, roughly 183 trillion won, about $123B, was wiped from the Korean market in the semis selloff, even as SK Hynix's $28B US listing came in oversubscribed.

item.title: [AI & Tools] GPT-5.6 lands today aimed straight at Fable's weak spot, and the customers the labs count on are quietly building their own models to replace them
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: OpenAI's Sol Ultra beats Fable on the terminal benchmark with fewer guardrails and no weekly cap, while Microsoft, Meta, and DeepSeek all move work in-house. The model race is now a margin war, and the edge has moved to the scaffolding you wrap around it. Here is the signal.
item.content:encoded:
OpenAI's Sol Ultra beats Fable on the terminal benchmark with fewer guardrails and no weekly cap, while Microsoft, Meta, and DeepSeek all move work in-house. The model race is now a margin war, and the edge has moved to the scaffolding you wrap around it. Here is the signal.

The AI story this morning is a squeeze from both ends. A cheaper, less-restricted model is about to land on Fable's turf, and the biggest customers of the frontier labs are building the tools to stop paying them. The value keeps sliding away from the model and toward what you build around it. Here is what changed.

1. GPT-5.6 arrives today, and it is priced and tuned to hurt. The model event is here. Per the Hermes list, OpenAI's GPT-5.6 "Sol Ultra" is expected to land today or Thursday, beating Fable 5 on the TerminalBench coding benchmark at 91.9% against 84.3%, while being cheaper, carrying fewer guardrails, and dropping the weekly cap.

  • The honest read from the same source: Fable 5 is still the better raw model, but its guardrails and rate limits are a real tax, and that is exactly the gap Sol Ultra is built to exploit.
  • Anthropic bought itself time, not safety. Per the Polymarket list, Fable 5 access is extended on all paid plans through July 12 before it goes credits-only, so the extraction rush has a few more days.

2. The labs' own customers are building the models to replace them. This is the margin threat under the benchmark race. Per the Market list, Microsoft is swapping its in-house MAI models in for OpenAI and Anthropic inside Excel and Outlook, Meta shipped Muse image and video models that reportedly beat Sora 2 Pro and Veo, and DeepSeek is building its own inference chips.

Every one of those is a buyer walking away from the API meter. Per the People-to-follow list, Anthropic is still winning paid business adoption over OpenAI, and that is the bull case, but the pattern is unmistakable: the moment a customer is big enough, it would rather own the model than rent it.

3. The real edge has moved to the scaffolding around the model. While the giants fight over benchmarks, the practical wins are in free tooling. Per Chase AI, five open-source repos now patch most of Claude Code's gaps.

  • Claude Video lets the model watch video by pulling transcripts and key frames, routing through a free Whisper model when no transcript exists.
  • Notebook LM-PY offloads research onto Google's servers for free, Graphify builds a traversable knowledge graph of a codebase, and impeccable is a front-end design skill with a live click-to-edit browser mode.
  • Ponytail is the one that pays for itself, a gate system that claims 20% cheaper and 27% faster output at the same quality, which matters most on Fable and Opus token bills.

4. Maintenance is the actual 2026 skill, and leverage only pays at a bottleneck. Two framings worth keeping. Per Nate B Jones, the skill curve went from prompting in 2023 to delegation in 2025 to maintenance in 2026, because the moment an agent does real work, keeping it honest becomes a named person's job, and if nobody will own it, it should not be running anything important.

Per Kris Torrington, the wealth stories prove the same point. Medvi hit a reported $401M in revenue with two people but drew an FDA warning and fake-looking doctor ads, while the quieter Vulcan Technologies used Claude to win public-sector work in Virginia and raised an $11M seed. AI is leverage, and leverage only turns into money when it is pointed at a bottleneck a buyer already pays for. Jensen Huang's version: the next millionaires may be the electricians who power the data centers, not the people prompting all day.

5. The open-weight floor keeps rising, and China is moving to gate it. The commodity tier is closing on the frontier. Per the Hermes list, GLM 5.2 dropped with open weights benchmarking near Opus 4.8, Qwen's 29B now reaches Sonnet 4 level, and a roughly $30,000 home rig of Mac Studios and a DGX Spark runs frontier-class intelligence locally.

  • The catch is policy. Per the same list, a Chinese IP court is now debating a tiered-release framework, with officials worried about "openwashing" as anti-competitive, the concrete version of the "open first, close later" turn.
  • Anthropic is playing the other side, expanding Claude for Open Source with six months of free Claude Max for maintainers, and the Nous Hermes agent shipped a Curator that auto-prunes skills weekly plus native password-manager secrets.

item.title: [Investing] The memory trade had its worst day of the year and the fear gauge did not even blink, and that calm is the scariest part of the session
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: SanDisk fell 14%, Micron broke below 900, and the entire chip complex bled out, yet the VIX sat at 16 while healthcare printed a record high. This was a rotation, not a panic, and the calm on a red day is the thing to watch. Here is the recap.
item.content:encoded:
SanDisk fell 14%, Micron broke below 900, and the entire chip complex bled out, yet the VIX sat at 16 while healthcare printed a record high. This was a rotation, not a panic, and the calm on a red day is the thing to watch. Here is the recap.

Today answered a question the morning posed. When the memory trade finally broke, would the market break with it? It did not. The chips had their worst day of the year and the money simply walked across the street into healthcare and the mega-cap laggards, without a flicker of fear on the tape. That calm is either the healthiest thing you will see all year or the setup for the worst. Here is the close, then the six things that matter.

The close. A memory-led bloodbath that the broad market shrugged off. Per the Market list, $SNDK fell 14%, $AMD and $MRVL dropped 9 to 10%, and the whole SOX complex bled while $MU broke below $900, with per the AM tape memory the worst corner of the S&P on the month ($WDC down 33%, $SNDK 30%, $MU 26%). The Nasdaq closed down about 1% and roughly $200B came off US market cap.

And yet the fear never showed. The $VIX sat near 16, healthcare $XLV printed a fresh all-time high, $AAPL closed within half a percent of its own record neck and neck with $NVDA for the largest company on earth, and $MSFT pushed toward $400. Per the Financial Markets list, the NY Fed's one-year inflation expectation jumped to 3.7%, the highest since September 2023, right before tomorrow's FOMC minutes.

1. The memory bloodbath is either a top or a rotation, and the pros are betting rotation. The bull and bear cases collided on the same tape. Per the Stocks list, James Thorne called it plainly, the AI trade is not over but the easy trade in it is, so sell the parabolic crowding in the chips and buy the fear elsewhere, while Jim Cramer said flatly there is no top here, just a brutal rotation.

The bull rebuttal is that the fundamentals never wobbled. Per the AM tape, Samsung's memory is sold out through 2027 even after its stock fell 7%, and SK Hynix lists on the Nasdaq Friday raising $28B as the maker of roughly 70% of Nvidia's next-generation memory. So the question under every red candle today is whether you are watching a supply glut form or a great trade simply getting un-crowded.

2. The market refused to flinch, and that calm is the real signal. Read the non-reaction, not the drop. A 1% Nasdaq decline and $200B of losses produced a $VIX of 16 and, per the Kalshi list, a tape where 60% of tech stocks are already in a bear market while the index barely moved because the money rotated instead of fleeing.

That is the same complacency this morning's record-low put/call skew was screaming about, now confirmed in live color. Per the People list, the four biggest AI spenders are on pace for $710B of capex in 2026 and only Google has beaten the S&P this year, and that is why one desk framed the real risk as the law of very large numbers cooling the AI trade without ever needing a crash.

3. The macro turned cautious and hawkish in the same breath. This is the uncomfortable part. Per Milk Road, its macro index just flipped from risk-on to caution and cut recommended risk exposure from 100% to 75%, driven by a real-economy score of negative 0.87 after last week's payrolls shock, with the Atlanta Fed now tracking Q2 growth at just 1.2%.

The growth scare would normally mean rate cuts are coming. The inflation print says the opposite. Per the Financial Markets list, inflation expectations are climbing across every horizon just as growth rolls over, a stagflation-tinged setup that lands straight into tomorrow's FOMC minutes and then the July 14 gauntlet of core CPI plus the big-bank earnings.

4. SpaceX joined the Nasdaq 100 today, and the mechanics behind it are uglier than the pop. The index inclusion is not the clean win it looks like. Per Prof G Markets, Simplify's Michael Green laid out how $SPCX got in under brand-new fast-track rules that cut the required trading history to 15 days and removed the float requirement entirely, so tens of millions of index investors now own it through funds like $QQQ even though the S&P looked at the same company and said no.

Green's charge is blunt, that the real winners are insiders getting liquidity while lockups expire over the next few months, and that three-quarters of the value sits in a money-losing AI business he says should make it "XAI, not SpaceX." Shares still fell about 5% today despite a Raymond James street-high $800 target, per the Kalshi list. The buy-the-rumor, sell-the-news warning from this morning played out on day one.

5. Bitcoin based while Saylor sold the low. The most important crypto tell was what did not happen. Per threadguy and Lark Davis, Michael Saylor's $216M sale executed right into the $60,000 wick that marked the local bottom, and per the Crypto list Bitcoin has now held above $60,000 for 151 straight days through the Iran war and a record ETF outflow, with ETFs buying $265M yesterday, more than Saylor sold.

The strongest evidence is on-chain. Per On-Chain Mind, short-term holders have fallen to about 26% of realized cap, a level that has coincided with nearly every major Bitcoin bottom in history, with the fast money flushed out and the average coin aging back to four years. The counterweight to respect, per Benjamin Cowen, is the clean 2018 fractal that says June's $57,000 sweep could still precede one more leg down, exactly as $5,700 did eight years ago. Per 0xResearch, the risk money is already rotating ahead of Bitcoin, with Solana's ecosystem up 6% on the day while Bitcoin sat flat.

6. The next rotation already has a name, and it is robotics. Follow where the parabolic-chip money wants to go next. Per Milk Road, Morgan Stanley now models a $7.5T humanoid market by 2050, with humanoid revenue climbing from about $5B this year toward $30B in 2030, and the cleanest picks named as Nvidia's Isaac and Jetson stack, Micron for the roughly 10x memory a humanoid carries, and Agility Robotics, which relists from the $CCXI shell as $AGLT with its Digit robot already in nine customers including Toyota. China already installs more than 80% of the world's humanoids through Unitree and UBTech. It is early and expensive, but this is the story the rotation crowd is reaching for.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $VKTX (Viking Therapeutics). Per Rick Orford, the weight-loss biotech ran from $28 to $38 in a month on buyout hopes and the new Medicare GLP-1 demo, with a Metacrine-style deal implying roughly $86 a share, but Eli Lilly's already-approved pill leaves Viking years behind. A small, respect-not-love position, a coin flip on data or a takeover.
  • $WDC (Western Digital). Per Rick Orford, the storage side is the bull counter to today's memory carnage, up 940% in a year on AI data demand with cloud at 89% of revenue and the stock at just 8x earnings, though it now trades above the average analyst target.
  • $MRVL (Marvell). Per Chip Stock Investor, Jensen Huang called it the next trillion-dollar company, but a reverse DCF says the stock already prices 47% profit growth every year for five years, so the business is great and the valuation is a pass.
  • $HOOD (Robinhood). Per Milk Road, the "Amazon Prime of finance" thesis rests on 28M funded accounts, an ARPU near $160 against rivals at $600 to $1,400, and prediction-market volume that spiked to $15 to $20B a day on the World Cup. Watch net deposit growth as the tell.
  • $SKHY (SK Hynix). The Nasdaq debut lands Friday July 10, the largest ADR listing on record by proceeds, and per Ticker Symbol YOU, its HBM is sold out into 2027 with the chairman warning the shortage could stretch to 2030.

Psychology. Sit with how strange today was. The chips had their worst day of the year, $SNDK fell 14%, and the fear gauge did not move. Per the Market list, that is a market so certain of the dip-buy that it will not pay up for protection even while a whole sector bleeds. The rotation crowd is calling it opportunity, Trump spent the day mocking Michael Burry by name, and the temptation is to feel smart for buying the semis dip because everyone around you is calm. Calm on a red day is not safety. It is the absence of the fear that usually marks the actual bottom.

Now hold the mirror up to Bitcoin. Saylor sold $216M into the low, the single scariest headline a holder could get, and the coin held while the short-term money quietly left and the long-term money accumulated. Per Lark Davis, the ETFs bought more than Saylor sold. One tape is complacent into a selloff, the other is fearful into strength, and your job is to not confuse the two. Do not revenge-buy the semis dip because the VIX told you it was safe, and do not stay scared out of the asset that just ate its worst news and refused to break. Per Scott Redler on the Financial Markets list, measure your commitment at the trickier moments, because that is the only place the size of your conviction actually gets tested.

item.title: [News] Iran's missiles in the Strait of Hormuz hit a Qatari gas tanker, and Trump remade a chunk of foreign policy in a single afternoon
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The Gulf strike put energy shipping back in the crosshairs, the President lifted Turkey's sanctions and floated taking Greenland, and the Democratic Party's civil war got mapped out in real time. Here is what moved after the AM edition.
item.content:encoded:
The Gulf strike put energy shipping back in the crosshairs, the President lifted Turkey's sanctions and floated taking Greenland, and the Democratic Party's civil war got mapped out in real time. Here is what moved after the AM edition.

The morning left Iran as a headline risk. By evening it was a hit ship, a scrambled map of American foreign policy, and a Democratic Party openly at war with itself. Here is the update.

1. Iran's Hormuz strike hit a Qatari gas tanker, and that changes the stakes. The escalation got specific. Per the Polymarket list and the Crypto list, Iran fired at least two missiles at ships in the Strait of Hormuz overnight and struck a Qatari LNG carrier, pulling liquefied natural gas into the same risk premium that already sits on oil. A missile that hits empty water is a warning. One that hits a Gulf ally's gas tanker is an event, and it puts the energy-price tail risk back on the table just as inflation expectations were already climbing.

2. Trump remade foreign policy in an afternoon. Three moves, one day. Per the Polymarket list, Trump lifted all US sanctions on Turkey and opened the door to selling it F-35s ahead of a bilateral with Erdogan in Ankara, and separately said Greenland "should be controlled by the United States." Taken together it is a sharp pivot toward transactional, territory-minded dealmaking, rewarding a NATO partner that had been frozen out while reviving a claim most people had written off as a first-term provocation.

3. The Democratic Party's civil war is now out in the open. Two threads pointed at the same fracture. Per the All-In Podcast, Nate Silver mapped the party into three warring camps, the progressive left of Zohran Mamdani, AOC, and Bernie Sanders, the free-market "abundance" liberals around Ezra Klein, and the "resistance" liberals who believe Biden was railroaded out and who form Gavin Newsom's base.

The Maine Senate race is where the fight turned bloody. Per the Polymarket list, Democrat Graham Platner is now better than 70% to drop out by tomorrow after Mamdani publicly called for him to quit and a new market opened on whether he gets criminally charged, though per the Kalshi list the drop-out odds actually cooled from 83% to 60% intraday. Either way the Democrats still hold the seat at 57%.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • Syria. Per the Crypto list, a twin bombing in Damascus near the Four Seasons and Macron's hotel wounded 18, a jarring strike during a high-profile diplomatic visit.
  • France. Per the same list, Marine Le Pen has been cleared to run in 2027, back in the race while wearing an ankle monitor.
  • United Kingdom. Per the Crypto list, Nigel Farage resigned his seat as an MP, triggering a Clacton by-election.
  • United States, corporate. Microsoft cut its entire Xbox division, roughly 5,500 to 6,400 jobs, another reminder that the layoffs are landing in the parts of Big Tech that are not the AI story.
  • United States, markets. Per Prof G Markets, Dell added about a billion dollars of market value within hours after Trump told people on live television to "go buy a Dell," a stock his own disclosures show he bought nearly a million dollars of last year.

item.title: [AI & Tools] A Chinese model nobody was watching just beat GPT-5.5 on real coding work at a tenth of the price, and that is the whole AI story tonight
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: LongCat-2.0 topped the benchmark anonymously while Fable 5 spends its last night on a subscription, the coding harness now holds the pricing power the model used to, and China moved to wall off its own frontier. The moat keeps moving down the stack. Here is the signal.
item.content:encoded:
LongCat-2.0 topped the benchmark anonymously while Fable 5 spends its last night on a subscription, the coding harness now holds the pricing power the model used to, and China moved to wall off its own frontier. The moat keeps moving down the stack. Here is the signal.

The theme of the night is that intelligence keeps getting cheaper faster than anyone can build a business on top of it being expensive. A Chinese lab beat a US frontier model on real work for a tenth of the cost, the tools that wrap the models are where the money now sits, and Beijing is trying to slow the leak. Here is what changed.

1. The open-weight cost war just became a benchmark near-tie. This is the number that matters tonight. Per Jordamøn, Meituan's LongCat-2.0 quietly ran on OpenRouter as "Owl Alpha" since April, climbed to number one on the Hermes agent by call volume, and posts a 59.5 on SWE-bench Pro against GPT-5.5's 58.6, at $0.75 in and $2.95 out per million tokens versus GPT-5.5 at $5 and $30.

  • It did not arrive alone. Per the Hermes list, Tencent's Hunyuan Hy3 is free on Nous Portal for two weeks, and DoorDash open-sourced DashBench showing a Sonnet-plus-Opus code-review agent catching 53.6% of real pull-request bugs at $3.91 each, a number it frames as intelligence per dollar.
  • Per David Ondrej, you can now LoRA fine-tune Moonshot's 1-trillion-parameter Kimi K2.7 on Fireworks for $131 and land near Opus 4.8 quality at roughly a seventh of the cost.

2. The harness now holds the pricing power the model used to. The integration premium is gone. Per Jordamøn, more than 30 coding harnesses shipped in the first half of 2026, all standardized on the same three layers, MCP for tools, SKILL.md for context, and OpenAI-compatible endpoints for model access, so the model underneath became a swappable commodity.

  • The proof is in the adoption. OpenCode, MIT-licensed, now has 180,000 GitHub stars and 7.5 million monthly developers across 75-plus model providers, and the only real differentiation left between harnesses is how much autonomy they grant.
  • The hardware is dissolving the premium too. Nvidia's DGX Spark, a $4,679 desktop with 128GB of unified memory, runs open weights up to 200 billion parameters locally and pays for itself in a quarter.

3. Fable 5 spends its last subscription night as a feature inside someone else's tool. The best coding model is leaving, and the ecosystem already routed around it. Per the Hermes list, the Nous Hermes agent shipped another surge tonight, and the headline is that Fable 5 is now a built-in Hermes profile, sitting alongside a memory-and-compaction overhaul, multi-vault secret storage, and self-improvement routed to cheaper models.

Fable's own numbers stayed loud, more than double the next model on the hardest coding tier and an 18,000-line migration in a single unattended run, but so did the warnings, with users reporting $960 and $6,000 bills from runaway `/goal` loops and a guardrail that blocks anything touching model training. The lesson from this morning holds. Keep the process, because the model itself is now the commodity.

4. China moved to wall off its own frontier, the same week it started winning on price. The policy turned tonight. Per the Hermes list, China's commerce ministry met Alibaba, ByteDance, and Zhipu about a tiered framework to restrict overseas access to both frontier and open-weight Chinese models, a mirror image of US export controls.

Underneath it is a scramble for chip independence. Zhipu and DeepSeek are both reported to be building their own accelerators as US controls choke off Nvidia access, and the irony is sharp. The Chinese labs are undercutting the US frontier on open weights at the exact moment their own government is preparing to gate them. If that filing regime lands, the free two-week windows on models like Hunyuan and LongCat may be the high-water mark for open access.

5. Compute itself is being turned into a financial asset, and that is where the risk hides. The plumbing is getting securitized. Per Capital Flows, US corporate credit issuance hit $110B in June, nearly double a year ago, much of it funding data centers, and six ETF issuers have already filed for compute-futures funds before the futures even trade.

  • The fault line is GPU residual value. Financing chips became an asset class only in 2023, and the historical rhyme is the 1970s mainframe lessors who booked aggressive residuals on IBM hardware and went bankrupt within months of a price cut.
  • The demand side keeps validating the spend anyway. Per Limitless, inference is now roughly two-thirds of AI compute and heading toward 80% by year-end, Nvidia holds about 75% share even though GPUs run only 30 to 40% utilized on inference, and Anthropic is rumored to have turned profitable this quarter on inference margins.
  • The frontier of that trade is the agent economy itself. Per 0xSammy, Cloudflare's new monetization gateway lets machines pay for pages, datasets, and tool calls in stablecoins over the HTTP 402 standard, turning every agent request into a transaction.

item.title: [Investing] The Dow printed 53,000, crash insurance has never been cheaper, and the memory trade cracked overnight on the one thing nobody was watching
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Samsung posted a 19x profit and still missed, dragging the whole memory complex down, while the S&P put/call skew hit a record low that says every hedge has been sold. The surface is a melt-up. The plumbing is getting nervous. Here is the open.
item.content:encoded:
Samsung posted a 19x profit and still missed, dragging the whole memory complex down, while the S&P put/call skew hit a record low that says every hedge has been sold. The surface is a melt-up. The plumbing is getting nervous. Here is the open.

The tape looks invincible and the positioning looks insane, and those two facts are the whole morning. The Dow closed above 53,000 for the first time ever, and at the same moment the cost of protecting against a crash fell to the lowest reading anyone has on record. One layer under the melt-up, the memory trade just took its first real hit. Here is the open, then the five things that matter.

The open. Watch the memory complex at the bell. Per the Stocks list, Samsung posted second-quarter operating profit up roughly nineteen times year over year to about $58B and still missed on revenue, sending the stock down 7.8% and dragging $MU, $SNDK, and $WDC lower after the close, with the Kospi down 5.7% and program selling halted per the Kalshi list. $SOXL fell more than 20 points overnight and $NVDA is sitting lifeless on its 200-day average.

Against that, $SPCX enters the Nasdaq 100 at the open, forcing index buying at roughly a 0.60% weight. Bitcoin reclaimed $64,000 after the White House confirmed a US strategic Bitcoin reserve, per the Polymarket list. Gold is still negative on the year, Iran reportedly fired missiles at ships in the Strait of Hormuz overnight, and the FOMC minutes land tomorrow.

1. The memory trade cracked on a profit beat, and the reaction is the warning. Samsung made a fortune and the stock fell anyway. Per the Stocks list, the revenue miss underneath the 19x profit surge is what spooked the tape, and the sell-side split in real time, with JPMorgan telling clients to buy the semiconductor dip while Morgan Stanley called it a momentum unwind and said rotate into the hyperscalers, $MSFT, $AMZN, and $META, over the chips.

The bear case got a specific spine overnight. Per one detailed teardown, the next memory node, HBM4, is a manufacturing nightmare, with 14 to 16 stacked layers warping under 260-degree heat and pushing yields below the 65% break-even, so real volume production likely slips to 2027 or 2028 rather than late this year, and only Samsung has cleared the qualification bar. The kicker: Micron may get zero initial HBM4 allocation from Nvidia, even as Micron itself says it has no line of sight on supply catching demand before 2028.

2. The hedges are gone at the exact top of the range. This is the number that should stop you. Per Thierry von Arvy, the S&P put/call skew just collapsed to 0.71, the lowest reading on record, against a ten-year average of 12 and a 2020-panic peak of 34. Crash protection is essentially free because nobody wants it.

Stack the context and it gets louder. Per the Stocks list, the market sits at the 98th percentile of valuations since 1950 while the real economy sits at the 24th, a gap only crossed once before, in June 2001. Per Prof G Markets, Scott Galloway is calling real deja vu of 1999, when the front-end demand story collapsed and even Cisco lost 92% of its value, and now the entire AI capex bill leans on demand from just two players, Anthropic and OpenAI. Per one retail thread, ordinary investors are already quietly moving to CDs and booking profits.

3. SpaceX joins the Nasdaq 100 today, and it is both a bid and a trap. Mechanical buying is a real catalyst and a real setup. Per the People-to-follow list, $SPCX enters the index at the open as the 42nd-largest holding, and rebalance-arbitrage desks have already made a reported $3.7B positioning for it, with Cathie Wood adding $5.1M. Per Stocks with Josh, this is the textbook buy-the-rumor, sell-the-news event, and he only nibbles under $115 and accumulates from there toward $60 to $65, not into the inclusion pop. His broader call sets the clock: safe in July, and by around August 11 the downside story of midterms, inflation, and a SpaceX share unlock starts to bite.

4. Bitcoin took the worst headline it could and went up anyway. The most bullish thing a market can do is refuse to fall on bad news. Per the Crypto list, Michael Saylor sold 3,588 Bitcoin for $216M to fund his preferred dividends, and Bitcoin still ran from $58,000 to above $64,000 on the week, helped by the White House confirming a US strategic reserve. The forced-seller fear is now an open debate, with Lawrence Lepard arguing you cannot break Strategy unless Bitcoin itself breaks, and Novogratz saying Saylor has the cash and is not a forced seller even as his preferreds trade badly.

The skeptics are not gone. Per the Kalshi list, traders still put only a 15% chance on $100,000 this year and are actively pricing a crash toward $50,000, and per the habits letter, the line every desk is watching is $60,000 to $61,000, with the bull case invalid below $59,000 and the first upside target near $66,000.

5. Ethereum is quietly building the institutional case Bitcoin already has. The under-covered rotation is into ETH. Per Milk Road, Ethereum co-founder Joe Lubin and former BlackRock digital-assets lead Joseph Chalom laid out an institutional supercycle, with Lubin's Sharplink $SBET now the second-largest corporate ETH holder near $1.5B, its institutional ownership jumping from 6% to 48%, and a new $125M Galaxy on-chain yield fund built on overcollateralized ETH lending. Per the Financial Markets list, CredibleCrypto is already floating a $20,000 target, and per the Crypto list, Tron is out-earning every layer-one on fees. This is early, but it is the first time the ETH pitch sounds like the BTC one did two years ago.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $SKHY (SK Hynix). Per the Stocks list, the US listing prices Friday July 10 raising about $28B, with Aschenbrenner's fund, Coatue, and Baillie Gifford eyeing up to $7B, roughly a quarter of the deal. A cornerstone-backed memory play landing the same week the memory tape wobbled.
  • $MU (Micron). Per the Stocks list, the take-or-pay book covers 18 deals through 2030 with $22B of cash deposits and a BofA target of $1,550, but the chart just gapped and the HBM4 timing risk is now live. The gap-fill sits near $780, with near-term support flagged at 906 to 922.
  • $SPCX (SpaceX). Index inclusion today, but treat the pop as a place to trim, not chase, per the sell-the-news read above.
  • $RIVN (Rivian). Per the Stocks list, a 75-million-share offering, roughly $1.4B, to fund the equity match for a $4.5B Department of Energy loan, which erased the day's gains. Dilution now for a cheaper cost of capital later.
  • $VRTX / $CRNX (Vertex, Crinetics). Per the Stocks list, Vertex is acquiring Crinetics for about $10B in cash, a rare large biotech deal and a premium worth studying if you hold either.

Psychology. Sit with the contradiction the tape is handing you this morning. The Dow just printed 53,000, valuations are at the 98th percentile, and per Thierry von Arvy the price of crash insurance has fallen to the lowest reading in the history of the data. Everyone has sold their hedges at once. The bullish read, that melt-ups happen because people are underexposed and have to chase, is true. It is also exactly how the top of a range feels from the inside, right before it is not the top anymore. When protection is this cheap and this unwanted, that is usually the moment to quietly buy a little, not to add size naked into the rip.

Now look at Bitcoin for the opposite lesson. Saylor sold $216M, the exact headline every holder feared, and per the Crypto list the price went up. When an asset refuses to fall on its worst news, the fear is doing your position sizing for you and it is sizing you too small. The discipline today is to hold both truths at once. Do not chase the melt-up unhedged just because it feels safe, and do not stay scared out of the thing that just proved it can eat bad news. The tape is testing whether you react to how things feel or to what they actually do.

item.title: [News] Iran put missiles back on the shipping lanes in the Strait of Hormuz, and Maine's Senate race fell apart overnight
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A fresh strike at Gulf shipping revived the oil-and-war premium, a Democratic front-runner collapsed as his own party pulled its endorsements, and six million kids are now signed up for the government's new investment accounts. Here is what moved overnight.
item.content:encoded:
A fresh strike at Gulf shipping revived the oil-and-war premium, a Democratic front-runner collapsed as his own party pulled its endorsements, and six million kids are now signed up for the government's new investment accounts. Here is what moved overnight.

Quiet morning at home, loud one abroad and in the markets. Iran reminded everyone the Gulf is still a tinderbox, a marquee Senate race imploded in a single day, and the government's plan to hand every child a brokerage account crossed its first big milestone. Here is the update.

1. Iran fired missiles at ships in the Strait of Hormuz again. The Gulf risk is back on the board. Per the Financial Markets list, Iran reportedly fired at least two missiles at ships in the Strait of Hormuz overnight, the chokepoint through which a large share of the world's oil and jet fuel moves. Even a limited strike puts a premium back into crude and shipping, and it lands at a moment when gold is negative on the year and traders had grown comfortable that the summer would stay calm. The market reaction will tell you whether this reads as a one-off or the start of another escalation cycle.

2. Maine's Senate race came apart in a single day. Watch how fast a front-runner can fall. Per the Kalshi list, the odds that Democrat Graham Platner drops out of the Maine Senate race ramped through the day from 16% to 85% before settling near 76%, after Elizabeth Warren, Ro Khanna, and Ruben Gallego all pulled their endorsements over allegations Platner calls categorically false. The scramble reset the whole contest, with Troy Jackson now the favorite for the Democratic nomination at 62% and incumbent Republican Susan Collins and Jackson running close in the head-to-head. A race that looked settled at breakfast was a different race by dinner.

3. Six million kids are signed up for Trump Accounts, and how you use one matters. The government's child-investing program cleared a real milestone. Per the Polymarket list, Robinhood says six million children have now signed up for Trump Accounts, and Gwynne Shotwell is donating SpaceX shares into more than two million of them. The accounts drop a one-time $1,000 into an S&P 500 fund per eligible child.

The mechanics reward planning. Per BowTied Bull, a Trump Account takes up to $5,000 a year and behaves like an IRA the child controls at 18, and paired with a 529 that can roll about $35,000 into a Roth and a taxable custodial account, a disciplined $19,000 a year across the three can compound past $600,000 by adulthood. The same piece flags the obvious risk of handing an 18-year-old a seven-figure balance with no strings, and makes the case for trusts with age and milestone triggers instead.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • South Korea. Per the Kalshi list, the Kospi fell 5.7% and authorities halted program selling after Samsung's revenue miss, a reminder of how much of that index rides on two chipmakers.
  • Texas. Per the Polymarket list, Toyota is putting $3.6B into a Texas plant and moving some Tacoma production out of Mexico, roughly 2,000 jobs, the latest concrete reshoring receipt.
  • United States. Per the same list, Trump touted Walmart cutting prices, including about 15% off ground beef, as evidence the inflation fight is turning.
  • China. Per the Stocks list, Alibaba is barring its staff from using Anthropic's Claude Code by July 10 amid a dispute over China-detection code, another brick in the US-China tech wall.
  • Washington. Per the Kalshi list, a Treasury internal report is now flagging AI-bubble risk, a notable tell when the official machinery starts naming the thing out loud.

item.title: [AI & Tools] Fable 5 leaves the subscription tonight, and the whole ecosystem spent the last day trying to bottle what it does before the meter starts
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: At 11:59pm Pacific the best coding model goes credits-only, so the playbooks are all about extracting its process into cheaper models, even as builders argue you cannot keep a model's judgment at all. Meanwhile Tencent dropped a frontier model for free and Groq is three weeks from the wall.
item.content:encoded:
At 11:59pm Pacific the best coding model goes credits-only, so the playbooks are all about extracting its process into cheaper models, even as builders argue you cannot keep a model's judgment at all. Meanwhile Tencent dropped a frontier model for free and Groq is three weeks from the wall.

Today is the last day Fable 5 is cheap. It leaves every Claude subscription tonight and moves to pay-per-token, so the entire creator layer spent the last 24 hours on one question: how much of what makes it good can you keep once you are paying by the token. Here is what actually matters.

1. Fable 5 goes credits-only tonight, and the economics are the whole story. Mark the clock. Per the Hermes list, Fable 5 leaves all Claude subscriptions at 11:59pm Pacific and then costs real money per token, which changes how you should drive it.

  • The biggest lever is the effort dial. Per Chase AI, Fable at low or medium effort matches or beats Opus 4.8 at a fraction of the cost, with concrete numbers of about $3.76 at low and $6 at medium against roughly $13 for Opus, and the max setting actively overthinks and produces worse output for more money.
  • The pattern to adopt is planner-plus-worker. Per Nate Herk, letting Fable or Opus orchestrate while Sonnet or Haiku do the execution lands roughly the same result at three times less cost.
  • The deadline chores worth doing today, per Chase AI: run a self-audit of your last 30 days of sessions to mint new skills, clean up your skill files, and point the model at your one hardest codebase before the subsidy ends.

2. The real argument: can you keep a model's judgment, or just its habits? This is the fight to watch. Per Nate Herk, you can install a "fable mode" skill that makes Opus 4.8 run Fable's five-gate discipline of scope, evidence, attack, verify, and report, and he even had Fable read its own leaked system prompt to extract the habits, like verifying that a file actually exists before trusting a prompt that implies it. Per Chase AI, that is "nonsense, the model is the model," and no skill file distills raw intelligence.

They are both partly right, and the honest middle is the useful part. You cannot copy the intelligence, but you can absolutely keep the process, the model-routing table, the verification gates, the effort discipline, and that process is most of what a careful operator actually felt when Fable seemed smart. Keep the checklist, not the magic.

3. The Hermes agent stack shipped again, and the security tooling is getting serious. The open-agent layer is not slowing down. Per the Hermes list, the Nous Hermes agent landed a six-feature update with a Mixture of Agents orchestrator, a `/learn` command that turns a URL into a skill, a `/journey` memory timeline, and Fable 5 built in as a callable profile.

  • The flare to watch is offensive security. Pliny released T3MP3ST, an autonomous red-team harness that runs on Claude Code, Codex, or Hermes, and it posted a 90.1% pass on the black-box XBEN benchmark and 98.7% white-box, beating the incumbent XBOW's 85%, while pinning eight of ten post-cutoff 2026 CVEs to the exact file and line.
  • The plumbing keeps consolidating too, with Nous Portal offering 300-plus models and a tool gateway under a single sign-on.

4. Tencent dropped a frontier-class model for free, and the cheap tier keeps rising. The floor under everyone keeps lifting. Per the Hermes list, Tencent released Hunyuan Hy3, a 295-billion-parameter mixture-of-experts model under Apache 2.0 that it claims rivals trillion-scale flagships, with a free API on OpenRouter for two weeks and open weights on HuggingFace. Alongside it, builders are already routing DeepSeek V4 Flash, MiMo, and MiniMax through their agents, and running Qwen 3.6 locally on Nvidia's DGX Spark. The pattern from yesterday holds: the expensive frontier is for the hard 5%, and the free tier now handles the rest.

5. Under the surface, the infrastructure is straining. Two signals that the boom has a cost side. Per the Hermes list, the chip startup Groq is reportedly three weeks from collapse and floating "Groq Bonds" that swap salary for equity, with 80% of staff taking the deal, a stark reminder that not every AI-infrastructure name survives the buildout. On the research side, John Carmack is pointing at NAND-flash inference accelerators that could be 100 times cheaper per gigabyte than the high-bandwidth memory everyone is fighting over, which would reroute the whole cost curve if it works. And per the Polymarket list, Cloudflare is rolling out a monetization gateway to charge AI crawlers for sites, APIs, and even tool calls, another sign the free-scraping era is closing.

item.title: [Investing] The tape was green every hour of the session, and underneath it the smartest money and the loudest bear were both quietly selling the leaders
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Stocks ran a clean trend day up on the first session back, but Aschenbrenner's fund is short the chips and long the power, Michael Burry says the end is near, and the money is rotating from the design layer into the stuff that keeps the lights on. AMD led, Micron faded, and Saylor sold Bitcoin into a shrug.
item.content:encoded:
Stocks ran a clean trend day up on the first session back, but Aschenbrenner's fund is short the chips and long the power, Michael Burry says the end is near, and the money is rotating from the design layer into the stuff that keeps the lights on. AMD led, Micron faded, and Saylor sold Bitcoin into a shrug.

Two tapes again, and today they sat right on top of each other. On the surface it was a picture-perfect melt-up, orderly and green into the close. One layer down, the people with the best track records spent the day rotating out of the exact names that led. Here is the recap, then the rotation nobody on the ticker was talking about.

The close. The first session back from the long weekend was a clean trend day up. Per the Financial Markets list, Linda Raschke called it a pure trend day out of a three-bar triangle with no reversal, the S&P held near 7520 into the bell, and the running joke was that the market has been green every day of July. Per the People-to-follow list, $AMD was the standout up more than 10% as shorts got liquidated and Lisa Su appeared at a Trump event, while ISM Services printed 54 with a firm 51.2 employment read, and the Fear and Greed index sits at a neutral 45.

$DELL ran 8% after Trump told people to go buy one and Michael Dell pledged billions to child accounts. SpaceX joins the Nasdaq 100 tonight under $SPCX, forcing more than $4B of passive buying at the close. The one date that matters this week is still the FOMC minutes on Wednesday July 8.

1. The smartest money spent the day rotating out of the chips and into the power. This is the story under the melt-up. Per Invest with Henry, Leopold Aschenbrenner's roughly $20B fund is running about $8.5B of downside against the crowded design layer, with put exposure to the $SMH semi ETF, $NVDA, $AVGO, $AMD, $MU, $TSM, and $ASML (the sole supplier of the EUV lithography every advanced node needs, per Rick Orford), while going long the deployment layer instead: Bloom Energy $BE, SanDisk $SNDK, CoreWeave $CRWV, and $IREN. It is not an anti-AI bet, it is a bet that the market overpays the chip designers and underpays the power, memory, and data-center slots that actually bind.

The institutional read agrees. Per Adam Parker, the former Morgan Stanley strategist, we are in the seventh inning on stock prices but only the third on fundamentals, and the tell he is watching is correlation: Eaton, GE Vernova, Vistra, Constellation, and even gold now track the AI-semis basket more tightly than Nvidia does. His advice is to pair semis with energy and healthcare, the two things that do not move as one when the AI trade wobbles.

2. Michael Burry stopped hedging his words and said the end is near. The loudest bear got louder. Per the Polymarket list and the Kalshi list, Michael Burry followed yesterday's $MU short by declaring the end is near for AI stocks and an imminent major collapse, and Morgan Stanley is now openly describing the AI trade as rotating away from the leaders with semis momentum fading. You do not have to trade on Burry, whose timing is famously early, but when the man who called the last blowup and the biggest sell-side desk are pointing at the same rotation the tape just ran, it is worth knowing which side of it you are on.

3. Memory gapped up at the open and then faded all day, and the calendar is why. The morning's melt-up did not hold in the one name it was built on. Per the Stocks list, $MU gapped up more than 100 points on the Sunday-night open and then lagged the rest of semis for the entire session, unable to reclaim its near-term moving average even as $AMD ripped. The overhang has a date on it. Per the People-to-follow list, SK Hynix filed to list in the US under $SKHY by the end of this week, raising around $28B in the largest US IPO marketing in memory, and Aschenbrenner's own fund is a cornerstone buyer, so the memory bid is rotating into the fresh paper. The offset is real demand: Micron signed a strategic supply agreement to put its memory in Ford's next-generation vehicles, and $MU is now the third-largest weight in the Nasdaq 100.

4. The money that left the chips showed up in power and the neoclouds. If you want to see where the rotation landed, follow the electricity. Per a Goldman map relayed by Milk Road, of the $7.6T headed into AI infrastructure through 2031, power is a small slice but the single gate that can stall everything downstream, which puts scarce-asset owners in the path of the capital. Per Rick Orford, GE Vernova $GEV is pricing new gas turbines 10 to 20% above last quarter into a book that is sold out to 2030, throwing off $4.8B of free cash in a single quarter against a $163B backlog.

And per Barchart and Yahoo Finance, Oklo $OKLO just won DOE safety approval for its test reactor and is building a 1.2 gigawatt Ohio campus to power Meta data centers, even after the stock fell 22% in June when the DOE floated a $17.5B loan program aimed at big traditional reactors instead of small modular ones.

The neoclouds, the high-beta version of the same trade, ripped today. Per the People-to-follow list, $IREN jumped 11% on a reported Anthropic data-center deal in Australia, $CRWV added 5%, and $WULF is carrying a roughly $19B twenty-year Anthropic lease. Own the picks and shovels if you want the theme with less blow-up risk than the levered cloud rentiers.

5. Broadcom is quietly building the chip that pressures Nvidia's whole story. One more crack in the design-layer thesis. Per Rick Orford, Broadcom $AVGO co-designed OpenAI's first custom inference chip, taped out in nine months and targeting gigawatt-scale deployments starting this year. That is a direct shot at Nvidia's pricing power on general-purpose GPUs. The catch is valuation. $AVGO trades north of 53 times earnings against Nvidia near 23, so the custom-silicon future is already partly in the price, and its revenue leans on a handful of giant buyers.

6. Saylor sold Bitcoin for the first time in four years, and the market shrugged it off. The overhang everyone feared arrived, and the tape absorbed it. Per Milk Road, Michael Saylor sold 3,588 Bitcoin for about $216M at an average near $60,197 to fund his preferred dividends, still holds around 850,000 coins, and has flagged room to sell up to $800M more, a known seller sitting above any rally. Yet per the Crypto list, the reaction was positive, with $MSTR and the $STRC preferred both firming and Bitcoin holding $61,000 to $62,000 into the close.

The bottom-callers are getting louder underneath the fear. Per Lark Davis, spot Bitcoin ETFs just flipped back to a $200M inflow, Bernstein notes the 54% drawdown from the $125K peak is far milder than the 75 to 90% declines of past cycles, and altcoin sell pressure has fallen to a multi-year low that usually marks capitulation. Per Jordi Visser, the two forces that crushed crypto, a Fed repricing from cuts to hikes and capital fleeing into AI infrastructure, are both close to exhaustion, and the level to watch is clean: $64,000 reclaimed turns the tape, $60,000 lost brings the $55,000 bid into play. Per 0xResearch, Bitcoin actually closed the day slightly ahead of both the Nasdaq and gold, and the money inside crypto is rotating toward the things that print cash, the exchange tokens and the prediction-market venues, rather than the high-beta majors.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $GEV (GE Vernova). Per Rick Orford, turbines sold out to 2030 with 10 to 20% pricing power and a backlog heading from $163B toward $200B. The risk is conversion, since a chunk of the pipeline is slot reservations on data centers that still need permits, fuel, and financing. A buy-on-dips name, not a chase after a double.
  • $OKLO (Oklo). Per Barchart, DOE safety approval in hand and a 1.2 gigawatt Meta campus in the plan, with a consensus target near $84 against a low-$50s price. Pre-revenue and policy-whipsawed, so size it as a call option on nuclear, not a core hold.
  • $CCXI (Agility Robotics). Per Milk Road, the first pure-play humanoid stock, a SPAC merging with Agility at a $2.5B valuation, whose Digit robot has logged 65,000 operating hours and $300M in binding orders with Amazon, Toyota, and Schaeffler backing it. It burned $100M in cash last year, so this is a multi-year venture bet in a public wrapper.
  • $PFE (Pfizer). Per Stocks with Josh, the same $23.50 entry from this morning, trading below its pre-COVID price at a 6 to 7% yield with the revenue-cliff fear priced in. Accumulate in a range, do not lump in.
  • $QBTS (D-Wave). Per Rick Orford, fifteen analysts rate it a strong buy with targets implying a possible double, helped by $100M of proposed government funding, though the valuation rests entirely on quantum becoming a real layer rather than on today's financials.

Psychology. Notice how good today felt. Green every hour, $AMD up double digits with the shorts carried out, SpaceX forcing four billion dollars of buying into the bell, the Fear and Greed needle sitting at a comfortable neutral. Everything about the tape invited you to relax and add. That is precisely the moment to look at who was selling into it. Per Invest with Henry, the sharpest young manager in the game is short the chips you want to chase, and per the Polymarket list, Michael Burry is telling anyone who will listen that the end is near. A green trend day does not make them right, but it is the exact backdrop where you are most tempted to oversize into the names they are fading.

Then look at Bitcoin for the mirror image. Saylor sold for the first time in four years, the single most bearish headline a Bitcoin holder could imagine, and per the Crypto list the market simply shrugged and held $61,000. When the worst-case news lands and the price refuses to break, that tells you something the fear does not. The discipline tonight is symmetrical. Do not let a flawless up day talk you into full size at the top of a rotation, and do not let a scary headline talk you out of a position that is quietly refusing to fall. The tape rewards the person who feels both pulls and sizes off the plan anyway.

item.title: [News] Washington started depositing 1,000 dollars into every newborn's brokerage account, and billionaires lined up to add to the pot
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Trump Accounts went live over the holiday with rare bipartisan backing and billions in private pledges, Russia hit Kyiv the day after a call with Trump, and the AI layoffs arrived with a very careful denial. Here is what moved after the AM edition.
item.content:encoded:
Trump Accounts went live over the holiday with rare bipartisan backing and billions in private pledges, Russia hit Kyiv the day after a call with Trump, and the AI layoffs arrived with a very careful denial. Here is what moved after the AM edition.

The morning was quiet, the evening filled in the edges. A new government program put investment accounts in the hands of every American child, the war in Ukraine escalated right after a leaders' call, and a fresh round of tech layoffs came wrapped in a careful message about who is really being replaced. Here is the update.

1. The government is now seeding a brokerage account for every newborn, and the money is bipartisan. This is the domestic story of the weekend. Per Anthony Pompliano, Trump Accounts went live on July 4, a tax-deferred custodial account that drops a one-time $1,000 government deposit into an S&P 500 fund for every US child born between 2025 and 2028, with families able to add up to $5,000 a year and the balance locked until age 18 for college, a business, or a home.

The private money piled in on top. Michael and Susan Dell pledged $6.25B to seed accounts for 25 million children, Gwynne Shotwell and her husband committed 2 million SpaceX shares worth more than $300M, and JPMorgan, Bank of America, and Coinbase each rolled out programs to match another $1,000 per employee child. Per the People-to-follow list, Trump personally rang in the launch for 1.4 million accounts. The rare part is the politics: this drew support across the aisle, a genuine attempt to hand the next generation a stake in the market rather than an argument about it.

2. Russia hit Kyiv the day after a Putin-Trump call, and Ukraine struck deep into Russia in return. The war took a harder turn. Per the Polymarket list, Russia killed at least eight people in a barrage on Kyiv just after a July 4 phone call between Putin and Trump, the kind of timing that reads as a message rather than a coincidence. Ukraine answered with its deepest strike of the war, hitting an oil refinery in Omsk some 2,500 kilometers inside Russia, extending its campaign against the fuel that moves the Russian military. A call meant to lower the temperature was followed within a day by an escalation on both ends.

3. The AI layoffs arrived with a careful denial, and the squeeze underneath it is real. The job cuts came, and so did the messaging around them. Per the People-to-follow list, Microsoft is cutting about 4,800 jobs, roughly 2.1% of its workforce, and its HR chief Amy Coleman went out of her way to say those workers are not being replaced by AI, while Porsche announced 4,000 cuts in Germany. The denial is doing a lot of work in a week when every headline is about automation.

Underneath the layoffs is a slower grind. Per ClearValue Tax, wages are up 23% since 2021 while prices are up 28%, property insurance has climbed 75% in five years, housing now eats more than 40% of the typical income, and credit card debt has hit a record $1.25T with delinquencies at their highest since 2008. The Trump Accounts are a bet on the next generation precisely because this generation cannot get ahead.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • China. Per the Polymarket list, Beijing test-fired a ballistic missile from a nuclear submarine into the Pacific, a rare and deliberate show of reach.
  • China, again. Per the same list, regulators forced ByteDance and Alibaba to pull AI-companion features from their apps, a sharp turn against the consumer AI its own firms are building.
  • Saudi Arabia. Per the same list, Riyadh cut its August Arab Light price to Asia by $11 a barrel, the largest such cut in more than 26 years, a signal it is fighting for market share as OPEC+ lifts output.
  • United States. Per a Prof G Markets clip, Scott Galloway used the fight over rolling back 14th Amendment birthright citizenship to needle Stephen Miller and Trump, noting that under the proposed change he, the son of immigrants, would not be a citizen, and joking he would like his decade of taxes back.

item.title: [AI & Tools] OpenAI offered Washington a 42 billion dollar stake in itself, and that tells you the AI race stopped being about who has the best model
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Sam Altman wants to hand the government 5% of OpenAI, Meta is renting out its GPUs while free Chinese models eat a third of all AI traffic, and three of the four US frontier labs got gated in a single release cycle. The scoreboard is now compute, distribution, and permission.
item.content:encoded:
Sam Altman wants to hand the government 5% of OpenAI, Meta is renting out its GPUs while free Chinese models eat a third of all AI traffic, and three of the four US frontier labs got gated in a single release cycle. The scoreboard is now compute, distribution, and permission.

The model-quality race is not what decides this anymore. Today made that explicit: the biggest lab in the world offered the government equity, the second-biggest social network turned into a cloud vendor, and free Chinese models kept taking share from everyone. Here is what actually moved.

1. OpenAI offered the US government a 5% stake in itself, and that is the whole story. Follow the equity, not the benchmark. Per Nate B Jones, OpenAI has floated donating roughly 5% of the company, about $42.5B at its $852B valuation, into a public wealth fund modeled on the Alaska Permanent Fund, and Altman reportedly wants the same 5% tithe applied to every leading US lab.

  • The trigger was permission rather than generosity. GPT-5.6 was held in limited preview after the Commerce Secretary personally called Altman, and a June executive order gives the government up to 30 days of pre-release access to frontier models.
  • The political frame is already a fight. Per Prof G Markets, Bernie Sanders has a competing bill that would pull 50% of AI-company stock into a sovereign fund, and OpenAI delaying its IPO reads to some as a demand-shock tell rather than patience.
  • The number under it all: OpenAI is running about $13B of revenue against $34B of spend, a $21B annual loss, so the friendliest possible relationship with Washington is not a nicety, it is strategy.

2. Meta turned its spare GPUs into a business, and the market repriced who owns the moat. The second big move was a landlord play. Per Nate B Jones, Meta is standing up a cloud business to rent out excess compute against AWS, Azure, and Google, on roughly $145B of 2026 spend, and it launched a consumer app called Gizmos that turns a prompt into a playable game.

  • Per Prof G Markets, Meta jumped about 9% on the pivot while CoreWeave and Nebius fell roughly 12%, because a hyperscaler renting spare capacity is bad news for the pure-play cloud rentiers.
  • The share picture explains the urgency. ChatGPT holds about 49% of usage, Gemini 20%, Claude 14%, Grok 5%, and Meta's own Llama just 2.1%, so Meta is monetizing infrastructure precisely because it is losing the model race.
  • Zuckerberg reportedly told staff internally that agent development has not accelerated the way they expected, the quiet admission under all the capex.

3. Free Chinese models now serve a third of all AI traffic, and that is the margin threat. This is the number that should scare the whole US stack. Per Prof G Markets, free Chinese models went from 30% to 60% of AI traffic in six months, and the enterprise defections are named: Coinbase and Cursor moved work to Kimi, Shopify and Airbnb to Qwen, Siemens to DeepSeek, and even Microsoft is testing DeepSeek. It has pushed OpenAI to weigh price cuts, and it means a chunk of the $300B infrastructure build was laid for customers who are leaving for something free.

4. Federal oversight is now routine, and the practical lesson is have a fallback. Three of the four US frontier labs got gated in a single release cycle. Per the AI Central landscape review, here is the timeline worth keeping:
