item.description: Burry is shorting Micron on nothing but a chart while Shkreli and Aschenbrenner argue the supply is structurally locked, the Fed conversation quietly flipped from when-they-cut to might-they-hike, and Bitcoin is being bought and doubted at the exact same $62,500.
item.content:encoded:
Burry is shorting Micron on nothing but a chart while Shkreli and Aschenbrenner argue the supply is structurally locked, the Fed conversation quietly flipped from when-they-cut to might-they-hike, and Bitcoin is being bought and doubted at the exact same $62,500.

Nothing traded today and nothing trades tomorrow, so this is the second holiday session to decide what you believe before Monday can price it for you. Three arguments hardened over the afternoon. The memory trade split into a clean bull-bear fight with names on both sides, the rate debate flipped from cuts to a live hike, and Bitcoin sat at one price with the accumulators and the doubters both pointing at it.

The week ahead, and the one thing that changed since this morning. US markets were closed Friday July 3 for Independence Day and stayed closed for the weekend, reopening Monday July 6. Five things to pre-load, and one of them is new since the AM. First and biggest: the Fed conversation is no longer only about cuts. Per the Market list, Polymarket now prices the odds of a rate hike at roughly a coin flip, up from near zero earlier this year, with Chris Waller already calling for one and new chair Kevin Warsh refusing to rule out July. Second, the FOMC minutes land Wednesday at 2pm ET and every line now gets read for hike risk, not just cut timing.

Three more to pre-load. The SK Hynix US listing is still on deck around July 10, a fresh pure-play memory bet in front of American money. $SPCX joins the Nasdaq-100 on July 7. And a record pile of money-market cash still sits on the sideline waiting for one of these to break.

1. The memory trade is now a real bull-bear fight, and the bull case got structural. This morning the receipts were Samsung's DRAM invoice. This afternoon the argument matured into two named sides. On the bear side, per the Market list, Michael Burry's Substack short on Micron is pure technical analysis, Micron overextended from its 200-day average targeting roughly a 30% drawdown, with nothing fundamental or HBM-related under it. That matters, because a short with no thesis about supply is a bet on gravity, not on the business.

The bull side answered with structure. In the Reader inbox, Martin Shkreli argues DRAM is not a commodity at all, because a commodity is something anyone can make with a little capex, and DRAM needs cleanroom fabs full of $ASML and Lam Research machines nobody can stand up on demand. His read is that rational new entrants match the incumbent price rather than start a self-harming war, so memory prices behave like a disciplined oligopoly and the market's assumed back-half decline for $MU may be too bearish. Leopold Aschenbrenner is voting the same way with real weights, per Milk Road Macro: his book is 35% $NBIS, 14.9% $SNDK, and 12.7% $BE, with $MU, $CRWV, $TSM, and $INTC behind them, roughly two-thirds in three names, a pure bet that compute, power, memory, and storage are the only bottlenecks that matter.

2. The 2026 scoreboard says the memory bulls have been right all year, and that is exactly when you check the crowding. The tables that circulated today are a gut-check. Per the People-to-follow list, Shay Boloor's top fifteen performers of 2026 are led by $SNDK up 635%, then $MU up 242%, $INTC up 226%, $WDC up 213%, $MRVL up 189%, and $ARM up 188%. Memory and semis are not participating in this market, they are the market. Stretch it to the decade on the Market list and the same names show up: $NVDA up 16,602%, $AMD up 10,113%, $MU up 7,704%, Lam Research up 4,135%.

The fresh catalyst under the leaderboard is real. Stocks with Josh put a new $1,500 target on Micron off a measured move, activating once price reclaims $1,250, with a hard bounce expected first off the $895 fibonacci level. And the supply side keeps invoicing: per the Stocks list, Infineon raised power-chip prices 10% to 25% for the second time in six months, with silicon content per data-center rack climbing from about $15,000 at 125kW toward $100,000 at a megawatt by 2029. My read: the fundamental bull case is the stronger one, but Burry is shorting the crowd, not the chips, and a tape this one-sided can hand him his 30% on positioning alone before the fundamentals win. Own the trade, respect the unwind.

3. The Fed just became the summer's biggest event, because Warsh made a hike thinkable again. The regime change under Kevin Warsh is the macro story the memory tape is riding on top of. Per the Stocks list, Warsh is killing forward guidance, would not confirm or deny a July hike, and wants what he calls a good family fight at a Fed that John Gillen describes as the most divided in decades, with Powell still seated and Waller already pushing to hike. The last time the Fed hiked was July 2023, and the market fell 10% after. A coin-flip on a hike that the tape has spent a year assuming was impossible is the definition of an unpriced risk.

4. Tom Lee mapped the rest of the year, and the scary part has a date on it. Two independent versions of Tom Lee's roadmap landed today and they agree. Via Lark Davis, Lee sees three phases: a bull run into late summer, an abrupt September-to-October correction that feels like a bear market, then one of the strongest recoveries in decades to close the year. The Market list version from Heisenberg puts numbers on it, the S&P at 7,500 to 7,800 in the next couple months, a 10% to 15% pullback to 6,600 to 7,000, then 8,000 by year end.

The named trigger for the autumn drop is specific and worth marking: the Anthropic and OpenAI IPOs are expected to hit at once and dump fresh supply equal to 5% to 6% of the entire S&P market cap. That is a supply shock with a date attached.

5. Bitcoin is being bought and doubted at the same $62,500, and the alts are where the conviction actually is. Every list quoted Bitcoin near $62,500 today, which the Kalshi list frames brutally: it was $108,100 on last July 4 and $58,660 the July 4 before, so the asset roughly halved year over year. The bulls are leaning in. On Lark Davis, the tell is a record 270,000-coin whale accumulation over 90 days against the worst sentiment in years, a price-bear-market sitting on top of a fundamentals-bull-market. Milk Road makes the patience case that bottoms are a bathtub, not a V, so you buy conviction and let time do the work rather than chasing the exact low. The Crypto list counters with Rekt Capital's caution that this still looks 2022-esque, with any relief rally turning the 50-month average into resistance.

Where the crypto conviction is loudest is not Bitcoin, it is the revenue coins. Per the Crypto list, Hyperliquid's Assistance Fund hit a $3.16B record having already burned $1.18B of $HYPE, Solana did $78M of protocol revenue last month against Ethereum's $39M at a fifth of the value locked, and Ansem is betting hard on a memecoin revival led by the $ANSEM runner. The through-line: if you believe the cycle, the cleaner risk-reward is in the protocols that actually earn, not the majors.

Single names worth a place on the watchlist.

  • $ETN, the pick-and-shovel for the power bottleneck. Rick Orford's case on Eaton is that the real AI constraint is not chips or plants but the transformers and switchgear that connect them, and Eaton owns that bottleneck with 42% electrical order growth and a backlog up 48%. The catch is a 40x multiple that leaves no room for a miss.
  • $INTC, the turnaround with a working fab. Per the Market list and Polymarket, Intel reportedly fixed its 18A yield variability and is ramping toward 30,000 combined wafers a month, framed as the only leading-edge US foundry. It is already up 226% on the year, so the easy money on the recovery is behind you.
  • $PLTR versus $MSFT, the same AI bet at two prices. Rick Orford's head-to-head frames Palantir at roughly 92x forward against Microsoft near 21x. His verdict, which I share, is that Microsoft is the safer way to own the same theme and Palantir is the higher-beta option only if AIP becomes the standard operating layer.
  • $NBIS, the neocloud that keeps signing. The Crypto list and Polymarket both flag Nebius adding an 18MW Spain lease on top of its $27B Meta deal and $2B Nvidia investment, with Q1 revenue up 684%. High-beta and not for the faint of heart, but the contracts are real.
  • $CCXI, the first robotics play from a team that called the memory run. Per the Milk Road PRO recap, Melvin bought Churchill Capital XI at $16.87 as a robotics SPAC while the same desk sold $MU at a 145% gain, a small tell that the smart money is starting to rotate the winnings forward.

The bigger rotation to settle: infrastructure, orchestration, or the apps. The single best debate on the tape is where the AI money goes next, and today it split three ways with real money behind each. Aschenbrenner is all-in on the physical infrastructure. Milk Road's AI desk argues the margin moves past the frontier models to the orchestration layer and the hyperscalers who own compute, citing Dylan Patel's claim that Opus 4.8 API tokens run at 80% margins and noting the Mag 7 trade at low-teens multiples with revenue landing in 2028 to 2030. And the Milk Road PRO desk is rotating out of infra into the applications on a profit-per-user thesis, liking $UBER at about $28 of operating profit per user, $NU at $35, and $COIN at $158. You do not have to pick one today, but you should know which of the three you are actually long.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The Stocks with Josh SPY diamond-pattern call is a clean technical marker, cautiously bearish on a candle close below 740 and cancelled only by a new high, so it stays here as a level to watch rather than a section. The threadguy livestream on trader psychology carried no tickers or levels and was dropped. The Reuters $2.3B Trump-family crypto figure from Trader Mayne went to News as a politics story. The Friedberg immigration test and the Galloway creator-economy segment went to News. The Fable 5 prompting playbook, the free-token weekend, and the Hermes agent tooling went to AI & Tools. Our own kairosllm Daily Tape page in the feed was skipped, since we do not brief our own output.

item.title: [News] Friedberg drew an immigration line by asking one question, and the AI job scare put numbers behind why it lands
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The makers-versus-takers test, 1.8 million Americans stuck in long-term unemployment as half the country fears AI takes the next job, and a Reuters tally that says the Trump family's crypto ventures are now worth $2.3 billion even as the tokens crater.
item.content:encoded:
The makers-versus-takers test, 1.8 million Americans stuck in long-term unemployment as half the country fears AI takes the next job, and a Reuters tally that says the Trump family's crypto ventures are now worth $2.3 billion even as the tokens crater.

Independence Day gave the feed a theme it did not plan: who gets to be an American, and what work is still worth doing once the machines want the same jobs. The immigration argument got a clean test, the labor data got grim, and the president's family got a $2.3 billion crypto scorecard. Below that, a quieter world kept moving.

1. Friedberg reduced the whole immigration fight to one question, and it is a good one. On the All-In podcast, David Friedberg made his immigration test not about culture or country of origin but about a single motive: are you coming to work or coming for benefits. His makers-versus-takers frame would deny entry to anyone whose primary aim is social support and grant it to anyone whose aim is to produce, earn, and advance their family. His deeper claim is the one people resist, that immigration is not a zero-sum fight over a fixed pile of jobs, because anyone who creates more value than they consume grows the economy and creates more jobs for everyone. Agree or not, it is the rare framing that gives both sides a rule instead of a slogan.

2. The AI job scare stopped being a vibe and started being a number. Friedberg's faith in work meets a labor market that is quietly hollowing. Per the Polymarket list, the count of Americans unemployed for at least 27 weeks has averaged above 1.8 million this year, half of Americans now say they fear AI could cost them or a family member a job, and in a tell about where the displacement runs, AI hiring in India's IT sector is rising even as overall recruitment there declines. The machines are not taking every job, they are taking the entry rungs and the routine middle, exactly the ladder Friedberg's makers climb. One more sign of the new normal from the same list: voters are reportedly turning to AI chatbots for help before casting their ballots, so the tool reshaping the labor market is now in the voting booth too.

3. The Trump family's crypto empire is now a $2.3 billion number, and the tokens are down anyway. Per Trader Mayne relaying Reuters, the Trump family's crypto ventures are collectively valued near $2.3 billion, even though the flagship assets have cratered: the TRUMP memecoin has generated roughly $635 million in fees while falling about 96% from its peak, and World Liberty Financial's WLFI token is down around 70% despite paper values in the hundreds of millions. The story is the gap. The ventures print fees and paper wealth for the family while retail holders eat the drawdown, the conflict-of-interest engine in one chart. It pairs with the fresh Trump Accounts wrinkle on the Polymarket list, where the Treasury will now reportedly accept donations of publicly traded stock to fund the new children's accounts.

4. Galloway put a price on the creator economy, and the money is leaving the middle. From Cannes Lions, Scott Galloway's read on the creator economy is that brand spending on creators is up about 24% year over year, with roughly half of it now flowing to nano and micro creators rather than the megastars. His blunt law of the attention economy is that the most takes the most, a winner-take-more dynamic that mirrors the same top-heavy pull reshaping labor and markets everywhere else in today's feed.

Around the world, quickly.

  • Iran. Per the Polymarket list, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's funeral procession will travel through five cities across Iran and Iraq, a staged succession moment for a state in transition.
  • Iran and China, at the strait. The same list reports Iran declaring that China will get special considerations in any new Strait of Hormuz fee arrangement, a sign the emerging chokepoint tolls will be political, not neutral.
  • Taiwan. China's Coast Guard deployed a new patrol task group east of Taiwan, per the Polymarket list, pushing its presence past the island's far side.
  • Moldova. The prime minister resigned, triggering the collapse of the government, another wobble on Europe's eastern edge.
  • Germany. Chancellor Friedrich Merz said Germany has no reason to shy away from its military spending push, while Berlin is reportedly weighing an end to its one-year crypto tax exemption under the 2027 budget.
  • Drones as an industry. German startup Quantum Systems raised $1.2 billion at an $8 billion valuation, per the Polymarket list, as Russia's largest jobs site advertises for drone operators to defend Moscow. The war economy is now a hiring market.
  • Canada. Ottawa unveiled plans for an oil pipeline to Asia in a bid to become an energy superpower and cut its dependence on the US.
  • Japan. Amid record immigration, Japan is hiking residence permit fees, including a reported 1,900% jump for permanent residency applications, the exact taker-screening instinct Friedberg was arguing about, applied with a price tag.

Filtered. Gold overtaking US government bonds as the world's top reserve asset appeared again today, but we covered it in the AM edition, so it stays noted rather than repeated. The Kalshi America250 novelty markets and the World Cup and hot-dog-contest prediction markets are holiday filler with no news value. A new Polymarket market asking whether the US government will remove public access to another Anthropic AI model in 2026 is a market to watch but not yet an event. The Trump-family crypto valuation is a politics story here, the memory and Bitcoin and neocloud material went to Investing, and the Fable 5 and agent-tooling items went to AI & Tools.

item.title: [AI & Tools] The whole skill just flipped from pushing the model to reining it in, right as the free tokens turn into a trap
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Dylan Davis says your best old prompts now make Fable 5 worse, Nate B Jones shows how to burn the free weekend tokens on hard problems, and the All-In crew warns the giveaway is a trojan horse for the app layer.
item.content:encoded:
Dylan Davis says your best old prompts now make Fable 5 worse, Nate B Jones shows how to burn the free weekend tokens on hard problems, and the All-In crew warns the giveaway is a trojan horse for the app layer.

For two years the AI skill was the same: push harder, prompt longer, add steps until it did enough. This weekend that skill inverted. The new models do too much, the labs are telling you to get out of the way, and the free tokens everyone is stacking come with a catch. Here is the shift, the playbook, and the warning.

1. Your best prompt is now a liability, and the fix is restraint. Dylan Davis, who runs an AI consultancy, argues the new generation makes your old prompts backfire. Fable 5 will run for hours, take actions nobody asked for, even claim work it never did. His fix is three controls plus one caveat.

  • Cut the scaffolding. Straight from Anthropic's own Fable 5 user guide: old prompts are too prescriptive and degrade output on smarter models. Anthropic cut 80% of the prompt behind Claude Code for the new models. They call it un-hobbling, getting out of the way.
  • Prompt the destination, not the steps. The template is a goal, the audience, and most of all what done looks like in binary terms. Point it and say go, because without a finish line it over-engineers.
  • The fence. Split thinking from acting. Tell it plainly to assess and then stop and do nothing, then in the action phase tell it to do the simplest thing that gets the one task done and nothing else.
  • The dial. Reasoning runs low, medium, high, extra, max. Reserve extra and max for genuinely hard multi-hour work, because those levels spawn parallel sub-agents and the cost climbs fast.
  • Cherish the pushback. When Fable 5 disagrees with your feedback, do not override it. Ask what it knows that you do not, and what would change its mind.
  • Two catches. Fable 5 goes paid after roughly July 7 regardless of your plan, so save it for the hardest tasks. And in this early government-gated rollout it may silently downgrade you to Opus 4.8 on biology or cybersecurity questions and stay dumb for the whole chat, so start a fresh conversation to get the good model back.

2. If the tokens are free this weekend, aim them at something hard. Nate B Jones's free-token playbook is the practical companion to the restraint argument. His moves: build a goal harness to steer Codex 5.5 toward an outcome rather than micromanaging it, plug the model into real tools like Blender so it acts instead of just talks, keep prompts short, and point all of it at a hard business problem rather than toy demos while the compute is subsidized. Same lesson as Davis from the other direction: set the destination, then let the model work.

3. But the free tokens are a trojan horse, and the target is the app layer. The All-In crew's warning is the strategic frame under the giveaway. Free tokens are not generosity, they are customer acquisition, and the analogy they reach for is Microsoft using cheap Excel and Word to bury WordPerfect and Lotus. The models are commoditizing, so the real prize is the application layer on top, and OpenAI is spending to own it before anyone else does. Take the free compute, but know what it is buying them.

4. The margin is moving to the orchestration layer. This is the tooling half of a thesis that also runs in Investing today. Milk Road's AI desk argues the money moves past the frontier model to whoever orchestrates the work: route the cheap, high-volume tasks to small or local models and reserve the frontier model for the hard calls. Coinbase is the cited example of a shop wiring exactly that kind of cost-aware routing. Dylan Patel's claim that Opus 4.8 API tokens run near 80% margins is why the labs can afford to give the entry tokens away and still win.

5. The agents shipping this week are doing real, pointed jobs. From the Hermes ecosystem roundup, the pattern is narrow agents that undercut incumbents:

  • UNBROKER, an agent that hunts down your personal data across data-broker sites and files the removals, aimed squarely at undercutting subscription services like DeleteMe.
  • Grok Composer 2.5 reportedly cloned the Rampage app in under 120 seconds, another data point that the build-an-app moat is thinning fast.
  • Hermes Vault v0.18.0 shipped, continuing the open agentic-tooling drumbeat from the Nous Research corner.

Filtered. Vox Day's Winter Market post was an AI-music-video devlog with no named model, tool, or technique worth passing on, so it was dropped. The equity side of the orchestration and capex debate, including the neocloud and hyperscaler names, went to Investing. The immigration and creator-economy segments from the same All-In and Galloway sources went to News.

item.title: [Investing] Bitcoin is sitting on the line it has not lost since 2023, and the two sides have never disagreed this loudly
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Whales bought 270,000 coins into the drop while Citi cut its target and Strategy sold for the first time ever, the memory hike went from forecast to invoice, and the new Fed chair is quietly arguing that AI makes rate cuts easier.
item.content:encoded:
Whales bought 270,000 coins into the drop while Citi cut its target and Strategy sold for the first time ever, the memory hike went from forecast to invoice, and the new Fed chair is quietly arguing that AI makes rate cuts easier.

It is a holiday weekend and nothing trades until Monday, so this is a morning to decide what you believe before the tape can bully you. The loudest argument on the board is Bitcoin at its 200-week average, where the accumulation data and the outflow data point in opposite directions at the same price. Under it sits a regulation story that finally has a real framework, and next to it a memory trade that just turned a price forecast into an actual invoice.

The week ahead, and what to pre-load before Monday's open. US markets were closed Friday July 3 for Independence Day and reopen Monday July 6. Five things to load. First, the FOMC minutes land Wednesday at 2pm ET, and after new chair Kevin Warsh started tearing up the Fed's script, every line gets read twice. Second, the SK Hynix US listing is still on deck for around July 10, a fresh pure-play memory bet in front of American money. Third, $SPCX joins the Nasdaq-100 on July 7 and GPT-5.6 is priced to arrive as soon as Tuesday.

Fourth, the crypto Clarity Act still has a coin-flip shot at a July vote. Fifth, a record $8.3T sits in money-market funds. That is either the fuel for the next leg up or a crowd that likes getting paid to wait.

1. Bitcoin lost its 200-week average, and the accumulation data and the outflow data are screaming past each other. Bitcoin is trading right at the 200-week moving average (the four-year trend line that has marked every major cycle low), the first time it has closed below it since 2023. Lark Davis makes the bull case with on-chain receipts. Whales accumulated roughly 270,000 BTC, about $16B, near the $60K zone, against only $5B of ETF net outflows, and the weekly RSI (a momentum gauge) is oversold for just the fourth time since 2012, with three of those prior four marking bottoms.

His key argument is that the one exception, 2022, only broke because Terra, Celsius, and FTX detonated a leverage chain that no longer exists in the same form. The reclaim line is clean: a weekly close back above roughly $63K puts the trend back on side.

The other side showed up with its own receipts on Swan's World Cup of Money. Citi cut its Bitcoin target to $82K and zeroed out its 12-month ETF inflow forecast, and for the first time in its history Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) reportedly sold Bitcoin, which pokes a hole in the Saylor-never-sells thesis the whole treasury trade was built on. Kalshi bettors now put 55% odds on Bitcoin tagging $50K before $100K. My read: the accumulation is real and the miner-cost floor near $60K still holds, so I am accumulating the dips I already wanted and treating a weekly close under $60K as the signal to stop. The Strategy sale is the single fact that would flip me, so I want it confirmed, not rumored.

2. The ETH short is the most crowded trade in crypto, and that cuts both ways. Underneath the Bitcoin fight is a barbell the whales have all piled onto: long Bitcoin, short Ether. Per the Crypto list, a single 54,200 ETH short worth about $89.2M sits on Hyperliquid with a liquidation price of $1,674, and roughly $95.6M of ETH shorts were already liquidated in 24 hours. When one side of a trade gets this lopsided, the fuel for a violent squeeze is the crowd itself.

The tell to respect is that 33% of all ETH supply is now staked and growing by 50,000 coins a day, which quietly pulls float off the market even while the derivatives crowd leans bearish. I am not chasing the short here.

3. Crypto regulation finally has a real framework, and it is more radical than the market has priced. The positioning story is the Clarity Act, the crypto market-structure bill, and Kevin O'Leary frames its signing as the trigger for the largest capital inflow in Bitcoin's history, with trillions waiting on legal clarity. The substance under that hope is worth reading directly. In the SEC's own "Project Crypto" outline, Chairman Paul Atkins lays out a four-bucket token taxonomy where digital commodities, digital collectibles, and digital tools are explicitly not securities, and only tokenized versions of real stocks and bonds stay securities.

The radical part is a doctrinal shift: an investment contract can end. A token sold under one does not stay a security forever, and once the network decentralizes and the promises are fulfilled, later trades are no longer securities transactions. Atkins wants rules letting those tokens trade on CFTC or state-regulated venues, a "Regulation Crypto" exemption package, and single-license "super-apps," and he states plainly that most tokens trading today are not securities. My read: this is the fundamental case sitting under O'Leary's flood. If the Clarity Act codifies even part of this taxonomy, the compliance box that has kept the largest pools of institutional money on the sidelines finally gets checked.

4. The memory hike went from forecast to invoice, and the trade got fresh receipts. Yesterday this was Micron's record print against Burry's short. This morning it is the supplier sending the bill. Samsung has begun issuing verbal notices to raise DRAM prices (dynamic RAM, the standard memory in every server and phone) by around 20% quarter over quarter for Q3, per the Market list. A price hike you forecast is a thesis, and a price hike your supplier invoices is a fact.

The numbers underneath keep getting louder. Bernstein sees Micron's DRAM gross margins above 90% in 2026 and above 92% in the back half, and Milk Road's Stocks list carries a $4,000 target with Sanjay Mehrotra saying Micron can meet only half to two-thirds of demand and no new industry supply arrives before 2028. SK Hynix just posted a single quarter's profit of $26.15B, which tops its entire 2023 revenue, on 60% HBM (high-bandwidth memory, the stacked chips bolted to AI accelerators) share and a 72% operating margin, and it lists on the Nasdaq next week. Jensen Huang called Marvell the "next trillion-dollar company," with $MRVL guiding FY2027 revenue to $11.5B, up 40%.

The fragility to keep in view is the crowding. South Korea's leveraged-ETF assets hit a record near $45B, up 800% year to date and about 2.9% of free float, so the same names everyone loves are also where the levered money is stacked. When that unwinds it does not ask politely.

5. The new Fed chair's real argument is that AI makes cutting easier. Kevin Warsh is not just tearing up forward guidance (the Fed's habit of telegraphing its next move). Per the Stocks list, his framing is an AI-deflation thesis: frontier inference cost fell from roughly $30 per million tokens in early 2023 to pennies, and AI has added only about 0.3 point to core PCE (the Fed's preferred inflation gauge). He wants a "good family fight" at the FOMC about four weeks out, will not confirm a July hike, and wants a smaller balance sheet, leaning on the Greenspan 1990s analogue where productivity ran hot and inflation stayed tame.

The honest counter is that software prices are going the other way, with Microsoft 365 up 30%, Adobe up 50%, and Intuit up 45% on the year. My read: this is the rates story for the back half, and a chair who removes the script guarantees a wider reaction to every print.

Single names worth a place on the watchlist.

  • $AMZN, the robotics-and-logistics AI trade. On Prof G Markets, Scott Galloway's 2026 big-tech pick is Amazon and Tom Lee agreed "100%." The case: Amazon runs about 1 million industrial robots against roughly 400,000 for the rest of the country combined, and Lee frames it as a logistics company whose addressable market is anything that needs moving, riffing that robots could eventually double its TAM (total addressable market) into housing and offices.
  • The neocloud dip keeps getting cheaper: $NBIS, $IREN. Nebius posted Q1 revenue of $399M, up 684% year over year, on a $27B five-year Meta contract and a $2B Nvidia investment, per the Crypto list. $IREN has now fallen nine straight days into its most oversold reading in a year even as its CEO sold zero shares and analysts carry a $200-plus target. High-beta and not for the faint of heart.
  • $PLTR, upgraded into the open-model shift. DA Davidson moved Palantir to Buy with a $175 target per the People-to-follow list, and the moat argument is that customers can now swap the AI model underneath without leaving the platform.
  • $SOL and the tokenization tape. Securitize tokenized $295M of its own NYSE stock on Solana at its IPO, and Solana's real-world-asset value went from $873M to $3.6B in six months across 291,000 holders, with SOL near $82. This is the cleanest live read on whether onchain adoption is actually happening.
  • $TAO, the decentralized-AI satellite. Milk Road's Bittensor deep-dive frames TAO near $200 with a 21M hard cap and roughly 11.5M in circulation, its top AI-compute subnets already running near $20M of annualized revenue, and Jensen name-dropping it on All-In. Size it as a long-dated option rather than a core hold.
  • $META, buying its way off TSMC. Meta signed a $6.5B deal with Samsung Foundry to build its third-generation MTIA AI chips on a 2nm process, per the People-to-follow list, a real step toward owning its own silicon.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The "Don't Miss this Move" clip from Stocks with Josh was a paid-Discord funnel, so its only reusable facts (a Micron 861 to 895 fib buy-zone and the Wednesday FOMC minutes) are better sourced elsewhere, and the rest stays out. Four other Stocks with Josh scalps (Nike, a Meta +65% intraday call, Tesla, and a soccer bet) were day-trade clickbait with no durable signal and got dropped. The SCOTUS birthright ruling, the Trump disclosure and Trump Accounts launch, and the Newsom budget went to News. The Fable 5 economics, Hermes agent release, and the DeepSeek and Alibaba price-war moves went to AI & Tools. The robotics-chokepoint names (Harmonic Drive and Schaeffler) are interesting but thin foreign microcaps, so they stay on the shelf for now.

item.title: [News] On the Fourth of July, the Supreme Court hands Trump a defeat on citizenship while his disclosure shows how much richer the office made him
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The Court reportedly struck the birthright-citizenship order, a new filing puts the president's portfolio near $3.8B with 327 stock buys the day before he paused tariffs, and the data says the AI squeeze is landing hardest on people under 25.
item.content:encoded:
The Court reportedly struck the birthright-citizenship order, a new filing puts the president's portfolio near $3.8B with 327 stock buys the day before he paused tariffs, and the data says the AI squeeze is landing hardest on people under 25.

The holiday is quiet but two American stories underneath it are not. The Supreme Court dealt the White House a loss on one of its signature orders, and a fresh financial disclosure shows just how well the presidency has paid. Then the labor data keeps saying the same uncomfortable thing about who AI is actually displacing.

1. The Supreme Court reportedly struck down Trump's birthright-citizenship order. Per the All-In show notes, the Court ruled against the executive order seeking to end birthright citizenship, leaving the Fourteenth Amendment's guarantee in place. This is the show's framing rather than a court transcript, so treat the reasoning as still to be read in the opinion itself. The politics are clean regardless of the legal detail. A conservative-majority Court drawing a line on one of the administration's defining orders is the kind of check that reshapes what the next two years of executive action can attempt.

2. Trump's own disclosure shows how much the office is worth. A new filing puts the president's portfolio near $3.8B, including more than $1.3B in crypto-related income, per the People-to-follow list. The sharper detail sits in the Stocks list: Kalshi and NBC reporting flags that Trump-linked investment accounts made more than 300 stock purchases the day before he paused his tariffs, a sequence critics frame as pocketing roughly $1.4B on the reversal.

The timing lands against a new policy launching the same weekend. Per the Crypto list, "Trump Accounts" formally launch on July 4, running about a million signups a month, with the Treasury set to accept donations of publicly traded stock into them. Whatever the merits, the optics of a president disclosing billions while launching a stock-funded savings program on Independence Day will define the week's coverage.

3. The AI jobs squeeze is landing on the young, and the numbers are getting hard to wave off. The clearest signal in the Crypto list is generational. A Stanford and ADP study finds workers aged 22 to 25 in AI-exposed jobs are down 13% since late 2022, recent-graduate unemployment sits at 5.6% against a 4.3% national rate, and 42.5% of graduates are underemployed. The counterweight is that firms adopting AI early are still adding people, with Ramp data showing AI-adopter headcount up 10.2% and entry-level roles up 12%.

Read together, the story is not "AI took the jobs" but "AI is reshaping who gets hired first." The bottom rung of the ladder is where the pressure shows up earliest, and that is a political problem long before it is an economic one.

Around the world, quickly.

  • California and Colorado, two Democratic story lines. Per the All-In show notes, Governor Newsom is touting a balanced California budget that critics say papers over real fiscal strain, while a democratic-socialist candidate, Melat Kiros, reportedly pulled a primary upset in Colorado. Both feed the 2028 jockeying already showing up in prediction markets.
  • Argentina. Kalshi bettors put Javier Milei's standing near 58% per the Kalshi list, keeping his reform experiment the one to watch in Latin America.
  • The wealth-tax line keeps moving down. Per the Financial Markets list, Representative Ro Khanna argued a wealth tax "must not stop at billionaires" and should reach fortunes as low as $50M, a marker for where the Democratic left wants the debate.
  • Gold quietly became the world's largest reserve asset. Central-bank buying pushed gold past US Treasuries as the top reserve holding, per the Kalshi list, the same week US federal debt topped 100% of GDP for the first time since 1946. Lyn Alden's read on the Stocks list is that the deficit now exceeds all private bank lending combined.
  • Washington cancelled its July 4 parade for extreme heat. A small but telling detail from the Financial Markets list as much of the country bakes.
  • Prediction markets are becoming an asset class, and the regulators noticed. Bernstein projects a $1T industry by 2030, per the Financial Markets list, even as Europe's ESMA warns event contracts could fall under its 2018 binary-options retail ban.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The Palantir and Nvidia open-model deal and Alex Karp's on-air combativeness came through the same All-In notes but belong to AI & Tools. The Trump portfolio's crypto-income line and the tariff-timed trades also carry a markets angle covered in Investing. The DeepSeek and Alibaba distillation fight went to AI & Tools. Tucker Carlson teasing an unspecified "major political plan" is a tease until there is a plan, so it waits.

item.title: [AI & Tools] Fable 5 is the best model on the board and it may leave in days, so the useful question is how to use it cheaply right now
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The practitioners who actually ship agents say Fable 5 is a step change, its own team is publishing the playbook for cutting the cost 80% before it goes, and the China price war just turned into a distillation fight.
item.content:encoded:
The practitioners who actually ship agents say Fable 5 is a step change, its own team is publishing the playbook for cutting the cost 80% before it goes, and the China price war just turned into a distillation fight.

The thread running through this morning is urgency. Fable 5 is drawing the strongest praise yet from the people who build agents for a living, and by their own account it is priced to leave via the API in a matter of days. So the practical move is not to debate whether it is good but to learn the cost playbook while you still can. Under that sits a China story that stopped being about price and became about who copied whom.

1. The people who ship agents for a living say Fable 5 is a genuine step change. This is not lab marketing, it is the practitioner tape. On the Hermes list, Shannon Sands and Uzi are calling Fable 5 the first model that makes long-horizon agents feel reliable, and Thariq published a "Field Guide" for driving it. The concrete example is Machina's deck-building agent reporting roughly 3x gains once it leaned on the model's memory instead of stuffing everything into the prompt.

The catch is the clock. Per Chase AI, the team behind it has signaled Fable 5 is "leaving via API pricing in a few days," so the window to build against it at current terms is short. If you have a real agent workload, this is the weekend to run it.

2. The cost playbook matters more than the benchmark, and the team published it. A frontier model you cannot afford to run in a loop is a demo. The useful Chase AI breakdown is that Fable 5's price swings enormously with its "effort" setting, the dial that controls how much reasoning it burns per task.

  • At max effort it runs about $22 a task at a 70% pass rate. That is real money once a background agent is looping on its own.
  • Dropped to low effort it falls to about $3.76 a task, more than 80% cheaper, and still beats Opus 4.8 on the same work.
  • The orchestration trick is to use the expensive setting as a planner or advisor that hands specs to cheaper models, rather than paying top rate for every keystroke. A "Ponytail" configuration reportedly trims another 20%.

The takeaway is that the cost of intelligence is now a tuning problem, not a fixed price. Most teams are overpaying because they run one setting for everything.

3. The killer use case is cloning software you already pay for. The demo that lands in Chase AI's use-case rundown is pointing the model's deep-research mode at an existing tool and having it rebuild a working version. The worked example pairs it with WhisperFlow, the dictation app, to reconstruct the feature set from scratch. The same loop covers auditing your own usage, standing up an agentic operating layer, and running code review. Treat these as proof the build-versus-buy line is moving, not as turnkey products yet.

4. Hermes shipped an agent release built entirely around not lying to you. Nous Research pushed Hermes Agent v0.18.0, the "Judgement Release," per the Hermes list. The design is a direct answer to the reliability complaint that has dogged autonomous agents. It adds completion contracts (the agent must satisfy an explicit checklist before it declares a task done), an evidence ledger that records why it believes each step succeeded, and a "goal_judge" that grades its own output. The interesting shift is that the frontier of agent work is moving from raw capability to verifiable honesty about what the agent actually did.

5. The China AI fight stopped being about price and became about distillation. For months the story was Chinese labs undercutting the frontier on cost. This week it inverted. Per the Financial Markets list, DeepSeek is raising its V4 Pro price to about 12 yuan (roughly $1.77) per million output tokens (the unit models are billed in), up from 6 yuan after a 75% cut in May, which reads as a company that thinks it is winning share and no longer needs to buy it.

At the same time Alibaba reportedly banned Anthropic's Claude Code over a "distillation scandal," distillation being the practice of training a cheaper model on a more expensive one's outputs. The accusation cuts both directions in this market, and it is now the sharp edge of the US-China model race. Separately, on the All-In show notes, Palantir and Nvidia are reported to be pairing on open Nemotron models, with Karp pushing government customers toward open weights. That is the same open-versus-closed argument playing out on the US side.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Bittensor and its TAO token, the decentralized-AI-compute network from Milk Road, is a real AI-infrastructure story but trades as a token, so it went to Investing as a satellite position. The Fable 5 API-pricing exit is also a stock-adjacent event, but the actionable read here is the build window, so the market angle stays in Investing. The GPT-5.6 launch timing and the Nasdaq "next Claude Sonnet release" prediction market are noise until something ships, so they wait.

item.title: [Investing] The memory bulls spent all day answering Burry, and Asia already voted with a green tape
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Micron is now the third-largest name in the Nasdaq-100 with record numbers behind it, the AI-capex bear case from this morning got its counterpunch, and Bitcoin's bottom-callers got louder even as the ETF money kept walking out the door.
item.content:encoded:
Micron is now the third-largest name in the Nasdaq-100 with record numbers behind it, the AI-capex bear case from this morning got its counterpunch, and Bitcoin's bottom-callers got louder even as the ETF money kept walking out the door.

This morning the story was Michael Burry leaning on the most crowded trade on the board. By this afternoon the other side had shown up with receipts, and the overseas tape had already picked a side. Markets are closed here for the holiday, so none of this trades until Monday, which gives you the weekend to decide whether the bounce is real or a trap.

The week ahead, and what actually matters at Monday's open. US cash and options markets were shut today, Friday July 3, for Independence Day, and reopen Monday July 6. Four things to pre-load. First, whether the memory names ($MU, $SNDK, $MRVL) follow Asia's Friday bounce or fade it. Second, the SK Hynix US listing: the Korean memory giant plans roughly a $29B Nasdaq ADR (a US-listed proxy for a foreign stock) around July 10 under ticker $SKHY, which puts a fresh pure-play memory bet in front of American money.

Third, $SPCX joins the Nasdaq-100 on July 7, which forces index funds to buy it. Fourth, the calendar is loud into the week: GPT-5.6 is priced to land as soon as Tuesday, the crypto Clarity Act has a coin-flip shot at a July vote, and Bitcoin has to prove the bottom-callers right or wrong at the $62K to $63K line.

1. Micron is now the third-biggest name in the Nasdaq-100, and the bulls spent all day making Burry look early. The counter to this morning's short case is not a vibe. It is a stack of numbers. Micron posted record fiscal Q3 revenue of $41.5B with adjusted EPS of $25.11 against a $20.49 estimate, and CEO Sanjay Mehrotra told Bloomberg the company can only meet "about 50% to about two-thirds" of key-customer demand right now, with no real new supply until 2028. Wolfe Research pegs Micron's DRAM gross margins above 90%.

The structural piece is that this cycle runs on two legs at once. AI servers pull memory twice, once as HBM (high-bandwidth memory, the expensive stacked chips bolted onto the GPU) and again as ordinary high-capacity DRAM feeding the rest of the box. That is why Wolfe calls this "more structural than the usual memory spike." $MU is now the third-largest weight in the Nasdaq-100 at roughly 4.9%, ahead of Microsoft and Amazon, so index money has to own it whether it loves the story or not.

And Asia already moved. On the inbox tape, SK Hynix closed up 11%, Samsung up 8%, and the Kospi up 6% on Friday. My read: I said this morning I still think the durable-shortage story wins, and today's numbers plus the overseas bounce are the bull case answering the bell. Burry short into a $1,000 print can still be right on a wobble, so let Monday's follow-through tell you whether to add, and respect that H1 already ran this group vertical ($SNDK finished the half up 764%, $MU up 301%, $WDC up 278%).

2. The AI-capex bear case from this morning got its counterpunch by the afternoon. At 5:30am the story was three voices calling the buildout a bubble. By the close the other side had answered on every front.

  • Meta's own numbers say the panic is old. The Meta capex breakdown is the sober version. FY2025 capex hit $69.7B, about 35% of revenue, and free cash flow fell 15% to $46.1B. But Meta did this exact thing in 2022, when a capex surge dropped free cash flow to $19B and the stock under $90 before it ran to $790. A discounted-cash-flow model (valuing the stock on future cash discounted back to today) says Meta is worth $659 if the buildout is temporary and $160 if 35% capex is permanent. That gap is the whole debate in one number.
  • The compute-glut story got refuted by the people building it. On the Market list, Meta AI chief Alexandr Wang said a model in training, codenamed Watermelon, is already matching GPT-5.5 on an order of magnitude more compute than its predecessor. SemiAnalysis went further and said Meta's data-center spend will accelerate and 2027 capex will be "shockingly high." Bill Ackman called the stock "very cheap." That is the opposite of a company that thinks it overbuilt.
  • Nvidia quietly changed how it gets paid. Nvidia rolled out a revenue-share and credit-support model for the neoclouds (the rent-a-GPU firms like CoreWeave). Instead of a one-time chip sale, Nvidia now takes a cut of the cloud revenue on that capacity. That turns a boom-bust hardware sale into a recurring stream, and it is a direct tailwind for $CRWV, $NBIS, and $IREN, all of which got flushed 12% to 17% yesterday on the Meta scare. $IREN alone is sitting on a $9.7B Microsoft contract and a $3.4B Nvidia one.

The honest close is that both mornings can be true. The bears are right that the trade is levered and crowded. The bulls are right that the buyers keep buying. When a name like $IREN gets sold 17% on a narrative that its biggest customers just contradicted, that is where the dip-buyers live.

3. Tom Lee raised his target to 8,000 and told you to expect a scary autumn first. Fundstrat's Tom Lee lifted his year-end S&P 500 target from 7,700 to 8,000, built on 2027 earnings of $400 times a 20x multiple. His real point is that the market is cheaper now than in January despite being up 9%, because earnings estimates rose faster than price (the forward multiple actually fell from 19.4 to 18.4). The S&P sits near 7,300, so 8,000 is another 10%.

The catch is he expects a "very severe correction" in the fall before that, and he named the triggers. New Fed chair Kevin Warsh is tearing up forward guidance (the Fed's practice of telegraphing its next move), which leaves the market without its usual script. The SpaceX share unlock this fall dumps a wall of new supply onto a stock with only a $90B float. Iran and the Strait of Hormuz keep an oil-shock tail alive. And margin debt (money investors borrow against their portfolios to buy more) is up 55% year over year, the fifth-largest jump in about seventy years, which historically front-runs a drawdown. Take the bull target, but keep some dry powder for the air pocket he is describing.

4. Bitcoin's bottom-callers got louder, and the ETF money kept ignoring them. The chart crowd piled in today. An on-chain "dorito" indicator that last flashed at the FTX bottom lit up again on the inbox tape, the monthly RSI (a momentum gauge, now its most oversold reading ever) is stretched, and Lark Davis notes 270,000 BTC were accumulated near $59K, the single largest accumulation event on record. Standard Chartered is still carrying a $500K long-term BTC target and $40K on ETH.

Against all that, the flows say wait. Bitcoin ETFs have bled for weeks, with BlackRock's fund alone posting ten straight days of outflows totaling $2.24B, and over $11B has left the ETF complex since the peak. The cleanest floor framework came from Charles Schwab, which prices Bitcoin off miner economics: the cheapest, best-run miners produce a coin near $60K, and that production cost has marked the bottom in past bear markets.

Benjamin Cowen is the patient-bear counterweight worth holding in mind, with total crypto market cap near $2.125T and a call for a lower low later this year before the real move into 2027. My read is unchanged from this morning. Accumulate the dips you already wanted, respect the $60K miner floor as your line, and let the ETF flows turn green before you believe the bounce.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $LLY, the AI trade nobody files under AI. Per Milk Road, Eli Lilly is a $1T+ name up 2x in a year, throwing off free cash flow that grew over 100% to about $10B, now pouring it into a $5B data center, a drug-discovery tie-up with Google's Isomorphic Labs, and a direct-to-consumer channel with over a million customers. The thesis is a rotation from AI infrastructure to AI application, and healthcare is the first place that spend actually earns its keep. Even Dario Amodei said he would put his AI-in-biology money here.
  • The neocloud dip: $IREN, $CRWV, $NBIS. Yesterday's 12% to 17% flush ran on a Meta narrative that Meta itself contradicted today. Nvidia's new revenue-share model rewards exactly these names. High-beta and not for the faint of heart, but this is the cleanest oversold setup on the board into Monday.
  • $OKLO, the nuclear-power leg of the AI trade. From two Rick Orford shorts: Oklo signed a fuel letter of intent with Centrus Energy for domestic enriched uranium to feed up to five Aurora powerhouses, with deliveries from 2029. It is a pre-revenue $10B name, so size it as a long-dated option you can afford to lose.
  • $QBTS, the government-blessed quantum bet. D-Wave landed a $100M letter of intent under the CHIPS Act, with the Commerce Department taking stock. It trades at 377x sales, a pure belief stock, but the government validation is real. Analysts carry a strong-buy with targets implying it could double. Satellite-sized position only.
  • Optical, the flush that may be a setup: $LITE, $COHR. Chip Stock Investor flags that Nvidia put $2B each into Lumentum and Coherent. The wrinkle worth knowing: the same research shop that wrote the optical sector down 40% to 60% in June then launched an ETF to buy those exact names. When the bear turns buyer, watch for the V.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The Standard Chartered $66K-to-$500K BTC and $1,700-to-$40K ETH targets folded into the Bitcoin section. Trader Mayne's meme-coin clip carried one useful stat (under 1% of Pump.fun wallets cleared $10K in profit) and no tradeable name, so it stays a caution, not a pick. The AMD "can it hit $1T" reddit thread was retail sentiment with no fresh catalyst, so $AMD stays on radar off its $584.73 high. The stablecoin distribution war (140 firms backing Open USD against Circle's $CRCL) is a real crypto-structure story but moved slow today, so it sits here as a flag into next week.

The Fable 5 model news, the Etched inference chip, and the Anthropic-profitability report went to AI & Tools. The Hormuz oil tail and the quiet labor-market crack went to News. Trump ringing the NYSE bell next week is noise until it prints.

item.title: [News] The Strait of Hormuz is back as the world's most dangerous chokepoint
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Iran is threatening ships again as Brent slips under $72, Washington quietly warned Poland that Russia may stage a provocation, and over the holiday a lab in Minnesota built the first synthetic cell from scratch.
item.content:encoded:
Iran is threatening ships again as Brent slips under $72, Washington quietly warned Poland that Russia may stage a provocation, and over the holiday a lab in Minnesota built the first synthetic cell from scratch.

Two stories run under the holiday quiet, and both deserve more than the thin headlines gave them. The oil chokepoint that defined this year's war risk is live again, and the labor market is softening in a way the cheerful top-line numbers hide. Then, almost unnoticed, science had one of its biggest weeks in years.

1. Iran is threatening ships in the Strait of Hormuz again, and the oil market is starting to price it. The Financial Markets list has Iran warning of a "forceful response" against ships using unapproved routes through the Strait, the channel that carries a fifth of the world's oil. Brent crude (the international oil benchmark) slipped under $72 a barrel. The measured read comes from analyst Anas Alhajji: Iran cannot actually close the Strait, but it can make it expensive and slow, and shipping data already shows crossings down about 10% day over day. A new Polymarket contract now takes bets on whether traffic returns to normal by the end of August.

The escalation is not only in the Gulf. Washington reportedly warned Poland that Russia may stage a limited provocation against NATO in the coming months, a missile strike on infrastructure or a small cross-border incursion, calibrated to pressure allies over Ukraine without triggering a full war. Layer that over Ayatollah Khamenei's funeral drawing millions into Tehran today, and the geopolitical tail that markets spent the spring fearing is back on the table.

2. The labor market is cracking quietly, even as the recession bet keeps losing. Here is the disconnect worth holding onto. The household survey shows the US has lost 1.7 million jobs so far this year, dropping employment to its lowest level since December 2024, per both the Polymarket and Financial Markets feeds. Yet the odds of a US recession this year still sit near record lows.

Both things can be true at once, and that is the point. Lyn Alden's framing captures it: this is a two-speed economy, where older Americans on fixed income and defense contractors do fine while a lot of working people quietly do not. When the jobs data and the recession odds point in opposite directions, the jobs data is the one you can count.

3. Over the holiday, science had a genuinely historic week. This is the part that got buried under the market noise, and it should not have. From Packy McCormick's roundup, three real firsts landed at once.

  • A synthetic cell, built from non-living chemicals. A University of Minnesota team led by Kate Adamala assembled the first synthetic cell from scratch, one that can feed, grow, and reproduce. It is the biology equivalent of the Wright Flyer, crude and enormous at the same time.
  • Human eggs, grown from stem cells. Conception Bio, out of Berkeley, produced the first early human eggs starting from blood cells reprogrammed into an ovary. The implications for fertility are obvious and large.
  • A machine that reads words off your brain. Meta's non-invasive decoder hit 61% word accuracy, up from 8% for previous methods, translating brain activity into text without surgery.

And the nuclear industry hit a cluster of reactor "criticality" milestones (the point a reactor sustains its own chain reaction) around July 4, with Aalo Atomics, Radiant, and Deployable Energy all reaching or loading fuel. The AI-power crunch is pulling nuclear forward faster than almost anyone forecast a year ago.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • Europe is turning on Palantir. Per the Financial Markets list, France, Germany, and Spain have instructed state-linked companies to avoid Palantir, and the UK's NHS is reviewing a £330M contract. The pushback is part politics and part a real fight over who controls government data.
  • The US-China AI wall got taller. Alibaba banned its own employees from using Claude Code, and Anthropic moved to close the loopholes that let Chinese firms reach its models, per Kalshi. The decoupling is now happening tool by tool, not just chip by chip.
  • Republicans firmed up their Senate edge. The GOP is now around 60% to hold the Senate on the prediction markets, a recent high. Slow drift in a party-control market usually means more than a single poll.
  • Trump says he "didn't know" he made $1.4B from crypto. Also on Kalshi, a disclosure of the president's crypto gains that will not be the last word on it.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The memory-stock fight, Bitcoin's contested bottom, the Meta and Micron capex battle, and Tom Lee's market call are all in Investing. The Fable 5 relaunch, the Anthropic profitability report, and the Etched inference chip are in AI & Tools. Nothing on the news side was dropped as noise today.

item.title: [AI & Tools] Fable 5 is back for real, and the day's other news is that the labs suddenly make money
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The model everyone missed returned with new guardrails and genuinely new tricks, Anthropic reportedly turned its first profit while a startup chip threatens Nvidia on inference, and the cheap-clone war got a price cut aimed straight at China.
item.content:encoded:
The model everyone missed returned with new guardrails and genuinely new tricks, Anthropic reportedly turned its first profit while a startup chip threatens Nvidia on inference, and the cheap-clone war got a price cut aimed straight at China.

This morning the story was that Fable 5 came back so locked down its power users revolted. This afternoon fills in the other half. The model can actually do things again, the economics under the whole industry quietly turned positive, and the frontier labs are now pricing to kill their Chinese clones. The unit-economics doubt from this morning got a real answer.

1. Fable 5 is genuinely back, and the capabilities are the part worth your attention. The guardrail revolt from this morning is real, but the Limitless podcast fills in what the model does when you get past them. It now has what they call visual intelligence, an actual sense of aesthetic taste, so the websites and artifacts it builds stop looking like AI slop. One demo built a fully playable Hogwarts game (physics, sound, spatial awareness) from a single prompt in one shot. And Claude Science, a research-tuned version with a tool harness, is doing 100-plus-page literature reviews for the Allen Institute in hours instead of what used to take up to two years.

The new safeguards are the price of the return. Fable now monitors every prompt for attack patterns and routes anything it flags to a heavier, slower model, and that is why ordinary requests keep getting caught. The practical facts to plan around, from The AI Advantage: it burns roughly 70% more tokens than Opus 4.8 (not the 2x people assumed), it is free only through July 7 before usage-based pricing kicks in, and it carries its own 50% weekly usage cap. If your work has real judgment in it, this is the sharpest tool on the shelf right now. Just budget for it.

2. The unit-economics story flipped today, and that answers this morning's bear case. The 5:30am edition ran OpenAI's cash burn as evidence the whole thing leaks money. The afternoon numbers cut the other way.

  • Anthropic reportedly turned profitable in Q2 2026, the first frontier lab to do it, on inference margins said to run near 80%. If that holds, the "nobody makes money on inference" thesis is already dated.
  • OpenAI claims it halved its own inference cost, dropping the GPU count needed to serve free-tier users from hundreds of thousands to a couple hundred, per the same podcast. Take the exact figures with salt, but the direction matches Anthropic's.
  • Etched is the wild card in the hardware stack. The inference-chip startup has raised $800M and booked $1B in orders for a chip that has not shipped, promising 75% less power for the same inference output versus a GPU. The backers are the story: Peter Thiel, Jane Street, and TSMC, which took equity. A chip foundry taking a stake in a would-be Nvidia alternative is the kind of signal that does not show up in a benchmark. It ships by year end.

Put together, the picture is not an industry bleeding out. It is one where the cost of serving a model is falling fast enough that the margins are finally showing up.

3. Sonnet 5 got priced to kill the cheap-clone trade, and the distillation war went public. The strategic move today was pricing. Sonnet 5 launched cheaper than Sonnet 4.6 (through August 31) while doing roughly Opus 4.8's job, an open bid to keep builders inside the Claude ecosystem instead of drifting to a subsidized Chinese model like GLM 5.2. This is the same cost-curve pressure from this morning, answered with a price cut instead of a shrug.

The uglier subplot: that "spyware-like" code people found in Claude Code was reportedly an experiment to detect distillation attacks (rivals training their cheap models on the outputs of an expensive one). Alibaba's Qwen got caught doing exactly that, and Dario Amodei confirmed it publicly. DeepSeek V4 Flash still sits at the top of the open-model rankings, so the cheap tier keeps closing the gap. The frontier's answer is to make its own mid-tier too cheap to bother cloning.

4. If you are actually building with this stuff, here is the day's practical playbook. The most useful content today was not the model news, it was people showing how to run these tools without going broke or hitting a wall.

  • Cut Fable's cost by dialing effort, not switching models. Per Chase AI, the same task ran $22 at max effort and $3.76 at low, an 80%-plus cut, and Fable-on-low still beat Opus-on-max. Token-saver skills like ponytail (about 22% cheaper on Fable) and caveman help, and `/deep-research` can fan out over a hundred sub-agents when you actually need the depth.
  • Every agent demo dies at the last step, so design for it. Nate B Jones laid out a reusable agent skeleton of nine primitives (context pack, ingest, chunk, normalize, store, retrieve, cite, export, and a human gate at the end) and pointed it at real drudgery like insurance appeals and tax prep. His point ties to the cost story: once your data is clean, a cheap or open model does the job, so the moat is the plumbing, not the model.
  • Build a second brain that any model can read. Nate Herk wired Andrej Karpathy's LLM-wiki idea into Claude Code and Obsidian: drop in sources, the model splits them into cross-linked markdown pages with routing rules, and any agent can reason over the result. Fable understood a "keep it simple for a beginner" design instruction that Opus 4.8 could not get after a full day. Because it is just markdown with routing, it is model-agnostic.
  • UNBROKER, an open-source privacy tool for your weekend. Released as a Hermes skill by the Nous Research crowd, it finds where data brokers expose your personal info and files the legal removals for you, all locally. It is a free replacement for the $330-a-year DeleteMe tier, and a clean example of an agent pointed at a boring, valuable job.

5. One caveat before you trust next week's benchmark war. GPT-5.6 is priced to land as soon as Tuesday, and the wiki-building demo surfaced a sharp catch: OpenAI benchmarked GPT-5.6 Sol against a Mythos preview model, not against Fable 5, and used different test harnesses. So when the launch numbers hit, the head-to-head claims will not line up cleanly. Read the release, but do not let the marketing pick your model. The move that got everyone through the Fable outage still holds: keep your harness model-agnostic so next week's drop is a config change.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The Meta compute-glut refutation (Alexandr Wang's Watermelon model), the Nvidia revenue-share model, and the Anthropic-versus-Micron capex fight are the market story in Investing. The Kris Torrington Fable recap and the "Fable is a magician" clip repeated what section one already covers, so they were folded, not listed twice. The self-help anxiety video and a culture-war newsletter were out of scope. Alibaba banning its own staff from using Claude Code went to News with the rest of the US-China AI friction.

item.title: [Investing] Michael Burry is short Micron, and the whole AI-capex trade wobbled overnight
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The memory bull wall now has a famous bear leaning on it, three separate voices called the AI buildout a bubble, and Bitcoin's bounce is being sold by whales while the chartists call a bottom.
item.content:encoded:
The memory bull wall now has a famous bear leaning on it, three separate voices called the AI buildout a bubble, and Bitcoin's bounce is being sold by whales while the chartists call a bottom.

Markets are closed today for the holiday, which gives you a full session to think instead of react. Use it, because two things shifted overnight. A brand-name bear stepped in front of the memory trade, and the AI-capex bull case took its first coordinated punches.

Closed today, and what to watch Monday. US cash equity and options markets are shut today, Friday July 3, for Independence Day (observed, since the Fourth is Saturday), and reopen Monday July 6. So there is no session to react to. What is worth pre-loading for the reopen: whether the semis that got flushed Thursday ($MU, $SNDK, $MRVL) find a bid or keep sliding, whether Bitcoin holds the $62K bounce or rolls over toward the mid-$50Ks, and the calendar into next week. $SPCX joins the Nasdaq-100 on July 7, which forces index funds to buy it, and the market expects GPT-5.6 as soon as Tuesday, which would land straight into the AI-bubble argument below.

1. Michael Burry is short Micron, and that reframes the whole memory fight. The biggest new thing overnight is that Michael Burry opened a bearish $MU position, flagged across the Stocks list and four others. He is leaning directly on the most crowded bull wall on the board. The bull case is genuinely strong: Q3 revenue was $41.46B, up 346% year over year and a beat, gross margin ran 84.9%, the Q4 guide is $50B in revenue and $31 EPS, and the stock trades near 6x forward earnings against a CEO target of $4,000.

The bear case is now a person rather than a chart, and it comes with a real risk attached, that $AAPL is exploring Chinese memory suppliers and SK Hynix is pulling price caps off its long-term contracts. My read: I still think the durable shortage story wins over a multi-year horizon, but Burry short into a $1,000 breakdown is exactly the kind of thing that keeps you from sizing up early. Watch how $MU trades on the Monday reopen and let the $4,000 target wait.

2. The flush was a momentum unwind, not a market break. This is the framing that matters for how you play the reopen. Thursday's damage was concentrated and brutal in one place: $AEHR fell 18.3%, $AAOI 17.1%, $SNDK 14.8%, $TER 13.8%, $MRVL 11.2%, and $NBIS ran 30% from its peak. Meanwhile the $SPX finished down just 0.13% and the Dow actually printed a record high, with the $VIX (the market's fear gauge) closing at its lowest in a month and recession odds collapsing from 32% toward 12%. That is high-beta AI names getting sold while the broad market shrugs. Call it rotation rather than a risk-off break. The tell to respect is that it happened into a three-day weekend, when traders cut the risk they do not want to hold blind.

3. The AI-capex bull case took three separate punches overnight. Yesterday the doubt was one bear (Ed Zitron). Overnight it became a chorus, and that convergence is the actual signal.

  • Meta stopped pretending. On Prof G, the read on Meta launching a business to sell its "excess" AI compute is blunt, that the second or third largest compute buyer on earth is walking away from building its own AI products. Zuckerberg's own May line is the receipt: he would not sell "because we think we have a use for the compute, but obviously if we get to a point where we feel that we have overbuilt, then that is an option." In a town hall he added that agent development has not accelerated the way he expected and the stock "would be higher" without the AI spend.
  • China is the mechanism that could crack it. Capital Flows made the sharpest macro case: roughly half the S&P 500 is now an AI trade funded on borrowed money, and the risk is not who wins, it is that the price of intelligence falls faster than the most crowded trade in history can adjust. China runs the Amazon playbook, models one generation behind priced 10x to 100x cheaper and state-subsidized so they never need the API margins US labs depend on. When the US suspended its top model on June 12, a Chinese lab open-sourced a near-frontier rival the next day. The template is DeepSeek in January 2025, which erased $589B from $NVDA in a single day.
  • The unit economics are leaking. Zitron, quoted at length via threadguy, pegs OpenAI's 2025 cash burn at $20.9B with costs that scale linearly with revenue, and notes the IPO reportedly slipped to 2027 after it could not clear a $1T valuation. Nvidia's revenue-share model, confirmed by Bloomberg, hands token credits to AI startups for a cut of future sales. That is vendor financing dressed as demand. And the physical backlash is showing up, with Blackstone abandoning a Virginia data center and 20 Florida counties weighing data-center moratoriums.

The counter is real and I hold it partly: OpenAI delaying an IPO from a position of private funding is optionality, not panic, and none of these bears has been right on timing. But when Meta, a China cost-curve, and OpenAI's own burn rate all point the same way in one night, you respect it by trimming the most levered AI-beta names, not by flipping short.

4. Bitcoin's bottom-callers are buying what the whales are selling. The chart signals are genuinely bullish. On the Crypto list, long-term-holder SOPR (a metric that reads above 1 when holders sell at a profit and below when they sell at a loss) printed 0.615, meaning long-term holders are capitulating at a 38.5% average loss, a sub-0.65 reading seen only at the March 2020, October 2022, and July 2023 bottoms. $IBIT put in its first-ever weekly tweezer bottom and one entity absorbed 270,000 BTC at $59K.

Against that, the whales are handing it to them: an Irish dealer wallet sent 500 BTC to Coinbase Prime with 4,500 ($276M) left to go, a Tim Draper-linked wallet moved 1,000 BTC, and Novogratz keeps saying Bitcoin "lost its sponsorship." Kalshi still prices only 15% odds of $100K this year. Benjamin Cowen is the patient bear worth hearing: total crypto market cap sits near $2.125T, below its 2021 level, and he thinks the whole class stays under its fair-value trend line all year, with a summer counter-rally, a lower low later in 2026, and the real bull into 2027 and 2028. My read: accumulate the dips you were already going to buy, but the flows say the bottom is a process, not this week.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • The on-site power thesis has a small-cap leg now. Yesterday's names ($BE, $CEG, $TLN, $VST) were the blue-chip version. The small-cap version, driven by 3-to-5-year lead times on high-voltage transformers, adds $CGEH (microturbines with an 800-volt "Microgrids 4 AI" product aimed at server racks) and $FCEL (megawatt fuel cells, a 4-gigawatt-plus pipeline), with $POWL, $MOD, and $NVT flagged to research. Size these as high-risk satellites, because fuel-cell and microturbine small caps have a long history of burning cash and diluting holders.
  • $AVAV, the drone super-cycle name. From the People list: AeroVironment printed roughly $642M in fiscal Q4 revenue, a backlog up 65% to $1.2B, and a $500M Defense Department award, with $KTOS getting a JPMorgan upgrade alongside it. Defense-tech is the non-AI momentum pocket that kept working while semis flushed.
  • $TSLA, a defined swing setup into next week. Per Stocks with Josh, Tesla flushed 11% to 12% on its record 480,000-plus Q2 delivery beat, a classic sell-the-good-news move, rejecting $425 resistance and finding support near $381.68 with a $369 pivot below that. His stat: 85% odds of trading above the intraday low within 30 days, 95% within 60. Cathie Wood bought about $40M of it into the drop. A trade with clear invalidation, not a conviction hold.
  • $MRNA, the biotech that quietly went vertical. Fresh two-year high, up about 160% year to date, now the eighth-best performer in the S&P 500. When a beaten-down name reclaims a multi-year base while the hot trade unwinds, it earns a look.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Trader Mayne's Bitcoin-bottom video and the Market Bubble podcast with Ansem both shipped without transcripts, so there are no levels to pull. Lark Davis's "escape being broke" clip was a generic wealth-mindset piece with no specific calls. The three one-minute threadguy livestream clips (a company "cheat code," Ansem's Solana memecoin, a poker resurgence) carried no tradeable names. The $NXPI post-quantum-crypto thesis is a real secular angle but came from a promotional Substack with no numbers, so it stays on radar, not the watchlist. Trump walking back the OpenAI stake went to News. The Fable 5 usage backlash and the model-release cadence went to AI & Tools.

item.title: [News] The government is trying to buy into AI, and towns are trying to keep it out
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Trump walked back the proposed OpenAI stake on camera even as bettors still price it as likely, and the physical AI buildout is now running into local moratoriums.
item.content:encoded:
Trump walked back the proposed OpenAI stake on camera even as bettors still price it as likely, and the physical AI buildout is now running into local moratoriums.

The AI story turned into a government story overnight, and it cut both ways. Washington is circling equity in the labs while local governments start slamming the door on the data centers. Both are worth more attention than the holiday-thin headlines suggest.

1. Trump walked back the OpenAI stake on camera, but the market still bets it happens. Yesterday the framing was concrete, a proposed 5% government stake in OpenAI worth about $42.5B. On CNBC overnight, per the People to follow list, Trump would not confirm it and pointed instead to the 10% stake the government already holds in Intel. Yet Polymarket still prices a US government equity stake in OpenAI at 57%, and Sam Altman is on record calling public ownership the best way to share AI's upside, in an arrangement that reportedly would have pulled in other labs too.

The specifics are soft and the president is hedging in public. The direction is not soft at all. The idea of the state taking direct equity in frontier AI has moved from unthinkable to a coin-flip bet in a matter of days.

2. The AI buildout is hitting the one wall money cannot move, local government. The counterweight to all that federal enthusiasm is showing up in county commission meetings. Blackstone abandoned a Virginia data center, and roughly 20 Florida counties are weighing data-center moratoriums, per Polymarket. This is the part of the AI story that does not live on a chart. The buildout needs land, water, and enormous amounts of power, and the people who live next to it are starting to vote on it. When the physical footprint of the boom becomes a ballot-box issue, that is a real constraint on the timeline, not a talking point.

3. Around the world, quickly.

  • Republicans firmed up their Senate edge. On Kalshi, the GOP is now 61% to hold the Senate, up from the high 50s a day earlier. Slow, quiet drift in the party-control market is usually more real than any single poll.
  • The recession trade got unwound. Odds of a US recession this year fell to about 8%, an all-time low on Kalshi, even after a soft jobs print. The market is pricing a slowdown that keeps not arriving.
  • The world's assets hit a new high. Global market capitalization set a record near $166T. Whatever the AI-bubble argument does to individual names, the aggregate pile of wealth has never been larger. That is its own kind of risk when sentiment turns.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Michael Burry's Micron short, the semiconductor flush, Bitcoin's contested bottom, and Tesla's delivery-day flush are all market mechanics and live in Investing. The AI-bubble bear case (Meta's compute sale, the China cost curve, OpenAI's burn rate) is the big Investing story this morning. The revolt over Fable 5's guardrails and the week's model releases are covered in AI & Tools. Nothing on the news side was dropped as noise today.

item.title: [AI & Tools] Fable 5 came back guardrailed to the point of revolt, and the price of intelligence is falling
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The relaunched model is so locked down that power users say it is unusable for real work, China keeps undercutting the frontier on cost, and the useful question is what actually still needs a human.
item.content:encoded:
The relaunched model is so locked down that power users say it is unusable for real work, China keeps undercutting the frontier on cost, and the useful question is what actually still needs a human.

The model everyone wanted back is back, and the first thing its heaviest users did was complain that they cannot use it. That gap, between the model on the benchmark and the model you are allowed to run, is the real story this morning.

1. Fable 5 returned so locked down that power users are in revolt. The export controls came off yesterday. The guardrails did not. On the Hermes list, the reaction is loud and specific: one heavy user says Fable 5 is "not nerfed, it is SLAUGHTERED," another reports it hardblocking legitimate biology work, and the safety classifiers that route sensitive tasks to Opus are catching ordinary requests.
